name: Goa
description: India's most accessible dive destination, with Grande Island a reliable spot for turtles feeding on coral patches.

sightings.notes: Most frequently spotted feeding near coral patches at Grande Island Oct-Nov.
Notable during the nesting season, Oct-Mar.
Commonly seen along the sandy floor near Shelter Cove.
Occasionally observed in deeper waters.
Goa is one of the best places to spot dolphins in India.

name: Kavaratti
description: Lakshadweep's capital island, with a calm beginner-friendly lagoon and a dedicated "Turtle Nest" dive site.

sightings.notes: The dedicated "Turtle Nest" site offers reliable sightings during the Oct-May dive season.
Eyeball contact is common at sites like Windmill Point.
One of the larger species recorded around the island.
Different turtle species are found at the aptly-named Turtle Nest site.
Spotted rays are seen at Windmill Point.

name: Phu Quoc
description: Vietnam's largest island, with a shallow, reef-fringed "Turtle Island" site well suited to beginners.

sightings.notes: Calm, shallow water around Turtle Island suits beginner divers and snorkelers Nov-May.
Occasionally seen resting among the reef.
Occasionally seen alongside bamboo sharks.
Moray eels are common residents of the rocky reef sites around the An Thoi Archipelago year-round.
An occasional sighting at more pristine reef sites; Phu Quoc's turtle populations are recovering.

name: Koh Lipe
description: Southern Thai island bordering Tarutao National Marine Park, with healthy coral gardens and occasional turtles.

sightings.notes: Occasional sightings at sites like Koh Yang and Koh Taru during the Nov-Apr season; not guaranteed.
A common sighting alongside hawksbills.
Sighted in summer if conditions and luck align.
An occasional summer sighting, requiring good luck.
An occasional shark sighting alongside reef sharks.

name: Koh Chang
description: Largest island in Thailand's eastern gulf, with reef diving and occasional whale shark visits.

sightings.notes: Resident on the reef during the Nov-Apr dry season, with occasional whale sharks passing through.
Found alongside green turtles, the most frequently encountered megafauna here.
Documented at sites like HTMS Chang wreck, best season Dec-May.
Notable for its white-spotted black body and wide wingspan.
A common sighting alongside barracuda and batfish.

name: Ujung Kulon
description: Remote, current-swept national park at Java's western tip, with challenging dives rewarded by whale sharks and manta rays.

sightings.notes: Strong-current dives occasionally reward experienced divers with whale sharks and manta rays.
Experienced divers are rewarded with manta ray sightings.
Found among the clear waters around the national park.
Encountered alongside barracuda and dogtooth tuna.
Schools of dolphins swim freely in these Indian Ocean waters.

name: Togean Islands
description: Gentle-current island group in the Gulf of Tomini, home to dugongs, hawksbill and green turtles in its reefs and mangroves.

sightings.notes: Reefs and mangroves are breeding habitat, but sightings remain a matter of extreme luck; diveable year-round, best Mar-Dec.
A reliable sighting in the gentle-current reefs during the Mar-Dec dry season.
Manta rays visit the Togean Islands alongside the large schools of pelagic fish.
Schools of bumphead parrotfish cruise the Togeans' vibrant reefs during the dry season.
Hammerhead sharks patrol the outer reef walls, an exciting but not guaranteed encounter.

name: Pulau Aur
description: Remote South China Sea island for experienced divers, with deep pinnacles and resident Napoleon wrasse.

sightings.notes: Best conditions Apr-Jun, when seas are calm enough for the deeper pinnacles.
Occasionally sighted at sites like Rayners Rock.
Occasionally sighted at the start and end of season.
Spotted at the Pinnacles dive site alongside morays.
Occasionally sighted off the coast.

name: Pulau Tenggol
description: Terengganu island with a turtle conservation program and an Aug-Sep chance of whale sharks.

sightings.notes: Most commonly seen Aug-Sep; the resort also runs a turtle egg conservation program.
Glimpsed from March to May.
Sightings are common around the island.
Found alongside the island's whale sharks.
One of the specialty creatures found here.

name: Serengeti NP
description: Vast savanna ecosystem famous for the Great Wildebeest Migration and dense lion prides.

sightings.notes: Large resident prides hunt wildebeest and zebra across the open plains year-round.
Family herds are seen throughout the park, particularly around the Seronera River.
Hundreds of thousands join the Great Migration moving clockwise through the ecosystem.
Over 1.5 million wildebeest drive the Great Migration; river crossings occur July–October.
The open short-grass plains of the Serengeti hold one of Africa's highest cheetah densities.
Leopards are regularly seen draped over acacia branches in the Seronera Valley, one of the best leopard-viewing areas in Africa.

name: Masai Mara NR
description: Kenya's iconic reserve where the Mara River crossing draws millions of wildebeest each July–October.

sightings.notes: The Mara's famous large prides are reliably seen on morning and evening game drives.
Large herds move through the Mara year-round, increasing during the dry season.
Hundreds of thousands cross the Mara River alongside wildebeest in the dramatic annual migration.
The Mara River crossing is the climax of the Great Migration, drawing wildlife and photographers worldwide.
The Mara holds very high hyena densities; large clans are active both day and night.
The Mara's riverine forests along the Talek and Mara rivers hold some of East Africa's most habituated and reliably-seen leopards.

name: Ngorongoro CA
description: Collapsed volcanic caldera sheltering the world's densest large-mammal population including all of the Big Five.

sightings.notes: The crater's enclosed ecosystem supports a high-density lion population reliably seen on the crater floor.
Large bull elephants with impressive tusks are frequently seen descending the crater walls.
One of the best places in Africa to see black rhino in the wild — around 20–30 individuals live on the crater floor.
Hippo pools in the Gorigor Swamp are a reliable highlight of any Ngorongoro day trip.
Crocodiles bask on the banks of the Munge and Lerai rivers on the crater floor.
Leopards inhabit the crater's Lerai forest, completing crater sightings of all Big Five, though they are shyer and less predictable than the resident lions.
Large buffalo herds graze the crater floor year-round, forming one of the densest concentrations in Tanzania.

name: Amboseli NP
description: Renowned for large free-roaming elephant herds set against the backdrop of Mount Kilimanjaro.

sightings.notes: Amboseli's elephants are among the largest-tusked in Africa; family groups are seen daily against Kilimanjaro.
Resident prides hunt the park's abundant prey species across the open swamps and plains.
The flat open landscape makes Amboseli ideal cheetah habitat; mothers with cubs are regularly seen.
Masai giraffe are abundant throughout the park, feeding on the acacia woodland edges.
Impala herds are among the most visible prey animals throughout the Amboseli ecosystem.

name: Tarangire NP
description: Dry-season refuge for the largest elephant concentrations in northern Tanzania, with towering baobabs.

sightings.notes: Tarangire hosts the largest dry-season elephant concentration in northern Tanzania — herds of hundreds.
Lions are present year-round; the dry season concentrates prey and makes sightings more predictable.
Masai giraffe browse the iconic baobab and acacia woodland throughout the park.
Large wildebeest herds spread across the Tarangire floodplains in the wet season.
Warthog families trot through open clearings and kneel at waterholes throughout the park.

name: Tsavo NP
description: Kenya's largest park, split into East and West, known for red-dust elephants and large lion prides.

sightings.notes: Tsavo's red-dust elephants are iconic; the park supports Kenya's largest elephant population.
Tsavo's maneless lions are legendary; large prides roam both East and West sections.
Leopards are present throughout Tsavo but secretive; rocky kopjes and riverine forests are best.
The Mzima Springs in Tsavo West host large hippo pools viewable from an underwater observatory.
Cheetah are present but less common than in more open-plain parks; open areas of Tsavo East are best.

name: Lake Nakuru NP
description: Alkaline lake park famous for flamingos and a black and white rhinoceros sanctuary.

sightings.notes: Nakuru's alkaline lake turns pink with flamingos; numbers fluctuate with lake level and algae blooms.
Nakuru's sanctuary holds one of East Africa's most reliable white rhino populations — seen regularly from the road.
A small but growing black rhino population is tracked within the fenced sanctuary.
A resident pride uses the Baboon Cliffs and surrounding woodland; sightings are frequent.
Rothschild giraffe, one of Africa's rarest subspecies, have been reintroduced and are commonly seen.

name: Samburu NR
description: Arid northern Kenya reserve with the "Samburu Special Five" including reticulated giraffe and Grevy's zebra.

sightings.notes: Large elephant herds converge on the Ewaso Ng'iro River throughout the year.
Samburu's leopards are habituated and among the most reliably seen in Kenya.
Resident prides hunt along the Ewaso Ng'iro River corridor.
Resident cheetah families are reliably seen on the arid plains north of the river.
Reticulated giraffe — one of Samburu's "Special Five" endemic species — are seen on every game drive.
Samburu NR is one of the world's best places for Grevy's Zebra; large groups are reliably seen alongside the Ewaso Ng'iro River — distinct from plains zebra by their narrow stripes.
Samburu NR holds the best populations of Reticulated Giraffe in Kenya; their bold, clean-lined polygon patterning makes them unmistakable against the acacia-lined river.
Gerenuk are abundant in Samburu and one of the park's flagship "Samburu Special Five"; they are seen daily standing upright on hind legs browsing acacia shoots, a uniquely comical sight.
Kori Bustards are frequently seen in Samburu's open scrub; the large males are particularly conspicuous in the dry river terraces near Archer's Post.

name: Ruaha NP
description: Tanzania's largest park and one of Africa's most pristine wild spaces, with large lion and wild dog populations.

sightings.notes: Ruaha holds one of Africa's largest lion populations; prides of 20+ are not unusual.
Ruaha supports large elephant herds that congregate along the Great Ruaha River in the dry season.
Ruaha is one of Tanzania's best parks for wild dog; large packs with pups are regularly encountered.
Leopards are present throughout the park; rocky outcrops along the river are favoured resting spots.
Large hippo pods occupy pools along the Great Ruaha River, visible year-round from river-view camps.

name: Mikumi NP
description: Tanzania's most accessible safari park, with open floodplains harbouring lion prides and large hippo pools.

sightings.notes: Resident prides hunt the Mkata floodplains, often visible resting in the shade of acacia trees.
Elephant herds are a daily sight on the Mkata plains — Mikumi's most accessible safari zone.
Hippo pools at Mkata and Chamgore are among Mikumi's most visited wildlife highlights.
Masai giraffe are abundant in the acacia woodlands bordering the Mkata floodplains.
Zebra herds graze across the Mkata floodplain alongside wildebeest year-round.

name: Katavi NP
description: Remote western Tanzania park with some of Africa's most dramatic dry-season hippo and crocodile concentrations.

sightings.notes: Katavi's dry-season hippo spectacle — thousands packed into shrinking pools — is one of Africa's great wildlife events.
Crocodiles mass alongside hippos in the drying Katuma River pools during the long dry season.
Large lion prides hunt the floodplains year-round; their numbers are boosted by the dry-season prey concentration.
Katavi supports large elephant populations ranging across its remote miombo woodland and floodplains.
Enormous buffalo herds — sometimes thousands strong — are a defining feature of Katavi's dry-season spectacle.

name: Nyerere NP
description: Former Selous GR — Africa's largest protected area by land, with exceptional wild dog and crocodile populations.

sightings.notes: Nyerere holds Africa's largest wild dog population; large packs with pups are frequently encountered.
Huge lion prides roam Nyerere's miombo woodland and seasonal floodplains.
Nyerere supports one of Africa's largest elephant populations across its vast pristine landscape.
The Rufiji River hosts enormous hippo populations; boat safaris give excellent close-up views.
The Rufiji River is teeming with large Nile crocodiles — Nyerere hosts one of Africa's largest populations.

name: Bwindi NP
description: UNESCO World Heritage rainforest sheltering roughly half the world's remaining mountain gorillas.

sightings.notes: Bwindi protects roughly half the world's mountain gorillas; habituated family groups are visited daily on treks.
Chimpanzee communities inhabit Bwindi's forest alongside gorillas and are tracked by researchers.
Forest elephants move through Bwindi's Ruhija sector; encounters on gorilla treks are rare but memorable.
Leopards inhabit the forest but are very rarely seen due to the dense vegetation.
Buffalo herds enter forest clearings and woodland edges within the park.

name: Volcanoes NP
description: Virunga volcano slopes where Dian Fossey studied mountain gorillas; trekking permits allow close encounters.

sightings.notes: Rwanda's Volcanoes NP is the most accessible gorilla trekking destination in Africa; all treks are guided.
Forest elephants traverse the Virunga volcano slopes between Rwanda and DRC.
Leopards inhabit the dense montane forest but are very rarely encountered on gorilla treks.
Buffalo herds are commonly seen on the lower slopes and bamboo-forest edges of the volcanoes.
Spotted hyenas inhabit the forest edges and surrounding farmland around the park.
Golden monkey trekking in Volcanoes NP is highly successful; habituated groups of 60–80 individuals are found in the bamboo zone at 2,500–3,000 m alongside mountain gorillas.

name: Virunga NP
description: Africa's oldest national park, spanning volcanoes, savanna, and forest with mountain gorillas and forest elephants.

sightings.notes: Virunga protects over a third of all remaining mountain gorillas across its volcanic slopes.
Forest and savanna elephants inhabit different zones of this sprawling park.
Lake Edward holds one of Africa's largest hippo populations within the park's boundaries.
Savanna lions inhabit the Ishango sector along Lake Edward's shores.
The lowland forest of Virunga supports large chimpanzee communities studied for decades.
Golden monkeys are resident in Virunga's bamboo forest on the lower volcanic slopes; troops of 50+ individuals are encountered on guided walks.
Okapi, once feared locally extinct, were rediscovered in Virunga in 2006 and remain exceptionally rarely seen.

name: Queen Elizabeth NP
description: Uganda's most visited park, famous for tree-climbing lions in the Ishasha sector and large hippo schools.

sightings.notes: The Ishasha sector holds Uganda's famous tree-climbing lions, regularly seen resting in fig trees.
Large elephant herds are seen throughout the park, particularly in the Kasenyi and Mweya sectors.
The Kazinga Channel boat cruise passes dozens of hippo pods — one of Africa's best hippo spectacles.
The Kyambura Gorge hosts a habituated chimpanzee community; treks depart daily from the gorge entrance.
Leopards inhabit the thickets and woodland throughout the park; night drives offer the best sightings.
Shoebills are occasionally spotted in the Kazinga Channel papyrus and the Ishasha floodplains; less reliable than Murchison but documented.
Sitatunga inhabit the Kazinga Channel papyrus swamps of Queen Elizabeth NP; best spotted from boats in the early morning, wading at the papyrus edge.
Southern Ground Hornbills are commonly encountered striding through the Kasenyi plains and channel areas of Queen Elizabeth NP.

name: Murchison Falls NP
description: Uganda's largest park, where the Nile is forced through a 7-metre gap in spectacular falls above huge croc and hippo pools.

sightings.notes: Large elephant herds congregate along the Albert Nile; the north bank circuit offers excellent sightings.
The Albert Nile boat cruise to the falls base passes hundreds of hippos in the water.
Massive Nile crocodiles bask on the riverbanks throughout the Albert Nile boat cruise.
Resident lion prides hunt the open savanna on the north bank of the Albert Nile.
Rothschild giraffe — one of the world's rarest subspecies — are abundant on the Murchison north bank.
Murchison Falls is Uganda's premier Shoebill location; the Nile delta (Albert delta) has resident birds — boat trips into the papyrus swamps regularly encounter them standing motionless.

name: Kruger NP
description: South Africa's flagship park, hosting all of the Big Five across 20,000 km² of diverse bushveld.

sightings.notes: Kruger's 2,000+ lions make it one of Africa's best big cat destinations; prides are seen daily.
Around 20,000 elephants inhabit Kruger; herds are seen on virtually every game drive.
Kruger holds a high leopard density; river courses and rocky terrain in the south are hotspots.
South Africa holds the world's largest white rhino population; Kruger has over 6,000 individuals.
Enormous buffalo herds of hundreds are a daily sight throughout the park's savanna.

name: Hluhluwe-iMfolozi
description: KwaZulu-Natal park that saved the white rhinoceros from extinction; now home to both white and black rhino.

sightings.notes: The birthplace of white rhino conservation — Operation Rhino saved the species here; sightings are almost guaranteed.
A significant black rhino population inhabits the dense bush; morning drives in iMfolozi give the best sightings.
Lion prides were reintroduced and are now established; sightings are frequent on night drives.
A growing elephant population ranges across both the Hluhluwe and iMfolozi sections.
Large hyena clans are active throughout the park; night drives regularly encounter them hunting.

name: Pilanesberg NP
description: Ancient volcanic crater park in the North West Province, a Big Five destination a few hours from Johannesburg.

sightings.notes: Reintroduced prides are now well established in the volcanic crater; morning drives give reliable sightings.
A large elephant population ranges throughout the crater, frequently seen at waterholes.
White rhino are abundant and easy to find at the many waterholes throughout the park.
Leopards inhabit the rocky ridges of the crater but are elusive; night drives offer the best chance.
Hippos occupy the Mankwe Dam and surrounding water features at the park's centre.

name: Addo Elephant NP
description: Eastern Cape park that grew from saving 11 elephants in 1931 to over 600 today, plus black rhino and lion.

sightings.notes: Addo's 600+ elephants descend to the main waterhole daily in a continuous procession.
Reintroduced prides are established; nocturnal activity means night drives are best.
One of South Africa's best parks for black rhino; the dense Eastern Cape thicket is ideal habitat.
Buffalo herds number in the hundreds and are frequently seen at the main Addo waterhole.
Spotted hyena clans were reintroduced and are now thriving; active on night drives.

name: Kgalagadi Transfrontier Park
description: Vast Kalahari desert park straddling South Africa and Botswana, famed for black-maned lions and cheetahs against red dunes.

sightings.notes: Kgalagadi's black-maned lions are world-famous; prides habitually rest in the riverbed shade.
The open red sand dunes provide the ultimate backdrop for cheetah sightings; females with cubs are regularly seen.
Leopards inhabit the camelthorn trees bordering the dry riverbeds; night drives are productive.
Brown and spotted hyena coexist here; spotted hyena clans are active on night drives throughout the park.
Wild dogs occasionally pass through the Kgalagadi on wide-ranging movements; sightings are a special bonus.
Kgalagadi is superb for Kori Bustards; Africa's heaviest flying bird stalks the red dune grasslands and pans, and males inflate their throat sacs in display during the dry season.
The gemsbok is the defining herbivore of the Kalahari's red dunes; black-maned Kalahari lions hunting gemsbok is one of the park's signature sights.

name: Chobe NP
description: Home to Africa's largest elephant population — tens of thousands gather along the Chobe River in dry season.

sightings.notes: Africa's largest elephant population — tens of thousands — concentrates on the Chobe floodplain in dry season.
Large lion prides hunt the floodplain and woodland throughout the park.
Leopards inhabit the riverine forest and woodland fringing the Chobe River.
Large hyena clans are active throughout the park and commonly seen on morning and evening drives.
Enormous buffalo herds are seen throughout the Chobe and Savuti sections year-round.
Red lechwe are very common along the Chobe Riverfront floodplains; large herds are routinely seen running through the Chobe's seasonal waters.
Southern Ground Hornbills are frequently seen in Chobe's woodland and riverfront areas; family groups of up to 10 birds stride through the savanna in search of snakes and lizards.

name: Moremi Game Reserve
description: Heart of the Okavango Delta, a flooded mosaic of channels and islands with exceptional predator concentrations.

sightings.notes: Moremi's exceptional predator density makes it one of Africa's top lion-viewing destinations.
Elephant herds move throughout the delta's islands and channels; mokoro trips offer close encounters.
Moremi holds one of Africa's most stable wild dog populations; denning season (May–July) offers pup sightings.
The riverine forest and mopane woodland harbour high leopard densities.
Hyena clans are omnipresent in Moremi; their calls are one of the signature sounds of the delta at night.
Moremi's Okavango Delta papyrus islands hold sitatunga; mokoro (dugout canoe) trips into the waterways give the best views of this semi-aquatic antelope.
Red lechwe are abundant in Moremi; herds of hundreds splash through the shallow Okavango floodplains — one of Africa's most spectacular antelope sights.
Southern Ground Hornbills are a regular sight in Moremi's mixed woodland; their distinctive deep booming call carries far across the delta at dawn.

name: Hwange NP
description: Zimbabwe's largest national park with the country's largest elephant population — over 40,000 animals; artificial waterholes attract spectacular dry-season wildlife concentrations.

sightings.notes: Hwange's solar-powered waterholes attract thousands of elephants at a time in the dry season — one of Africa's great spectacles.
Hwange supports Zimbabwe's largest lion population; prides are regularly seen around the main waterholes.
One of Africa's most important wild dog strongholds; packs with large litters are seen April–August.
Massive buffalo herds of hundreds gather at Hwange's waterholes throughout the dry season.
Large hyena clans follow the dry-season prey concentrations; active on night drives.
Plains zebra are extremely abundant in Hwange; tens of thousands aggregate around waterholes during the dry season.
Blue wildebeest are common throughout Hwange, forming large herds in the open grasslands.
Giraffes are commonly seen browsing the mopane and acacia woodland throughout Hwange.
Warthogs are abundant throughout Hwange; families are a fixture at every waterhole.
Impala are the most numerous antelope in Hwange, forming large herds throughout the woodland.
Cheetahs are present in Hwange, particularly in the open grasslands of the south; sightings require luck.
White rhinos survive in Hwange despite heavy poaching pressure; sightings are rare and largely in protected areas.
Sable antelope inhabit the mopane woodland of Hwange; bulls' jet-black coats and sweeping horns are among Africa's most dramatic sights.
Common elands are regularly encountered in the open woodland and grassland of Hwange.

name: Mana Pools NP
description: UNESCO World Heritage Zambezi floodplain park where elephants balance on hind legs for acacia pods and African wild dogs hunt openly on the seasonal floodplain.

sightings.notes: Mana Pools' elephants are famous for standing on their hind legs to reach the ana tree pods above their heads.
Resident prides hunt the Zambezi floodplain year-round, with best viewing in the dry season.
Mana Pools is one of Zimbabwe's top wild dog destinations; habituated packs allow close observation on foot.
The Zambezi pools are teeming with large crocodiles; spectacular feeding frenzies occur at the pool edges.
Leopards inhabit the riverine forest fringing the Zambezi; night walks reveal them on hunting forays.
Hippos are abundant in the Zambezi channels and the Mana pools themselves throughout the year.
Plains zebra are very common on the Mana floodplain; large herds gather at the seasonal pools.
Impala are the dominant antelope on the Mana Pools floodplain; huge herds are visible from camp.
Warthogs are abundant throughout Mana Pools; they kneel to graze the open floodplain grass.
Spotted hyena clans patrol the Mana floodplain at night; they compete openly with lions and wild dogs for kills.
Giraffes are occasionally seen in the woodland zones above the Mana floodplain.
Common elands are occasionally seen in the acacia woodland above the Mana Pools floodplain.
Sable antelope occur in the miombo on the escarpment fringes above the Mana Pools valley.

name: South Luangwa NP
description: Birthplace of the walking safari; the Luangwa River and oxbow lagoons support one of Africa's highest leopard densities alongside enormous hippo pods and superb birdlife.

sightings.notes: South Luangwa's lions are among the most habituated in Africa — walking safaris bring memorable encounters.
South Luangwa is one of Africa's top leopard-viewing parks; night drives regularly find them hunting.
Elephant herds are seen daily along the Luangwa River; the dry season concentrations are spectacular.
The Luangwa River hosts one of Africa's highest hippo densities; thousands occupy the pools year-round.
Large Nile crocodiles bask on every sandbank along the Luangwa River.
Plains zebra are extremely abundant in South Luangwa, forming herds of hundreds on the floodplains.
Blue wildebeest are common in the open grasslands of the South Luangwa valley.
Impala are the most numerous antelope in South Luangwa; enormous herds are present everywhere.
Warthogs are abundant throughout South Luangwa; families are seen at every waterhole and open area.
Thornicroft's giraffe — a subspecies endemic to the Luangwa Valley — is commonly seen browsing the acacia woodland.
Puku are a near-endemic Zambian antelope; South Luangwa holds one of the largest populations — they graze the floodplain in great numbers.
Sable antelope occur in the miombo woodland on the escarpment above the Luangwa Valley.
Common elands move through the woodland zones of South Luangwa; large bulls are occasionally seen near waterholes.

name: Kafue NP
description: Zambia's largest and oldest national park, roughly the size of Wales; the Kafue River floodplain hosts important populations of African wild dogs, cheetahs, and lions.

sightings.notes: Kafue's Busanga Plains host one of Zambia's most productive lion-viewing areas.
Leopards are common throughout Kafue; the riverine woodlands along the Kafue River are productive.
Kafue holds Zambia's largest wild dog population; packs are regularly tracked on the Busanga Plains.
Large elephant herds are found throughout the park's vast woodland and floodplain zones.
The open Busanga Plains are prime cheetah habitat; small coalitions and mother-cub groups are seen.
Hippos are abundant in the Kafue River and its floodplain lagoons throughout the park.
Large Nile crocodiles inhabit the Kafue River and all its major tributaries.
Plains zebra are common throughout Kafue; large herds graze the Busanga Plains in the dry season.
Blue wildebeest are common on the Kafue floodplain, particularly in the Busanga area.
Impala are the most numerous antelope throughout Kafue NP.
Warthogs are abundant throughout Kafue in all habitats.
The Kafue Lechwe is endemic to the Kafue Flats and found nowhere else on Earth; herds of tens of thousands wade through the flooded grasslands.
Puku are abundant throughout Kafue's floodplain grasslands alongside the lechwe; a Zambian near-endemic.
Sable antelope inhabit the miombo woodland throughout central and northern Kafue NP.
Common elands are encountered throughout Kafue's mixed woodland and grassland habitat.

name: Etosha NP
description: Namibia's premier wildlife park centred on a vast salt pan visible from space; waterholes provide some of Africa's most reliable big game sightings with lions, rhinos, elephants, and cheetahs.

sightings.notes: Hundreds of elephants gather at Etosha's mineral springs daily during the dry season.
Lions are commonly seen at waterholes day and night; Etosha's floodlit camps allow nocturnal viewing.
Etosha holds one of Africa's largest black rhino populations; Okaukuejo waterhole is particularly productive.
Cheetah are frequently seen on the flat open plains and around waterholes in the dry season.
Angola giraffe are abundant throughout Etosha's savanna; their arrival at waterholes is a spectacular sight.
Plains zebra are extremely abundant at Etosha; enormous herds gather at the salt pan waterholes in the dry season.
Blue wildebeest are common throughout Etosha, forming large herds on the open grass plains.
Ostriches are commonly seen striding across the open Etosha plains; males display their dramatic plumage year-round.
Spotted hyena clans are common around the waterholes at night; their whooping calls are a signature sound of Etosha.
Warthogs are ubiquitous throughout Etosha; families trot across the open plains and kneel to graze.
White rhinos were reintroduced to the Mushara area of Etosha; encounters are rare but possible in the eastern section.
Wild dogs occasionally move through Etosha; sightings are rare as packs range widely outside the park.
Impala are abundant throughout the eastern section of Etosha; they form the primary prey base for cheetahs and leopards.
Gemsbok are one of Etosha's most iconic species; huge herds are seen on the pan and at waterholes — superbly adapted to desert heat.
Springbok are extraordinarily abundant at Etosha; herds of thousands gather at the waterholes and perform the bounding "pronking" display.
The black-faced impala is a Namibian endemic restricted to the north-west; Etosha is one of the few places in the world to see this vulnerable subspecies.
Common elands are regularly seen in the woodland bordering the Etosha pan; bulls in small groups, cows in larger herds.

name: Bale Mountains NP
description: Afro-alpine plateau park protecting the endangered Ethiopian Wolf and Mountain Nyala.

sightings.notes: Lion are present in the Harenna forest and woodland zones of the park.
Leopards inhabit the afroalpine moorland and surrounding forest; tracks are regularly found.
Spotted hyena are commonly seen, particularly around livestock farming areas at park edges.
Buffalo herds inhabit the forest zones of Bale Mountains.
Common warthog are abundant in the grassland and open heath zones of the plateau.
The Sanetti Plateau in Bale Mountains holds the world's largest population of Ethiopian wolves; packs are seen daily hunting giant mole rats on the open moorland.
Bale Mountains NP is the global stronghold for Mountain Nyala; large bulls with magnificent spiral horns are commonly seen in the Harenna forest edge.
Gelada troops inhabit the escarpment edges of Bale Mountains; the park holds a smaller population than Simien but they are reliably encountered on the plateau.

name: Simien Mountains NP
description: UNESCO World Heritage highland park with dramatic escarpments, home to Gelada baboons and Walia ibex.

sightings.notes: Leopards inhabit the rocky escarpments and forest zones; nocturnal tracks are regularly recorded.
Spotted hyena are frequently seen around villages and camping areas at dusk.
Common warthog graze the montane grassland throughout the plateau.
Small antelopes inhabit the lower-elevation woodland at the park's edges.
Buffalo herds are often seen grazing the high plateau meadows.
Ethiopian wolves are present in the high moorland of Simien Mountains; a smaller population than Bale, but sightings are recorded on the plateau walks.
Simien Mountains is the world's best place to see geladas; troops of hundreds graze the escarpment edge and can be approached on foot within metres.
Simien Mountains is the only place on Earth where Walia Ibex can be seen; small groups cling to the sheer northern escarpment cliffs above 3,000 m.

name: Gonarezhou NP
description: Wild southeastern Zimbabwe park whose name means "place of elephants" — large herds roam its mopane floodplains.

sightings.notes: Gonarezhou means "place of elephants" — enormous herds gather along the Runde and Save rivers.
Large prides hunt the floodplains and mopane woodland throughout the park.
Hippo pods occupy pools along the Runde River, especially during the dry season.
Leopards are present throughout but secretive; rocky outcrops along the river are favoured.
Hyena clans are active across the mopane woodland especially during and after rains.

name: Liwonde NP
description: Shire River park with recovering populations of elephant, black rhino, lion, and wild dog after decades of protection.

sightings.notes: Liwonde's Shire River boat trips encounter large elephant herds drinking and bathing.
Black rhinos reintroduced under African Parks' management are now regularly tracked on foot.
Lions were reintroduced in 2018 and are now established; prides are tracked daily.
Wild dog packs were reintroduced and are now breeding; pup sightings delight visitors in season.
Shire River boat safaris pass hippo pods of 30–50 animals — one of Malawi's greatest wildlife experiences.

name: Pendjari NP
description: West Africa's most important predator stronghold, with significant lion, cheetah, and elephant populations.

sightings.notes: Pendjari holds West Africa's most significant lion population; prides are tracked by the park's Lion Guardians.
One of the very few remaining places in West Africa where cheetah can still be found.
Large elephant herds graze the floodplains bordering Burkina Faso's W NP.
Pendjari is one of the last refuges for wild dogs in West Africa; occasional sightings are reported.
Leopards are present throughout the park's gallery forest and rocky outcrops.

name: Garamba NP
description: UNESCO World Heritage park that once hosted the last Northern White Rhinos; still shelters elephant and giraffe.

sightings.notes: Garamba is a critical refuge for forest and savanna elephants; numbers are recovering under protection.
The Kordofan giraffe subspecies is found in Garamba — one of the rarest giraffe subspecies.
Lion prides inhabit the savanna woodland of this vast and remote park.
Leopards are present but rarely encountered given the park's remoteness and dense vegetation.
The Dungu and Garamba rivers host large hippo populations throughout the park.

name: Lopé NP
description: UNESCO-listed rainforest-savanna mosaic with forest elephants, western lowland gorillas, and mandrills.

sightings.notes: Lopé holds over 3,000 chimpanzees across its mosaic of forest and ancient savanna.
Forest elephants are abundant at Lopé, often seen in the open ancient savannas between forest blocks.
Leopards are the top predator in Lopé and regularly leave tracks and scrapes on forest trails.
Forest buffalo herds inhabit the park's ancient savanna clearings.
Common warthog graze the savanna openings throughout the UNESCO World Heritage site.
Lopé NP holds one of the highest densities of Western Lowland Gorillas in Central Africa; habituated groups are tracked through the gallery forest and savanna mosaic.
Lopé NP is one of the world's very best places to see mandrills; super-troops of 300–800 animals sweep through the forest — a staggering wildlife spectacle.

name: Ranomafana NP
description: Cloud-forest park discovered to harbour multiple lemur species including the greater bamboo lemur.

sightings.notes: Indri are present at Ranomafana's higher elevations; their haunting calls carry through the forest.
Several lemur species including ring-tails inhabit Ranomafana's mid-altitude forest.
Madagascar's native Nile crocodile population inhabits the rivers flowing through Ranomafana.
Flamingos occur in wetland areas near Ranomafana at lower elevations.
Ospreys patrol the river systems within and surrounding the national park.
The Fossa is Madagascar's apex predator and is present in Ranomafana; sightings are rare and require patience on night walks, but it is reliably camera-trapped here.
Ranomafana is one of Madagascar's best parks for Aye-aye; specialist night walks with experienced guides regularly locate them by their tapping behaviour on tree limbs.
Ranomafana holds Milne-Edwards' sifaka rather than Verreaux's; if present at edges of the dry corridor species' range, sightings are rare.

name: Andasibe-Mantadia NP
description: Eastern Madagascar rainforest famous for the calls of the Indri and home to a dozen lemur species.

sightings.notes: The Indri's haunting wail is the signature sound of Andasibe; groups are tracked daily by guides.
Multiple lemur species including ring-tails inhabit the forests of both Andasibe and Mantadia.
Crocodiles inhabit the rivers and wetlands within the Andasibe forest reserve complex.
Flamingos are sometimes seen in the lowland marshes bordering the Andasibe complex.
Ospreys are resident around water features in the Andasibe forest.
The Fossa is present in Andasibe-Mantadia's rainforest; night-time encounters near the park's streams are the most likely scenario for a sighting.
Aye-ayes are present in Andasibe-Mantadia; night walks in the Mantadia sector offer the best prospects, as they forage in the canopy for wood-boring grubs.

name: Isalo NP
description: Sandstone canyon landscape where ring-tailed lemurs and Verreaux's sifaka are seen daily along trails.

sightings.notes: Ring-tailed lemurs are the flagship species of Isalo; troops are seen daily on park trails.
Crocodiles inhabit the deeper pools and streams flowing through the sandstone canyons.
Flamingos occasionally gather at saline pans in the drier landscapes bordering Isalo.
Ospreys hunt along the river systems flowing through Isalo's canyon landscape.
Large raptors including fish eagles patrol water bodies within and around Isalo.
The Fossa inhabits Isalo's canyon forests; night sightings near the natural pools and rocky canyon walls are occasionally reported by guided groups.
Isalo NP is a prime location for Verreaux's Sifaka; these white dancing lemurs are seen daily in the canyon vegetation, and their sideways bounding across rocky clearings is unmissable.

name: Tsavo East NP
description: Kenya's largest park — vast semi-arid plains known for red-dust elephants and large prides of maneless lions.

sightings.notes: Tsavo East's iconic red-dust elephants are seen in large herds on the vast open plains.
Maneless lion prides hunt the open plains; Aruba Dam is a reliable waterhole sighting spot.
Leopards are present in riverine forest corridors and rocky outcrops throughout the park.
Hippo pods are found in the Galana River; boat trips from Crocodile Camp provide close views.
Cheetah are present on the open Yatta Plateau plains in the northern section of the park.

name: Ranthambore NP
description: Historic Rajasthan fortress park; one of India's best places to photograph Bengal tigers in the open.

sightings.notes: Ranthambore offers some of India's best tiger sightings, especially around the lakes Oct–May.
Sambar deer are the primary prey species and are seen in large numbers around the park's lakes.
Ospreys winter at Ranthambore's lakes, diving for fish in the park's three main reservoirs.
Ranthambore's rocky hills and lakeside cliffs hold a healthy leopard population that thrives alongside the reserve's tigers.

name: Kanha NP
description: Madhya Pradesh meadow park that inspired Kipling's Jungle Book; strong populations of tiger and barasingha deer.

sightings.notes: Kanha's open meadows give exceptional tiger sightings; the park inspired Rudyard Kipling's Jungle Book.
Sambar are abundant throughout Kanha's sal forest and meadow zones.
Kanha holds one of central India's most significant gaur populations in its teak-sal forest.
Osprey winter at Kanha's water bodies and the Banjar River flowing through the reserve.
Kanha is where the hard-ground barasingha was brought back from fewer than 66 individuals in the 1970s to a thriving population today — the park's signature conservation story.
Leopards inhabit Kanha's sal forests and are seen resting on tree limbs, though they stay more hidden than the park's tigers.

name: Bandhavgarh NP
description: India's highest tiger density per km², with sal forests and ancient caves providing dramatic sighting backdrops.

sightings.notes: India's highest tiger density per area; safaris into the core zone offer near-guaranteed sightings.
Sambar herds are abundant throughout Bandhavgarh's varied sal forest and meadow habitat.
Gaur herds inhabit the denser forest sections; impressive bull encounters are not uncommon.
Elephants occasionally move into the park from adjacent forest during the monsoon season.
Migrant ospreys winter along Bandhavgarh's streams and water bodies.
Bandhavgarh has one of India's highest leopard densities, often spotted on the fort's rocky slopes.

name: Kaziranga NP
description: UNESCO World Heritage Assam park hosting two-thirds of the world's one-horned rhinoceroses and exceptional tiger density.

sightings.notes: Kaziranga harbours two-thirds of the world's one-horned rhinos; sightings are almost guaranteed year-round.
Kaziranga has one of India's highest tiger densities; open grassland makes sightings relatively easy.
Large elephant herds frequent the park's floodplain grasslands and woodland edges.
Gaur inhabit the woodland and grassland transition zones throughout the park.
Ospreys winter along the Brahmaputra floodplain and park waterbodies.
Fishing Cats inhabit the wetland margins and river islands of Kaziranga; camera traps have documented them regularly and jeep safaris along the Brahmaputra channels occasionally produce sightings.
Kaziranga holds the world's largest population of the Eastern swamp deer subspecies, grazing the park's tall grasslands in large herds.
Leopards are present in Kaziranga's forested fringes, though far less conspicuous than the park's rhinos, tigers, and elephants.
Kaziranga holds the large majority of the world's genetically pure wild water buffalo, grazing the floodplain grasslands in large herds.

name: Jim Corbett NP
description: India's oldest national park, in the Himalayan foothills, with tigers, leopards, and Asian elephants.

sightings.notes: Corbett is India's oldest tiger reserve; the Dhikala grasslands provide excellent open sightings.
Corbett supports over 600 wild elephants; herds are seen daily in the Ramganga riverbed.
Sambar are ubiquitous throughout Corbett's mixed forest and grassland zones.
Ospreys are a common winter visitor to the Ramganga reservoir and river system.
India's oldest national park holds a strong leopard population in its sal forest and grassland edges.

name: Sundarbans NP
description: World's largest tidal mangrove forest, the only tiger habitat where Bengal tigers regularly hunt by swimming.

sightings.notes: Sundarbans tigers are world-famous swimmers; boat safaris navigate the mangrove waterways hoping for a glimpse.
Saltwater crocodiles inhabit the tidal channels of the Sundarbans; regular sightings from boats.
Ospreys are common wintering raptors along the Sundarbans waterways.
White-bellied sea eagles are year-round residents, hunting fish along the tidal channels.
Sambar deer inhabit the mangrove islands and are the main prey of Sundarbans tigers.
The Sundarbans mangrove forest is one of the few places to see Fishing Cats in their prime habitat; the cats wade through tidal creeks and are occasionally spotted from boats in the creek network.

name: Gir Forest NP
description: Last wild habitat of the Asiatic Lion; home to around 700 individuals, the only population outside Africa.

sightings.notes: Gir is the only place on Earth to see wild Asiatic lions; encounters are almost guaranteed on safari.
Sambar are the primary prey of Gir's lions and are abundant in the forest.
Ospreys winter at Gir's water bodies and the Kamleshwar reservoir.
Gir has one of India's highest leopard densities, living alongside the Asiatic lion in the same forest.

name: Nagarhole NP
description: Karnataka park with fertile teak forests, the best elephant counts in southern India, and reliable leopard sightings.

sightings.notes: Nagarhole's teak forest holds strong tiger populations; the Kabini reservoir zone is most productive.
The Kabini backwaters host spectacular dry-season elephant gatherings — among India's best.
Gaur are abundant in Nagarhole and regularly seen along forest roads and at water.
Sambar herds graze the open grassland clearings along the Kabini River daily.
Ospreys are abundant winter visitors at the Kabini reservoir, one of India's top birding spots.
Leopards share Nagarhole's deciduous forest with tigers, dholes, and elephants, often seen along the Kabini backwaters.

name: Periyar WS
description: Kerala tiger reserve built around a reservoir; boat safaris offer close views of wild elephants bathing at the shore.

sightings.notes: Periyar's boat safaris on the Periyar Lake offer close encounters with bathing elephant herds.
Tigers are present but rarely seen; camera trap images show healthy numbers in the dense forest.
Gaur herds are seen on the lakeshore grasslands, particularly in the dry season months.
Sambar deer wade into the shallows of Periyar Lake and are seen from boat safaris.
Ospreys winter on the Periyar reservoir, diving for fish alongside local fishermen.
Leopards move through Periyar's hill forests, occasionally seen crossing forest roads at dawn and dusk.

name: Chitwan NP
description: UNESCO World Heritage Terai park, Nepal's premier destination for one-horned rhino and Bengal tiger.

sightings.notes: Rhino sightings are virtually guaranteed at Chitwan — the best time is the dry season elephant-back and jeep safaris.
Chitwan holds Nepal's most accessible tiger population; the Bis Hazari wetland is a reliable spot.
Wild elephant herds move through the park from adjacent forest; domestic elephants are used for safaris.
Sambar are plentiful in Chitwan's sal forest and riverine grasslands.
Leopards occur in Chitwan's forest edges, though sightings are less frequent than the park's tigers and rhinos.

name: Bardia NP
description: Remote western Terai park offering excellent tiger and rhino sightings away from Chitwan's crowds.

sightings.notes: Bardia offers Nepal's best chance of multiple tiger sightings; the Karnali floodplain is prime territory.
Rhinos have recovered strongly in Bardia; dry-season safaris on the Babai floodplain give good sightings.
Wild elephant herds move through Bardia from India; large groups are seen in the Karnali riverside.
Sambar are abundant throughout Bardia's sal forest and riverine grassland.
Ospreys winter along the Karnali River and are a regular sight on morning boat safaris.
Bardia's remote sal forests hold leopards alongside tigers, though dense cover makes them harder to spot.

name: Sagarmatha NP
description: Mount Everest's UNESCO-listed park; high-altitude home of snow leopard, red panda, and Himalayan tahr.

sightings.notes: Snow leopards inhabit the high ridges above 4,000m; camera traps confirm a resident population.
Red pandas inhabit the temperate bamboo-rhododendron forest below 4,000m in the Pharak zone.
Deer inhabit the lower forested zones of the park below the treeline.
Ospreys are occasionally seen passing through on autumn migration over the high Himalayan passes.

name: Jigme Dorji NP
description: Bhutan's largest park, spanning subtropical jungle to alpine meadows — a critical corridor for snow leopard and Bengal tiger.

sightings.notes: Snow leopards inhabit the high alpine pastures; winter brings them to lower elevations following prey.
Jigme Dorji bridges Himalayan and subtropical zones; tigers inhabit the lowland subtropical forest.
Elephant herds move through the lower subtropical zones connecting to Indian forest corridors.
Red pandas are relatively common in the temperate bamboo forest of Jigme Dorji.
Sambar are the most commonly encountered large mammal in the park's mid-altitude forests.
Takin are Bhutan's national animal and inhabit Jigme Dorji NP's bamboo forests and high pastures; most reliably seen in the warmer months when herds move to higher meadows.
Leopards range through Jigme Dorji's lower forested valleys below the snow leopard's alpine territory.

name: Khao Yai NP
description: UNESCO World Heritage park 3 hours from Bangkok; one of Asia's finest for wild elephants, gibbons, and hornbills.

sightings.notes: Khao Yai's 300+ elephants are frequently seen on the park road, especially at salt licks at dusk.
Large gaur herds are regularly seen grazing the open grassland areas within the park.
Tapirs are nocturnal but reliably encountered on night drives along the main park road.
Sambar are abundant and easily seen grazing on the open golf course clearings at dusk.
Ospreys winter at Khao Yai's reservoirs and are among the most spectacular wintering raptors.

name: Huai Kha Khaeng WS
description: Western Thailand UNESCO site with Thailand's most viable tiger and wild elephant populations.

sightings.notes: Thailand's most important tiger stronghold; camera traps document a healthy breeding population.
Large elephant herds are seen at salt licks and on the forest roads within the sanctuary.
Gaur are the most frequently encountered large mammal on game drives in the sanctuary.
Tapirs are regularly seen on night drives; the sanctuary holds an important population.
Sambar herds graze the open areas along the Huai Kha Khaeng River corridor.

name: Gunung Leuser NP
description: Sumatra's northern UNESCO rainforest, one of the last places all four of Sumatra's flagship species coexist.

sightings.notes: Sumatran tigers inhabit the dense lowland forest; camera traps confirm a remnant population.
Tapirs are relatively commonly encountered by researchers and trackers in Leuser's lowland forest.
Sumatran elephant herds inhabit Leuser's forest corridors; Tangkahan is an excellent access point.
Sun bears are present throughout Leuser's lowland rainforest; tree hollows and honey are their focus.
Saltwater crocodiles inhabit the tidal rivers at the forest's coastal edge.

Gunung Leuser NP is one of only two places on Earth with wild orangutans, and the last place where tigers, rhinos, elephants, and orangutans still coexist; the Bukit Lawang area offers reliable sightings.

name: Bukit Barisan Selatan NP
description: Southern Sumatra's UNESCO forest park sheltering Sumatran tigers, elephants, and critically endangered Sumatran rhinos.

sightings.notes: One of the last strongholds for Sumatran tigers; camera trap monitoring reveals ongoing presence.
Sumatran elephant herds inhabit the park's lowland forest; Way Kambas borders the park to the east.
Tapirs inhabit the lowland forest and are regularly encountered on research trails.
Sun bears are present throughout the park's diverse forest zones.
Saltwater crocodiles are present in coastal and estuarine habitats at the park's margins.
Bukit Barisan Selatan holds one of the last few Sumatran rhino populations on Earth; dense forest cover makes sightings extremely rare.

name: Taman Negara NP
description: One of the world's oldest rainforests, covering 130 million years of history with tigers, tapirs, and gaur.

sightings.notes: Tapirs are regularly detected at Taman Negara's hides and salt licks; best seen at dusk.
Elephant herds move through Taman Negara's ancient forest; hide waits and river cruises increase chances.
Gaur visit the salt licks within Taman Negara regularly and are seen on guided night walks.
Sun bears are present but rarely encountered; scratch marks on trees indicate their presence.
Taman Negara is one of the last strongholds of the critically endangered Malayan tiger; camera traps confirm their presence but sightings are exceptionally rare.

name: Kinabatangan WS
description: Borneo's second-longest river corridor where Bornean orangutans, proboscis monkeys, and pygmy elephants congregate.

sightings.notes: Orangutans are seen daily along the Kinabatangan River; river cruises almost always produce sightings.
Kinabatangan holds the world's highest density of proboscis monkeys; large groups are seen daily at dusk.
Bornean pygmy elephants — the world's smallest elephant — are regularly seen in family groups on the riverbank.
Sun bears are present in the riverside forest and occasionally observed from river boats.
Large saltwater crocodiles are regularly seen on the Kinabatangan riverbanks on evening cruises.

The Lower Kinabatangan is the best place in the world to see Bornean Pygmy Elephants; family herds of up to 60 animals feed along the riverbanks and are observed from river boats — an unmissable Borneo wildlife experience.

name: Danum Valley CA
description: Pristine Bornean lowland rainforest rarely explored by tourists, with extremely high wildlife density.

sightings.notes: Danum Valley offers some of Borneo's best orangutan viewing in pristine unlogged rainforest.
Proboscis monkeys gather in large riverside trees at dusk to sleep; river walks are productive.
Sun bears are more frequently encountered at Danum than most Borneo sites due to pristine habitat.
Tapirs are present in the lowland forest and recorded on camera traps throughout the reserve.
Crocodiles inhabit the Segama River that flows through the Danum Valley reserve.
Bornean Pygmy Elephants inhabit the primary lowland rainforest of Danum Valley; sightings of herds at salt licks and on forest trails are regularly recorded.

name: Wolong NR
description: Sichuan's most important giant panda reserve, key to the species' captive breeding and reintroduction programme.

sightings.notes: Wolong is China's premier panda reserve; the captive breeding centre allows close encounters, and wild pandas are tracked.
Red pandas inhabit the bamboo-rhododendron forest at mid-elevation and are regularly tracked by researchers.
Sambar are the most visible large herbivore in the forest zones of Wolong.
Snow leopards inhabit the highest ridges of the Qionglai Mountains above the panda zone.
Ospreys winter along the Pitiao River flowing through the Wolong valley.

name: Sanjiangyuan NP
description: Vast Tibetan Plateau park — the headwaters of the Yangtze, Yellow, and Mekong rivers, home to snow leopard and Tibetan antelope.

sightings.notes: Snow leopards are the apex predator of Sanjiangyuan's high Tibetan Plateau; camera traps document good numbers.
Tibetan wolves roam the high plateau in family packs, preying on pika and Tibetan antelope.
Ospreys breed along the rivers of Sanjiangyuan and are seen fishing during the summer months.
Sanjiangyuan NP sits on the core of the Tibetan Plateau and holds the world's largest Tibetan Antelope population; thousands migrate to calving grounds in summer across the vast steppe.
Wild yak herds graze Sanjiangyuan's high plateau grassland at the source of the Yangtze, Yellow, and Mekong rivers.
Herds of kiang roam the open steppe across Sanjiangyuan's vast protected area.

name: Yala NP
description: Sri Lanka's most visited park, with one of the world's highest leopard densities and large elephant herds.

sightings.notes: Sambar are abundant throughout Yala's scrub forest and lagoons — the primary prey of Yala's leopards.
Sri Lankan elephants are frequently seen in the park's open areas and at waterholes.
Ospreys winter in large numbers at Yala's coastal lagoons — one of Sri Lanka's best birding sites.
White-bellied sea eagles are year-round residents of Yala's coastal lagoon system.
Yala Block I has the world's highest density of wild leopards; Sri Lankan Leopards are the apex predator here and are seen on virtually every game drive, resting in trees or hunting in the scrubland.

name: Wilpattu NP
description: Sri Lanka's largest park, with natural sand-basin lakes sheltering leopards, sloth bears, and Asian elephants.

sightings.notes: Elephant herds are commonly seen at Wilpattu's characteristic sand-basin lakes (villus) throughout the year.
Sambar deer are very abundant at Wilpattu and easily seen at the park's many villus.
Ospreys winter at Wilpattu's water bodies and are seen regularly on morning drives.
White-bellied sea eagles are resident along Wilpattu's coastal zone and inland water bodies.
Wilpattu NP is Sri Lanka's largest park and its ancient "willus" (natural lakes) attract leopards; sightings have increased significantly since the park's reopening after the civil war.
Fishing Cats are present in Wilpattu's willus (lake) edges and seasonal waterways; Sri Lanka holds a significant population of this vulnerable semi-aquatic wildcat.

name: Minneriya NP
description: Famous for "The Gathering" — hundreds of Asian elephants congregate at the Minneriya tank in August–October.

sightings.notes: The Gathering — up to 400 elephants converging on the Minneriya tank — is one of Asia's greatest wildlife spectacles.
Sambar are seen daily grazing the grassy fringes of the Minneriya reservoir.
Ospreys winter at Minneriya's large reservoir and are frequently seen plunge-diving for fish.
White-bellied sea eagles are resident at the Minneriya tank and are seen on most game drives.

name: Shiretoko NP
description: UNESCO World Heritage peninsula in Hokkaido, with a high density of brown bears, Steller sea eagles, and red foxes.

sightings.notes: Hokkaido's brown bears gather at salmon rivers in autumn — one of Asia's best bear-watching spectacles.
Hokkaido is the only place in Japan with moose; they are regularly seen along forest roads in Shiretoko.
Ospreys breed along Shiretoko's coastline and fish rivers; summer sightings are almost guaranteed.
Steller's sea eagles — the world's most powerful eagle — winter at Shiretoko in large numbers.

Shiretoko is the world's premier location for Steller's Sea Eagles; hundreds of these massive birds winter on the sea ice off the Shiretoko Peninsula, fishing through leads in the pack ice — a globally unique spectacle.

name: Golestan NP
description: Iran's first national park, with Caspian tigers now lost but still sheltering leopard, bear, and wild boar.

sightings.notes: Brown bears inhabit the Hyrcanian forest of Golestan; one of Iran's best mammal-watching parks.
Wolves roam Golestan in family packs; howling is often heard from the camp at night.
Caspian Red Deer are among the most commonly encountered large mammals in Golestan's forest.
Lynx are present in Golestan's dense forest; camera traps confirm their presence but sightings are rare.
Ospreys winter along the rivers and reservoir within Golestan NP.
Golestan NP holds one of the world's most important Persian leopard populations; camera-trap studies confirm regular use of the park's rugged terrain.

name: Arabian Oryx Sanctuary
description: Desert sanctuary that spearheaded the Arabian oryx's recovery from global extinction through captive breeding.

sightings.notes: The sanctuary holds the largest population of reintroduced Arabian oryx in the world; sightings are reliable.
Ospreys winter along the Omani coast adjacent to the sanctuary.
Large raptors patrol the sanctuary's wadis and coastal margins.
Arabian wolves — one of the smallest wolf subspecies — occasionally move through the sanctuary.
Nubian ibex inhabit the rockier fringes of the Jiddat al-Harasis plateau surrounding the sanctuary.
The sanctuary holds the largest wild population of Arabian gazelle anywhere on the peninsula.

name: Wadi Rum Protected Area
description: Dramatic red sandstone desert landscape supporting Arabian oryx, ibex, and a range of desert foxes and raptors.

sightings.notes: Arabian oryx were reintroduced to Wadi Rum and are now regularly seen in family groups among the dunes.
Ospreys pass through Wadi Rum on migration along the Rift Valley flyway.
Arabian wolves inhabit the wider Wadi Rum region and occasionally enter the protected area.
Nubian ibex inhabit Wadi Rum's sandstone mountains, recovering through anti-poaching enforcement and captive breeding.
Sand cat was confirmed in Wadi Rum in 2021, the first official record since the 1970s.

name: Yellowstone NP
description: America's first national park, sitting atop a supervolcano and hosting the most complete large-mammal ecosystem in the lower 48 states.

sightings.notes: Yellowstone's Lamar Valley grizzlies are among the most watched bears in North America; sows with cubs are seen spring–autumn.
Reintroduced in 1995, the Lamar Valley pack is the world's most observed wolf pack; dawn is best for sightings.
Over 4,000 bison form the largest wild bison herd in North America, blocking roads throughout the park.
Over 10,000 elk inhabit the park; autumn bugling in the Mammoth Hot Springs area is spectacular.
Mountain lions are present but extremely rarely seen; the northern range offers the best chance.

name: Yosemite NP
description: Sierra Nevada granite valley famed for giant sequoias, black bears, mule deer, and peregrine falcons.

sightings.notes: Around 500 black bears live in Yosemite; sightings are common in the Valley and along Tioga Road.
Mountain lions are present throughout Yosemite's wilderness but very rarely encountered.
Coyotes are frequently seen trotting across Yosemite Valley meadows at dawn and dusk.
Mule deer graze Yosemite Valley meadows year-round and are one of the park's most visible animals.
Bald eagles winter at Yosemite's reservoirs; a nesting pair has returned to the Valley in recent years.

name: Grand Canyon NP
description: One of Earth's great natural wonders; California condors soar the thermals and mule deer roam the South Rim.

sightings.notes: Mountain lions inhabit both North and South Rim forests; rare sightings are reported each year.
Coyotes are ubiquitous on the South Rim and frequently seen near visitor areas.
Desert bighorn sheep are seen on the canyon walls near Bright Angel Trail and at the Colorado River.
Mule deer graze the South Rim meadows and are commonly seen from the canyon viewpoints.
Bald and golden eagles winter along the Colorado River corridor through the canyon.

name: Everglades NP
description: World's largest subtropical wilderness, a UNESCO site protecting alligators, Florida panthers, and manatees.

sightings.notes: Alligators are the icon of the Everglades; dozens are seen on the Anhinga Trail daily year-round.
Spoonbills flock to the Everglades for the dry season; Mrazek Pond offers spectacular close views.
Bald eagles breed in the Everglades and are regularly seen soaring over the freshwater sloughs.
Ospreys are year-round residents of the Everglades, nesting on channel markers and dead trees.
White-tailed deer are abundant in the Everglades pineland and hammock habitats.

The Florida Panther is one of North America's rarest large cats, with fewer than 200 individuals surviving; camera traps document them throughout the Big Cypress and Everglades, though seeing one in the wild is extraordinarily rare.
West Indian Manatees congregate in the warm springs and coastal rivers of the Everglades in winter; they are commonly seen from boardwalks and kayaks in Florida Bay and the park's creek systems.

name: Great Smoky Mountains NP
description: Most visited US national park, home to 1,500 black bears and 19 species of salamander in ancient Appalachian forests.

sightings.notes: The Smokies host the highest density of black bears in the eastern US; Cades Cove is a top sighting spot.
White-tailed deer are seen daily in Cades Cove meadows, especially at dawn and dusk.
Coyotes have colonised the Smokies and are commonly heard howling; sightings are increasing.
Reintroduced elk have established a herd of ~200; the Cataloochee Valley offers reliable sightings.
Bald eagles winter along Fontana Lake and Abrams Creek; the species is recovering in the region.

name: Olympic NP
description: Diverse Washington State park spanning temperate rainforest, alpine meadows, and Pacific coastline with Roosevelt elk herds.

sightings.notes: Black bears inhabit Olympic's rainforest and alpine zones; the Hoh Rainforest trail is productive.
Mountain lions inhabit Olympic's wilderness; rare sightings occur on the park's remote trail system.
Roosevelt elk — the largest elk subspecies — inhabit the Hoh Rainforest in herds of up to 100.
Bald eagles are year-round residents of Olympic's coastline and river systems.
Ospreys breed throughout Olympic and are seen fishing in every river system in summer.

name: Denali NP
description: Alaska's crown jewel, surrounding North America's highest peak, with grizzly bears, wolves, caribou, and moose.

sightings.notes: Denali's grizzlies are the park's icon; the park road provides unmatched access to undisturbed bears digging for roots.
Denali's wolf packs are among the most studied in the world; the East Fork pack is most regularly seen.
The Denali caribou herd of ~2,000 animals is seen throughout the park in summer.
Moose are frequently seen along the park road and in spruce forest throughout the park.
Bald eagles nest along the Nenana River and are commonly seen fishing in Denali's rivers.
Pure-white Dall Sheep are among Denali's most iconic wildlife; large rams with massive curling horns are commonly seen on Polychrome Overlook and the rocky ridges above the park road.

name: Glacier NP
description: Montana's "Crown of the Continent," one of the few US parks retaining all native carnivores including grizzly, wolf, and wolverine.

sightings.notes: Glacier holds one of the densest grizzly populations in the lower 48; Many Glacier Valley is the top spot.
Wolves have recolonised Glacier and are occasionally seen along the North Fork and Two Medicine areas.
Moose are seen throughout Glacier's wetland habitats; Bowman Lake and McDonald Creek are hotspots.
Rocky Mountain bighorn sheep are seen on the alpine meadows along the Going-to-the-Sun Road.
Mountain lions are present throughout Glacier; tracks and sightings occur annually near the North Fork.

name: Rocky Mountain NP
description: Colorado's high-altitude park where elk rut spectacularly in autumn meadows at over 3,000 metres elevation.

sightings.notes: Rocky Mountain NP has the best elk rut spectacle in North America; autumn bugling in Moraine Park is unmissable.
Moose inhabit the willow-fringed wetlands in the park's west side; Kawuneeche Valley is reliable.
Coyotes are seen in meadows throughout the park year-round, hunting voles in the snow in winter.
Rocky Mountain bighorn are easily seen at Sheep Lakes and along Trail Ridge Road.
Black bears inhabit the montane forest; berry season in late summer brings them to forest clearings.

name: Badlands NP
description: Dramatic South Dakota eroded formations where bison herds and black-footed ferret reintroduction programs thrive.

sightings.notes: Over 1,000 bison roam the Badlands grasslands; the Sage Creek Wilderness provides excellent viewing.
Pronghorn are abundant in Badlands' mixed-grass prairie and are seen on virtually every drive.
Coyotes are ubiquitous in the Badlands; their howling at dusk is one of the park's signature sounds.
Bighorn sheep inhabit the rocky pinnacles and buttes throughout Badlands NP.
White-tailed and mule deer are seen in the grassland transitions throughout the park.
Black-footed Ferrets were reintroduced to the Sage Creek Wilderness of Badlands NP in 1994; they are most visible at night near prairie dog towns, where they hunt and den.

name: Theodore Roosevelt NP
description: North Dakota badlands park with free-roaming bison, wild horses, elk, and the prairie dog towns they depend on.

sightings.notes: Free-roaming bison herds are the park's star attraction; the South Unit drive always produces sightings.
Elk herds are frequently seen in the badland woodland; the North Unit is particularly productive.
Pronghorn are common throughout the park's open grassland and river terraces.
Coyotes are active day and night throughout the park; prairie dog town edges are prime hunting ground.
White-tailed and mule deer are seen in the wooded coulees throughout both park units.
Black-footed Ferrets were reintroduced to Theodore Roosevelt NP's prairie dog towns; nocturnal spotlighting tours offer the best chance of seeing North America's most endangered mammal.

name: Shenandoah NP
description: Virginia's Blue Ridge Mountains park with high black bear density and migrating raptors along the Appalachian Trail.

sightings.notes: White-tailed deer are among the most frequently seen animals in Shenandoah; meadows along Skyline Drive are best.
One of the highest black bear densities in the eastern US; berry season in late summer is particularly active.
Coyotes have colonised the Blue Ridge and are heard howling throughout the park year-round.
Bald eagles winter along the Shenandoah River and Skyline Drive overlooks.
Unconfirmed mountain lion sightings are reported annually; the Blue Ridge is considered potential habitat.

name: Wrangell-St. Elias NP
description: America's largest national park, covering 53,000 km² of Alaskan wilderness with Dall sheep, moose, and brown bears.

sightings.notes: Grizzly bears are abundant in Wrangell-St. Elias; the Kennecott area and Nabesna Road are good spots.
Moose are the most commonly seen large mammal; wetland areas along the Nabesna Road are prime.
The Chisana caribou herd migrates through the northern section of the park in summer.
Wolf packs range across Wrangell-St. Elias; tracks are found in snow throughout the park.
Bald eagles nest throughout the park's river valleys and are seen on virtually every flight or float trip.
Wrangell-St. Elias holds large populations of Dall Sheep on the rocky alpine slopes; the park's sheer size means herds of 50+ animals are found on many of the mountain ridges.

name: Banff NP
description: Canada's oldest national park, where grizzly bears, wolves, elk, and bighorn sheep inhabit stunning Rocky Mountain terrain.

sightings.notes: Banff supports a recovering grizzly population; the Bow Valley and Icefields Parkway are hotspots.
The Bow Valley wolf pack is regularly tracked; howling stations are held for visitors in autumn.
Elk herds are seen throughout the Bow Valley year-round; the town of Banff itself hosts a resident herd.
Rocky Mountain bighorn sheep are seen on mountain slopes and road margins throughout the park.
Moose inhabit the wetlands and willow flats of the Bow Valley and Kootenay Plain.

name: Jasper NP
description: Canada's largest Rocky Mountain park, with exceptional wildlife corridors sustaining grizzly, wolf, moose, and caribou.

sightings.notes: The Icefields Parkway through Jasper is one of Canada's best routes for grizzly sightings.
Multiple wolf packs roam Jasper; they are regularly seen crossing the Icefields Parkway.
Moose are abundant in Jasper's extensive wetland habitat; Pocahontas area is reliable.
The threatened woodland caribou herd ranges across Jasper's high alpine and subalpine zones.
Large elk herds are seen in the Athabasca Valley and around Jasper townsite year-round.

name: Wood Buffalo NP
description: UNESCO World Heritage park with the world's largest free-roaming bison herd and the only wild whooping crane nesting site.

sightings.notes: Wood Buffalo NP holds the world's largest free-roaming bison herd of approximately 5,000 animals.
Barren-ground caribou migrate through the park; wood bison and barren-ground caribou coexist here.
Wood Buffalo holds one of North America's largest intact wolf populations, preying on bison.
Moose are abundant in the park's boreal forest and wetland margins.
Bald eagles breed throughout Wood Buffalo and are regularly seen along the Peace and Athabasca rivers.

Wood Buffalo NP is the only natural nesting site of the Whooping Crane — the most dramatic conservation comeback in North America; the remote Peace-Athabasca Delta provides secure nesting wetlands for this giant white bird.

name: Pacific Rim NP
description: Wild Vancouver Island coastline park with sea otters, black bears, Roosevelt elk, and grey whale offshore.

sightings.notes: Black bears are commonly seen on Pacific Rim beaches digging for invertebrates at low tide.
Roosevelt elk herds roam the Tofino area and are often seen feeding in the estuary meadows.
Bald eagles are one of the most visible wildlife species on the Pacific Rim coast year-round.
Ospreys nest along the Tofino Inlet and are seen fishing in the estuaries throughout summer.
Vancouver Island grey wolves (sea wolves) swim between islands and are occasionally seen on beaches.

name: Manu NP
description: UNESCO biosphere reserve and Amazonian world heritage site with the highest biodiversity recorded in any single park on Earth.

sightings.notes: Manu's pristine Amazon basin is one of the world's best places to see wild jaguars, especially on river beaches.
Tapirs are seen at colpa (mineral lick) sites at dawn, where up to 10 individuals may gather.
Manu's oxbow lakes host resident giant otter families; daily fishing forays are reliably observed.
Large spider monkey troops swing through Manu's canopy; they are among the most acrobatic primates in the Amazon.
Harpy eagles soar above Manu's intact canopy; a resident pair near the Cocha Salvador oxbow lake is monitored.

name: Pantanal NP
description: Heart of the world's largest tropical wetland — the best place on Earth to see wild jaguars in the open.

sightings.notes: The Pantanal is the world's best place to see wild jaguars — spotted cat tours on the Cuiabá River deliver daily sightings.
Caiman densities in the Pantanal are extraordinary — tens of thousands are seen on every road and riverbank.
Capybara herds of dozens graze every floodplain margin in the Pantanal; they are unmissable.
Giant otter families inhabit the Pantanal's rivers and lagoons; boat trips reliably encounter their fishing sessions.
Spoonbills flock in hundreds throughout the Pantanal wetlands; their pink plumage is unmissable.

The Jabiru is the Pantanal's most spectacular bird; South America's tallest flying species nests in the large trees and hunts the shallow lagoons during the dry season — groups of 10+ are commonly seen from the Transpantaneira road.

name: Iguaçu NP
description: UNESCO-listed Atlantic Forest park surrounding the world's widest waterfall, with toucans, coatis, and tapirs.

sightings.notes: Tapirs are present in the forest surrounding the falls; camera traps record regular nighttime visits.
Jaguars inhabit the wider Iguaçu forest but are rarely seen near the tourist areas of the falls.
Toucans are seen daily from the walkways around the falls, nesting in the surrounding forest.
Scarlet macaws and other parrots fly over the falls in noisy flocks throughout the day.
Capybara families graze the parkland areas around the hotel and visitor centre daily.
Jabiru Storks are occasionally seen in the wetland margins of Iguaçu NP; the dry-season drainage areas on both the Brazilian and Argentine sides attract them.

name: Serra da Canastra NP
description: Brazilian plateau park with one of the world's best concentrations of giant anteaters and maned wolves.

sightings.notes: Serra da Canastra has one of Brazil's highest giant anteater densities; road-side sightings are almost daily.
The crepuscular maned wolf stalks the campo rupestre grassland; dawn and dusk drives are most productive.
Giant otters inhabit the São Francisco River headwaters; family groups are seen from river trails.
Jaguars are present in the park's gallery forest; camera traps confirm a resident population.
Capybara groups graze the wetland margins of the São Francisco headwaters year-round.

name: Chapada dos Veadeiros NP
description: Central Brazil UNESCO park on ancient crystalline plateau with giant anteaters, giant otters, and maned wolves.

sightings.notes: Giant anteaters are frequently seen crossing the park's cerrado grasslands; morning drives are best.
Maned wolves are active at dawn and dusk in the campo limpo and cerrado of Chapada dos Veadeiros.
Giant otters inhabit the São Miguel and Preto rivers flowing through the park.
Capybara herds are seen at every river crossing and waterhole within the park.
Scarlet macaws are seen in the gallery forest and are one of the park's most colourful residents.

name: Yasuní NP
description: Amazon basin UNESCO biosphere with the most biodiverse area on Earth; jaguars and harpy eagles inhabit intact forest.

sightings.notes: Yasuní's intact Amazon forest supports jaguars; river beaches and clay licks are the best opportunity spots.
Tapirs visit clay licks throughout Yasuní and are seen crossing rivers on motorised canoe trips.
Oxbow lakes within Yasuní's core zone host resident giant otter families.
Large spider monkey troops inhabit the canopy throughout Yasuní's undisturbed forest.
Harpy eagles are present in Yasuní's tall emergent trees; a nest monitored near Añangu is well-known.

name: Galápagos NP
description: UNESCO World Heritage islands where Darwin forged evolutionary theory; unique land species found nowhere else on Earth.

sightings.notes: Giant tortoises are seen at the Charles Darwin Research Station year-round; wild populations inhabit volcanic craters.
Yellow-hued land iguanas bask by the hundreds on Fernandina, Isabela, and North Seymour islands.
Blue-footed boobies perform their high-stepping courtship dance on North Seymour and Española year-round.
Ospreys are year-round residents of the Galápagos, fishing the productive upwelling waters.

name: Torres del Paine NP
description: Patagonia's iconic granite towers park, the world's best destination to observe pumas hunting guanaco in open terrain.

sightings.notes: Guanaco herds of hundreds are one of Torres del Paine's most iconic sights on the open steppe.
Torres del Paine is the world's premier puma-watching destination; resident cats hunt guanaco daily.
Condors soar on Patagonian thermals throughout the park; Los Cuernos viewpoints are ideal.
Ospreys nest along the shore of Lake Pehoé and are regularly seen fishing in the park's glacial lakes.

Torres del Paine NP holds one of the world's most accessible Huemul populations; small groups are reliably seen in the Lago Grey and Lago del Toro sectors in the Southern Hemisphere winter when they descend from high terrain.

name: Los Glaciares NP
description: UNESCO glacier park with the Perito Moreno Glacier; pumas, guanaco, condors, and foxes inhabit the surrounding steppe.

sightings.notes: Guanaco herds roam the Patagonian steppe between the glacier viewpoints and the Fitzroy massif.
Pumas are resident in Los Glaciares and increasingly tolerant of visitors; trails near El Chaltén are productive.
Condors are seen soaring above the Perito Moreno Glacier and along the Fitzroy ridge daily.
Ospreys fish the pristine glacial lakes of Los Glaciares and are seen on most boat trips.
South Andean Huemul inhabit the remote valleys of Los Glaciares NP; they descend to lower altitudes in winter and are occasionally spotted near the El Chalten hiking area.
