name: Nahuel Huapi NP
description: Argentina's oldest park, with Andean deer, pumas, condors, and introduced invasive species threatening native wildlife.

sightings.notes: Guanaco herds inhabit the drier eastern steppe zones of Nahuel Huapi.
Pumas are present throughout Nahuel Huapi and are one of the top wildlife-watching targets.
Condors are commonly seen soaring above the Nahuel Huapi lake basin and surrounding peaks.
Introduced European red deer are now abundant throughout the park's Andean forest and meadows.
Ospreys are present on Lake Nahuel Huapi and its tributaries throughout the austral summer.

name: Corcovado NP
description: National Geographic's "most biologically intense place on Earth" — jaguar, tapir, scarlet macaw, and harpy eagle in intact Pacific rainforest.

sightings.notes: Corcovado is Costa Rica's top destination for jaguar sightings; the Sirena ranger station area is most productive.
Baird's tapir are seen on the beach at Sirena at dawn and are regularly tracked in the forest.
Flocks of scarlet macaws fly noisily over the forest canopy at Corcovado throughout the day.
Harpy eagles inhabit the tall rainforest; a nest was documented near Sirena in recent years.
Spider monkey troops are seen daily swinging through the canopy along Corcovado's forest trails.
Resplendent Quetzals occur in the highland forest above Corcovado; sightings are less frequent than in Monteverde but documented in the Cerro Pirre cloud forest.

name: Monteverde CR
description: Cloud forest reserve famed for the resplendent quetzal and three-wattled bellbird with over 100 mammal species.

sightings.notes: Baird's tapirs are present in the cloud forest; tracks are found on muddy trails in the reserve.
Spider monkeys swing through the canopy of the cloud forest reserve and are seen daily by guided groups.
Scarlet and great green macaws are seen along the forest edges of the Monteverde zone.
Ospreys migrate through the Monteverde region and winter at Pacific slope water bodies.
Several toucan species including the chestnut-mandibled toucan are common in the Monteverde forest.
Monteverde is Costa Rica's most celebrated Quetzal destination; males with 60 cm tail plumes are reliably seen in fruiting wild avocado trees during the February–May breeding season.

name: Tortuguero NP
description: Caribbean canal-laced jungle park and world's most important nesting site for green sea turtles — jaguars patrol the beaches.

sightings.notes: Jaguars patrol the Tortuguero beach at night preying on nesting sea turtles; camera traps record regular visits.
Spider monkeys inhabit the rainforest canopy throughout Tortuguero and are seen daily from canoe trips.
Scarlet macaws are seen flying over the canopy and feeding on fruit trees along the park's waterways.
Ospreys winter along Tortuguero's Caribbean coastline and canal system in large numbers.
Spoonbills wade in the shallows of Tortuguero's lagoons and are seen on canal tours year-round.

name: Tikal NP
description: UNESCO Mayan ruins rising above jungle canopy, where spider monkeys, toucans, and jaguars inhabit the surrounding forest.

sightings.notes: Jaguars inhabit the forest surrounding the ruins; dawn visits occasionally reveal them near the temples.
Howler and spider monkeys are seen daily swinging through the trees around Tikal's towering temples.
Scarlet macaws nest in the rainforest and perch on the ruins; dozens are seen on the Gran Plaza at dawn.
Several toucan species including the keel-billed toucan are commonly seen perched near Tikal's temples.
Harpy eagles occasionally soar above Tikal's canopy; a nesting pair was confirmed in the northern sector.

name: Cockscomb Basin WS
description: World's first jaguar sanctuary; the lush Maya Mountains shelter jaguars, tapirs, ocelots, and scarlet macaws.

sightings.notes: The Cockscomb Basin holds the world's highest known jaguar density; camera traps record sightings weekly.
Baird's tapirs are common in the basin and frequently captured on camera traps along river trails.
Scarlet macaws nest in the forest and are seen flying over the Cockscomb Basin throughout the year.
Spider monkey troops are frequently encountered on guided hikes in the reserve.
Giant anteaters are present in the southern Belize lowlands and occasionally enter the reserve.

name: Madidi NP
description: Bolivia's Andean-Amazonian park; one of the most biodiverse areas on Earth, spanning altitude zones from Andes to lowland Amazon.

sightings.notes: Jaguars inhabit Madidi's vast intact forest corridor; river beaches and clay licks offer the best chances.
Tapirs are seen at clay licks and river edges; Chalalan Ecolodge on Lake Chalalan is a reliable spot.
Giant otters inhabit Lake Chalalan and other oxbow lakes within Madidi's lowland zone.
Spectacled bears inhabit Madidi's high-altitude cloud forest and Andean zones above the Tuichi valley.
Harpy eagles are present in the lowland Amazon forest; nests have been documented in the Tuichi valley.

name: Tayrona NP
description: Caribbean coastal Sierra Nevada park with howler monkeys, kinkajous, white-tailed deer, and nesting sea turtles.

sightings.notes: Jaguars inhabit the Sierra Nevada foothills behind Tayrona's beaches; camera traps confirm their presence.
Ospreys winter along Tayrona's Caribbean coastline and fish in the sheltered bays.
Scarlet macaws are seen in the forest behind the beach; their calls announce their presence overhead.
Spider monkey groups are frequently seen from the forest trails linking Tayrona's beaches.
Capybara families inhabit the mangrove lagoons and freshwater streams in the park interior.

name: Los Llanos
description: Flooded savannas of the Orinoco basin — the best place in the Americas to see wildlife in open grassland.

sightings.notes: The dry season concentrates wildlife at water and produces exceptional jaguar sightings along river banks.
Los Llanos holds the world's highest capybara density; herds of hundreds are seen on every road drive.
Spectacled caiman pack the Llanos rivers and pools in extraordinary numbers throughout the year.
Giant anteaters stalk the open savanna of the Llanos; daily sightings are virtually guaranteed.
Thousands of spoonbills flock to the Llanos wetlands; their pink flocks are visible from kilometres away.
Jabiru Storks are abundant in Los Llanos during the flooding season; their black-and-red bare heads and enormous wingspan make them unmistakable as they stalk through the shallow flooded grasslands.

name: Białowieża NP
description: Europe's last primeval lowland forest, UNESCO World Heritage site protecting the continent's largest bison herd.

sightings.notes: Europe's largest land animal; guided tours track bison herds through the primeval forest year-round.
Wolves are resident in the forest; tracks in snow are found on almost every winter walk.
Lynx inhabit the ancient forest; paw prints and tree scrapes are evidence of their presence.
Large red deer stags are commonly seen in the clearings and forest edges throughout the park.

name: Doñana NP
description: UNESCO World Heritage Andalusian wetland — one of Europe's most important bird reserves and the last stronghold of the Iberian lynx.

sightings.notes: Doñana is one of the last Iberian lynx strongholds; guided tours enter the reserve for reliable sightings.
Tens of thousands of flamingos winter in Doñana's marismas wetlands — one of Europe's great wildlife spectacles.
Ospreys winter throughout the Doñana wetlands in large numbers.
Red deer stags are commonly seen on the park's open monte negro scrubland.
Iberian wolves occasionally enter the Doñana reserve from the north; sightings are rare but confirmed.
Doñana NP is the stronghold for the Spanish Imperial Eagle; the park supports the largest breeding population and the birds are reliably seen soaring over the marshes and pine forest from hides.

name: Sierra Nevada NP
description: Spain's highest national park with Iberian ibex and golden eagles among the rocky summits.

sightings.notes: Red deer stags bugle spectacularly in the highland meadows of Sierra Nevada each autumn.
Spanish ibex (Capra pyrenaica) and chamois inhabit the rocky upper slopes above 2,000 metres.
Reintroduced lynx have been recorded on the lower slopes of Sierra Nevada in recent surveys.
Ospreys winter at the Laguna de Padul reservoir on the southern flanks of the sierra.
Flamingos occasionally visit saltpan wetlands on the Granada plain at the foot of the sierra.

name: Ordesa y Monte Perdido NP
description: UNESCO-listed Pyrenean park with Pyrenean chamois, brown bears, and one of Spain's last wolf populations.

sightings.notes: Pyrenean chamois are the most frequently seen large mammal in the Ordesa canyon and upper meadows.
Pyrenean brown bears occasionally cross from the French side into the Ordesa high forest.
Red deer are regularly seen in the Añisclo canyon and forested valleys of the park.
Lynx have been recorded in the Pyrenees; the Ordesa forest is considered potential recolonisation habitat.
Ospreys pass through the Pyrenees on migration and occasionally winter near the Embalse de Mediano.

name: Picos de Europa NP
description: Northern Spain's dramatic limestone massif where wolves, brown bears, and Cantabrian chamois persist.

sightings.notes: The Cantabrian brown bear population of ~300 individuals inhabits the Picos; encounters are increasingly frequent.
Iberian wolf packs are well established in the mountains around the Picos; howling is heard at night.
Large red deer herds graze the mountain meadows; the autumn rut fills the valleys with bellowing stags.
Cantabrian chamois are easily seen on the rocky upper ridges above the treeline.
Ospreys breed near reservoirs at the foot of the Picos and are seen on migration.

name: Pyrenees NP
description: French side of the high Pyrenees, protecting brown bears reintroduced from Slovenia and chamois herds.

sightings.notes: The Slovenian-origin brown bears reintroduced to the French Pyrenees are occasionally seen in the park's core.
Pyrenean chamois are abundant above the treeline and easily seen from the high GR10 trail.
Red deer are common in the beech and fir forest zone of the French Pyrenees park.
Wolves from the Alps have been crossing into the Pyrenees in recent years; occasional sightings reported.
Ospreys pass through the Pyrenees on spring and autumn migration.

name: Vanoise NP
description: France's first national park, an Alpine sanctuary where ibex and chamois populations have recovered dramatically.

sightings.notes: Vanoise holds 2,000+ ibex — the densest population in the Alps; animals are completely fearless of walkers.
Chamois are ubiquitous on the rocky slopes of the Vanoise; herds of 20–30 are commonly encountered.
Red deer inhabit the lower forest zones of the Vanoise and are seen at dawn in valley meadows.
Wolves have returned to the French Alps via Vanoise; packs use the park as a core territory.
Ospreys nest near Vanoise's alpine reservoirs and are seen fishing from late spring.

name: Mercantour NP
description: Maritime Alps park where wolves returned naturally from Italy in the 1990s; ibex, chamois, and marmots are common.

sightings.notes: Ibex are abundant in Mercantour's rocky terrain and frequently encountered on marked trails.
Chamois are found throughout Mercantour's high rocky landscape; large herds are seen on open slopes.
Wolves recolonised Mercantour naturally from Italy in the early 1990s; multiple packs are now resident.
Red deer have repopulated the lower valleys of Mercantour and the autumn rut is impressive.
Ospreys nest in the Maritime Alps and are seen over Mercantour's high lakes in summer.

name: Camargue NR
description: Rhône delta wetland famous for its white horses, black bulls, and flocks of greater flamingos.

sightings.notes: The Camargue hosts Europe's only wild breeding flamingo colony — up to 13,000 pairs nesting at Étang de Fangassier.
Ospreys winter in the Camargue delta in significant numbers, fishing the shallow ponds and lagoons.
Spoonbills winter in the Camargue wetlands in increasing numbers; the étangs near Saintes-Maries are best.
Wolves from the Provence population occasionally move through the Camargue scrubland.
Red deer inhabit the scrubland and wooded fringes bordering the Camargue wetlands.

name: Gran Paradiso NP
description: Italy's oldest park and the only one established to save a species — the Alpine ibex — now home to 4,000 individuals.

sightings.notes: Gran Paradiso saved the ibex from extinction — 4,000 animals now roam the park completely unafraid of people.
Chamois are found throughout the park's rocky terrain; herds are easily seen on open slopes.
Red deer are found in the forested lower valleys; the Aosta Valley forests are particularly productive.
Wolves have returned to Gran Paradiso from France; a resident pack was confirmed in recent years.
Bears from Trentino's Adamello population occasionally disperse into the Gran Paradiso area.

name: Abruzzo NP
description: Apennine park that saved the Marsican brown bear — a genetically distinct subspecies of around 50 individuals.

sightings.notes: The Marsican brown bear — a unique Apennine subspecies — is reliably seen around Pescasseroli in summer.
Apennine wolves are well established in Abruzzo and are frequently detected by their tracks and howling.
Apennine chamois are abundant in the rocky terrain above the treeline and are easily observed.
Red deer are common in the park's beech forest and mountain meadows; the rut is dramatic.

Abruzzo NP is the sole refuge of the Critically Endangered Marsican Brown Bear; with only ~50 individuals, it is one of Europe's rarest animals — sightings around the park's meadows at dusk are recorded regularly by rangers.

name: Swiss NP
description: Switzerland's only national park, a strictly protected Alpine wilderness where ibex, chamois, and red deer roam freely.

sightings.notes: Ibex are the park's most iconic species; herds of 20+ are regularly seen on the rocky ridges above Champlunc.
Chamois are ubiquitous in the Swiss NP; they are the prey base supporting the park's eagle populations.
Large red deer herds inhabit the valley bottoms; they descend to graze in the open areas at dusk.
Wolves have re-established packs in the Engadin region; they are occasionally seen crossing park terrain.
Lynx are present in the Swiss NP; their paw prints are found in snow and their kills located by rangers.

name: Berchtesgaden NP
description: Bavaria's only national park, with chamois, red deer, and golden eagles among the Watzmann's limestone crags.

sightings.notes: Chamois are the most visible large mammal in Berchtesgaden; the Kehlstein plateau is particularly reliable.
A reintroduced ibex population inhabits the limestone crags above the Königssee.
Red deer are commonly seen in the Klausbachtal valley and the Wimbachtal forest.
Bears from Austria's Salzburg population occasionally cross into Berchtesgaden; the JJ bear was famous.
Wolves passing through Bavaria from eastern Europe are increasingly recorded in the Berchtesgaden region.

name: Bavarian Forest NP
description: Central Europe's first national park, a dense Bohemian forest with lynx, wolves, and abundant deer.

sightings.notes: Bears are occasionally sighted crossing the Czech-German border near the Šumava-Bavarian Forest complex.
Lynx were reintroduced to the Bavarian Forest in the 1970s; a self-sustaining population is now established.
Wolves have naturally recolonised the Bavarian Forest from the east; packs are now confirmed.
Red deer are abundant throughout the park's spruce and beech forest; herds of 50+ are seen at dusk.
Bison were reintroduced to the Bavarian-Bohemian forest; herds of 15+ are regularly seen from hides.
Bavarian Forest NP protects a significant Capercaillie population; males perform their extraordinary bubbling song displays in the old-growth spruce forest at dawn in spring.

name: Cairngorms NP
description: Britain's largest national park, with the UK's only free-ranging reindeer herd, ospreys, and recovering red squirrel populations.

sightings.notes: Red deer are the largest wild land mammals in the UK and are abundant on the open Cairngorm plateau.
Ospreys bred at Loch Garten since the 1950s; this became the UK's most celebrated wildlife recovery story.

The Cairngorms hold Scotland's last Capercaillie population; the spring lek can be witnessed in the ancient Caledonian pinewoods around Loch Garten, though disturbance rules require keeping to marked paths.

name: Snowdonia NP
description: Welsh highland park with red kites, polecats, and one of Wales' last populations of red squirrels.

sightings.notes: A small red deer herd inhabits the Rhinog mountains of southern Snowdonia.
Ospreys breed at Cors Dyfi Nature Reserve on Snowdonia's southern edge; a dedicated visitor centre operates.

name: Plitvice Lakes NP
description: UNESCO cascading lake system in Croatia, where bears, wolves, and lynx still inhabit the surrounding Dinaric forest.

sightings.notes: Bears from the Dinaric population are regularly recorded in the forests surrounding Plitvice.
Wolf packs inhabit the Dinaric forest surrounding the lakes; howling is heard from the park at night.
Lynx are resident in the Dinaric forest complex; paw prints are found on the park's forest trails.
Red deer herds are commonly seen in the forest clearings and meadows surrounding the lakes.
Chamois inhabit the rocky karst landscape in the higher terrain of the Mala Kapela mountains.

name: Triglav NP
description: Slovenia's only national park, centred on the Julian Alps, with chamois, brown bears, and eurasian lynx.

sightings.notes: Chamois are abundant on the rocky slopes of the Julian Alps and are seen on every mountain walk.
Slovenia has one of central Europe's densest brown bear populations; Triglav is part of their range.
Dinaric lynx inhabit the forest zones of Triglav; they are rarely seen but present.
Ibex have been reintroduced to the Julian Alps; herds are seen on rocky ridges above the treeline.
Wolves inhabit the Dinaric forest zone of Triglav; tracks in the snow confirm their regular presence.

name: Retezat NP
description: Carpathian park with Europe's largest brown bear population alongside wolves, lynx, and chamois.

sightings.notes: Romania holds Europe's largest brown bear population; Retezat bears are increasingly seen near dawn.
Wolf packs inhabit the Retezat Mountains; their howling is heard regularly from mountain huts.
Carpathian lynx are present throughout Retezat; camera traps show regular activity.
Large red deer stags are commonly seen in Retezat's mountain meadows and forest edges.
Chamois are abundant above the treeline in Retezat's rocky alpine terrain.

name: Bieszczady NP
description: Poland's easternmost park on the Ukrainian border — a stronghold for wolves, bears, Eurasian lynx, and European bison.

sightings.notes: A significant bison herd roams the Bieszczady; guided tours from Ustrzyki Górne regularly encounter them.
One of Poland's most important wolf strongholds; packs are tracked by GPS collars throughout the park.
Bears are regularly seen in the upper meadows of the Bieszczady in the summer months.
Lynx are resident in the Bieszczady forest; camera trap footage is regularly captured by researchers.
One of Poland's densest red deer populations inhabits the Bieszczady mountains.

name: Rondane NP
description: Norway's oldest national park, with a pristine reindeer population and wolverines among the high plateau.

sightings.notes: Wild mountain reindeer herds of hundreds roam the Rondane plateau — the largest wild reindeer in Norway.
Wolverines inhabit the Rondane high plateau; tracks in the snow are found throughout winter.
Wolves occasionally move through the Rondane corridor following reindeer migrations.
Moose inhabit the birch forest and valley bottoms of the Rondane foothills.
Ospreys breed along the rivers and lakes of the Rondane foothills each summer.
Rondane NP protects Norway's most important wild reindeer population; herds of hundreds migrate across the high plateaux in summer, guided by ancient routes.

name: Sarek NP
description: One of Europe's last true wildernesses, with brown bears, wolverines, lynx, and golden eagles in a roadless Swedish alpine expanse.

sightings.notes: Bears are regularly seen in Sarek's remote river valleys; the park has a healthy breeding population.
Wolverines are resident in Sarek; their distinctive tracks in the snow are found on almost every winter expedition.
Lynx are present in Sarek's boreal forest zones; encounters are rare but sightings are recorded annually.
Moose are the most commonly seen large mammal in Sarek's river valleys and wetlands.
Wild Scandinavian reindeer herds migrate across Sarek's open fjäll throughout the year.
Wild reindeer migrate through Sarek's high tundra in summer; both wild and semi-domesticated herds cross this remote Swedish wilderness.
Capercaillie inhabit the boreal forest of Sarek's lower valleys; the Swedish population is larger and more stable than in Britain.

name: Peneda-Gerês NP
description: Portugal's only national park, with Garrano horses, Iberian wolves, and golden eagles in ancient granite highlands.

sightings.notes: The Iberian wolf pack of Peneda-Gerês is Portugal's most reliably documented wolf population.
Red deer are common throughout the park's granite uplands and oak-pine woodland.
Ospreys winter at the Caniçada and Vilarinho das Furnas reservoirs within the park.

name: Kakadu NP
description: UNESCO World Heritage Northern Territory park — one of the world's great wildlife destinations with saltwater crocodiles, 280+ bird species, and abundant macropods.

sightings.notes: Kakadu's Yellow Water billabong boat cruises reveal dozens of large saltwater crocodiles basking daily.
Ospreys are year-round residents of Kakadu's river and wetland systems.
White-bellied sea eagles are one of the most visible large birds at Yellow Water and East Alligator River.
Antilopine wallaroos and agile wallabies are abundant throughout Kakadu's open forest.
Emus are seen throughout Kakadu's open woodland, particularly in the dry season.

name: Daintree NP
description: World's oldest continuously surviving tropical rainforest, home to southern cassowary, tree kangaroos, and Bennett's tree kangaroo.

sightings.notes: The cassowary is the icon of the Daintree; encounters on forest roads and tracks are regular.
Crocodiles inhabit every waterway in the Daintree; river cruises encounter them on every trip.
Ospreys are year-round residents fishing the Daintree River and surrounding waterways.
White-bellied sea eagles nest along the Daintree coast and are seen on every river cruise.
Agile wallabies and tree kangaroos inhabit Daintree's rainforest; Lumholtz's tree-kangaroo is present.

name: Blue Mountains NP
description: UNESCO escarpment park west of Sydney with swamp wallabies, eastern grey kangaroos, and nesting platypus.

sightings.notes: Koalas are present in the Blue Mountains' eucalyptus forest; Wollemi is the most productive zone.
Eastern grey kangaroos graze the open meadows and cliff-top grasslands throughout the park.
Ospreys fish the Hawkesbury River and the broader water systems of the Blue Mountains foothills.
Sea eagles are regularly seen soaring above the grand valleys of the Blue Mountains.
Emus are occasionally seen in the drier woodland margins of the western Blue Mountains.
Common Wombats are regularly encountered in the bush around Blue Mountains villages and campsites; they emerge at dusk to graze on grass and clover in the cleared areas near the escarpment.

name: Uluru-Kata Tjuta NP
description: Sacred Red Centre UNESCO landscape with red kangaroos, dingoes, perenties, and wedge-tailed eagles.

sightings.notes: Red kangaroos are the most abundant large mammal around Uluru; they graze the spinifex plains at dusk.
Emus are frequently seen strutting across the open landscape around the base of Uluru.

name: Flinders Ranges NP
description: Ancient South Australian mountain range home to yellow-footed rock wallabies, emus, and wedge-tailed eagles.

sightings.notes: Red and euro kangaroos are abundant throughout the Flinders Ranges and easily seen at dawn and dusk.
Emus are a common sight throughout the Flinders Ranges open terrain.
Koalas inhabit the river red gum and blue gum woodland of the park's lower elevation creek lines.

name: Purnululu NP
description: Kimberley's beehive-striped Bungle Bungle range, sheltering black wallaroos, freshwater crocodiles, and carpet pythons.

sightings.notes: Wallaroos and agile wallabies are commonly seen in the gorges of the Bungle Bungle Range.
Freshwater and saltwater crocodiles inhabit the permanent waterways near the Bungle Bungle Range.
Emus are seen in the open savanna surrounding the Bungle Bungle Range.
Ospreys visit the Ord River waterways adjacent to Purnululu.
Large raptors including sea eagles are occasionally seen above the Bungle Bungle gorges.

name: Kosciuszko NP
description: Australia's highest alpine park with threatened mountain pygmy possums, corroboree frogs, and broad-toothed rats.

sightings.notes: Eastern grey kangaroos are ubiquitous in the lower Kosciuszko valleys and around Jindabyne.
Koalas inhabit the lower-elevation eucalyptus forest on the foothills of the Snowy Mountains.
Emus are seen in the open grasslands on the western slopes of the Kosciuszko range.
Ospreys fish the Snowy River and Tantangara Reservoir within the park.
White-bellied sea eagles are regularly seen hunting above Lake Eucumbene and the Snowy River.

Common Wombats are abundant in Kosciuszko NP and one of its most iconic residents; they are frequently seen grazing the subalpine meadows at dawn and dusk, and their cubic dung piles line every trail.

name: Fiordland NP (Land)
description: UNESCO Fiordland wilderness where kiwi, moose (rare), kea, and Fiordland crested penguins inhabit ancient beech forest.

sightings.notes: Fiordland tokoeka (southern brown kiwi) inhabit the park's forest; nocturnal encounters on bush trails are possible.
Kea are encountered at the Milford Sound car park and Homer Tunnel almost daily — notoriously cheeky.
Introduced red deer are abundant in Fiordland's forest and are frequently seen on valley walks.
Introduced chamois inhabit the rocky ridges of the Fiordland mountains and are seen by trampers.
Introduced moose are thought to persist in tiny numbers in remote Fiordland; camera trap evidence exists.

The southern Fiordland coastline holds Yellow-eyed Penguin nesting colonies; guided tours to remote beaches access breeding sites where penguins return from sea at dusk — one of the world's rarest penguin species.
Hector's Dolphins inhabit the shallow coastal waters around Fiordland; the world's smallest and rarest dolphin is seen in the sheltered sounds and along the South Island coastline on boat trips.

name: Abel Tasman NP
description: Nelson coastal park with weka, kaka, and Nelson falcon among restored forest and granite headlands.

sightings.notes: Kiwi are present in the coastal bush of Abel Tasman; nocturnal encounters on camping trips are possible.
Kea occasionally descend from the Takaka Hill to the Abel Tasman coastal track.
Red deer inhabit the forest behind the Abel Tasman coast and are seen on the inland tracks.
Chamois inhabit the inland Takaka Hill marble and limestone country above the park.
Ospreys occasionally visit the sheltered bays of Abel Tasman during Austral summer.
Yellow-eyed Penguins are occasionally seen around the rocky headlands and beaches at the southern end of Abel Tasman NP during the breeding season.
Hector's Dolphins are commonly encountered on sea-kayaking and boat trips around Abel Tasman NP's coast; the species is endemic to New Zealand and regularly seen in small groups in the bay.

name: Tongariro NP
description: New Zealand's oldest park and first UNESCO dual World Heritage site, with kiwi, endangered North Island kaka, and whio in volcanic terrain.

sightings.notes: North Island brown kiwi inhabit the lower forest zones of Tongariro; nocturnal expeditions find them.
Kea are regularly encountered near the Whakapapa ski village and along the Alpine Crossing.
Introduced red deer are abundant in the forest and tussock grassland surrounding the volcanoes.
Chamois inhabit the higher volcanic terrain and surrounding ranges of Tongariro NP.
Ospreys occasionally visit the lakes of the Tongariro area in the austral summer months.

name: Aoraki/Mount Cook NP
description: New Zealand's highest peaks, home to kea, chamois (introduced), and Himalayan tahr among alpine glaciers.

sightings.notes: Kea are ubiquitous at the Mount Cook Village — they investigate every parked car and jacket pocket.
Chamois are abundant on the rocky ridges and moraines of the Aoraki massif.
Introduced red deer are found in the valley bottoms and forest margins below the glaciers.
Kiwi inhabit the lower Hooker and Tasman valley forest at night.
Ospreys occasionally fish Lake Pukaki and Lake Tekapo adjacent to the Aoraki park boundary.

name: Westland NP
description: South Island glacier and rainforest park where kiwi and kea inhabit dense podocarp forest beside Franz Josef Glacier.

sightings.notes: Western brown kiwi are present in the dense podocarp forest; guided nocturnal tours find them regularly.
Kea congregate at Franz Josef and Fox Glacier car parks and are encountered on every visit.
Red deer are abundant throughout the Westland forest; helicopter hunting keeps numbers partially controlled.
Chamois inhabit the upper alpine zones of the Southern Alps above the Franz Josef and Fox glaciers.
Ospreys occasionally visit the Westland rivers and lagoons during the austral summer.

name: Kahurangi NP
description: New Zealand's second-largest park, the heartland of kiwi recovery in the Nelson/Marlborough region.

sightings.notes: Kahurangi is a key stronghold for the great spotted kiwi (roroa) — New Zealand's largest and rarest kiwi.
Kea are frequently encountered along the Heaphy Track and other alpine trails in Kahurangi.
Red deer are abundant in Kahurangi's beech forest and tussock grassland.
Chamois inhabit the marble karst and alpine terrain of the Kahurangi high country.
Ospreys occasionally fish the Karamea River and other waterways flowing through Kahurangi.

name: Niokolo-Koba NP
description: UNESCO World Heritage site and West Africa's most important savanna park, protecting one of the region's last elephant populations and critically rare West African lions.

sightings.notes: A small and declining elephant population remains in Niokolo-Koba; sightings require luck and patience.
West African lions cling on in Niokolo-Koba; this subspecies is critically endangered with very few individuals remaining.
Leopards are the most reliably seen large predator in Niokolo-Koba, present throughout the woodland.
Hippos are common in the Gambia River and its tributaries flowing through the park.
Warthogs are abundant and easily seen across all of Niokolo-Koba's savanna habitats.
West African chimpanzees are present in the gallery forest of Niokolo-Koba; one of their last strongholds in Senegal.
The North African ostrich subspecies is present in the open savanna of Niokolo-Koba; one of the last populations in West Africa.
Secretary birds stride through the open grasslands of Niokolo-Koba hunting snakes and large insects.
Spotted hyenas are common in Niokolo-Koba and frequently heard calling at night near camps.
Niokolo-Koba holds one of the last wild populations of the Western Derby eland — a critically endangered giant antelope; fewer than 200 survive.

name: Gorongosa NP
description: Mozambique's flagship park devastated by civil war and now one of Africa's most celebrated wildlife recovery stories; elephants, hippos, lions, and wild dogs are all rebounding.

sightings.notes: The elephant population has recovered dramatically; herds of 50 or more are now frequently seen on the floodplain.
Lion numbers have rebounded strongly; the open Lake Urema floodplain offers excellent sightings.
Hippos are abundant in Lake Urema and the Pungue River, central to the park's ecosystem recovery.
Large Nile crocodiles are resident in Lake Urema and all major watercourses in the park.
Wild dogs were reintroduced to Gorongosa from South Africa; the pack is establishing and sightings are increasing.
Plains zebra populations have recovered dramatically; large herds now roam the Urema floodplain.
Blue wildebeest have rebounded strongly in Gorongosa; herds of hundreds are seen on the open grassland.
Impala are among the most abundant animals in Gorongosa; they are everywhere in the woodland and grassland.
Warthogs are abundant throughout Gorongosa; families are seen daily in every habitat.
Leopards are relatively common in Gorongosa; night drives regularly encounter them in the riverine forest.
Secretary birds stalk through the open grasslands of Gorongosa hunting snakes and lizards.
Sable antelope are recovering strongly in Gorongosa; the Mount Gorongosa woodland holds a growing population of this striking antelope.
Common elands are present in the woodland zones of Gorongosa, particularly on the slopes of Mount Gorongosa.

name: Ifrane NP
description: Middle Atlas cedar forest park holding roughly a quarter of the world's Barbary macaques, alongside Barbary stag, Barbary sheep, and elusive striped hyena.

sightings.notes: Ifrane holds roughly a quarter of the world's Barbary macaque population, easily seen in the cedar forest around Azrou.
Barbary stag, North Africa's only native deer, inhabits the park's cedar and oak forest.
Barbary sheep (locally called arruis) range across Ifrane's rockier terrain.
Striped hyena are among Ifrane's more elusive nocturnal predators.
Caracal and serval are both recorded in Ifrane's cedar forest, though rarely seen.

name: Tassili n'Ajjer NP
description: Vast Saharan sandstone plateau and UNESCO World Heritage site famed for prehistoric rock art; among the last refuges anywhere of the critically endangered Saharan cheetah.

sightings.notes: Tassili n'Ajjer is one of the last confirmed refuges of the critically endangered Saharan cheetah, camera-trapped periodically since 2015.
Dorcas gazelle range across the plateau's rocky wadis and plains, though populations have declined under poaching pressure.
Striped hyena are among the park's recorded Saharan predators.
Caracal inhabit Tassili's rockier plateau terrain.
Barbary sheep are among the flagship species monitored across the plateau's sandstone formations.

name: Ichkeul NP
description: Tunisia's only UNESCO natural World Heritage site, a shallow lake and marsh that shelters up to 300,000 wintering ducks, geese, and coots — one of the Mediterranean's great wetland spectacles.

sightings.notes: Greater flamingos gather on Lake Ichkeul in spring, part of the exceptional density of wintering and passage waterfowl.
Ichkeul is one of three globally important sites for the endangered white-headed duck.
Marbled duck are among the threatened waterfowl species wintering on the lake.
Ferruginous duck winter on Ichkeul in large numbers alongside pochard and tufted duck.
Wild boar inhabit the marshland and scrub surrounding the lake.

name: Saint Katherine Protectorate
description: Egypt's largest protected area, high in the granite mountains of southern Sinai around Mount Sinai; a stronghold for the endangered Nubian ibex at the northern edge of its range.

sightings.notes: Saint Katherine holds one of Egypt's most important Nubian ibex populations, though numbers are estimated at only 200-250 individuals.
Rock hyrax colonies are common among the granite boulders of the high Sinai massif.
Striped hyena range through the protectorate's mountain wadis at night.
Dorcas gazelle inhabit the lower wadis and plains surrounding the high peaks.
Egyptian vultures soar over the protectorate's granite peaks, among more than 46 reptile and bird species recorded here.

name: Kahuzi-Biega NP
description: Montane and lowland forest park named for its two dormant volcanoes, holding one of the last strongholds of the critically endangered Grauer's (eastern lowland) gorilla, the largest gorilla subspecies.

sightings.notes: Kahuzi-Biega protects one of the last significant Grauer's gorilla populations, a subspecies found nowhere outside eastern DR Congo.
Chimpanzees share Kahuzi-Biega's forest with the park's gorillas.
Forest elephants inhabit the park's lowland sector, though numbers have declined sharply from poaching.
Golden monkey range through the park's bamboo zones in the Albertine Rift highlands.
Leopards range through Kahuzi-Biega's forest, rarely seen beneath the dense canopy.

name: Odzala-Kokoua NP
description: One of Africa's oldest national parks, in the heart of the Congo Basin; holds the largest western lowland gorilla population of any park on Earth alongside thousands of forest elephants.

sightings.notes: Odzala-Kokoua holds close to 7,600 western lowland gorillas, the largest population recorded in any national park in Africa.
Over 7,300 forest elephants inhabit Odzala-Kokoua, around 7% of the species' entire remaining population.
Chimpanzees share Odzala's forest with its gorillas, occasionally seen at the park's bais.
Bongo, the largest forest antelope, are occasionally glimpsed at Odzala's forest clearings.
Sitatunga wade through Odzala's swamp forest, their splayed hooves adapted to soft ground.

name: Dzanga-Sangha
description: Rainforest reserve around Dzanga Bai, "the elephant village", where up to 200 forest elephants gather daily at a forest clearing alongside one of the world's densest western lowland gorilla populations.

sightings.notes: Up to 200 forest elephants gather daily at Dzanga Bai, "the elephant village", one of the most reliable elephant spectacles in Central Africa.
Dzanga-Sangha holds one of the highest gorilla densities anywhere, with roughly 2,000 individuals; the Bai Hokou group is habituated for tracking.
Chimpanzees inhabit Dzanga-Sangha's forest alongside its gorillas and elephants.
Bongo are regularly seen at Dzanga Bai alongside the forest elephants.
Sitatunga inhabit the swampy forest margins around the reserve's rivers.

name: Waza NP
description: UNESCO Biosphere Reserve floodplain and savanna park in Cameroon's far north, a drought-prone Sahelian refuge for lion, elephant, giraffe, and a wealth of antelope species.

sightings.notes: Waza's lion population has dwindled to an estimated 14-21 individuals, making sightings increasingly rare.
Elephants gather at Waza's remaining waterholes as the surrounding Sahel dries out through the dry season.
Giraffe browse Waza's acacia woodland, a regionally important population for the species in the Sahel.
Roan antelope range across Waza's savanna grassland.
Kob are among the most commonly seen antelope on Waza's floodplain.

name: Taï NP
description: UNESCO World Heritage rainforest, one of the last major blocks of untouched primary forest in West Africa; famous for tool-using chimpanzees and one of the last viable pygmy hippopotamus populations.

sightings.notes: Taï's Western chimpanzees are internationally known for using stone tools to crack nuts and hunting colobus monkeys cooperatively.
Taï holds one of the last viable pygmy hippopotamus populations, an estimated 500 individuals in the park's swamps and rivers.
Leopards are among five threatened mammal species recorded in Taï's primary rainforest.
Olive colobus, uniquely among colobus monkeys, carry their infants in their mouths through Taï's forest canopy.
Taï is one of the last strongholds of the extremely rare Jentink's duiker.

name: Mole NP
description: Ghana's largest and oldest protected area, a Guinea savanna woodland holding around 600 elephants — the most of any site in West Africa — alongside kob, hartebeest, and roan antelope.

sightings.notes: Mole holds around 600 elephants, the largest population anywhere in West Africa, commonly seen near lodges and waterholes.
Kob are among the most abundant antelope in Mole's Guinea savanna woodland.
Western hartebeest graze Mole's open savanna alongside kob and waterbuck.
Roan antelope are among the species of special interest recorded in Mole.
Defassa waterbuck are regularly seen near Mole's rivers and waterholes.

name: Aïr and Ténéré NR
description: Africa's second-largest nature reserve, spanning the Aïr Mountains and Ténéré desert; a last refuge for the critically endangered dama gazelle alongside dorcas gazelle and Barbary sheep.

sightings.notes: Aïr and Ténéré is among the last refuges of the critically endangered dama gazelle, reduced to a few hundred individuals across the Sahara.
Dorcas gazelle persist across the reserve's desert plains, confirmed present in recent UNESCO monitoring missions.
Barbary sheep inhabit the rocky slopes of the Aïr Mountains within the reserve.
Nubian bustard numbers have declined sharply in the reserve, though the species is still occasionally recorded.
Fennec fox, the world's smallest canid, inhabits the sandy desert margins of the reserve.

name: Skeleton Coast NP
description: Fog-shrouded Namibian coastline named for shipwrecks and whale bones; desert-adapted lions here have relearned how to hunt Cape fur seals along beaches lined with vast seal colonies.

sightings.notes: Desert-adapted lions have relearned how to hunt Cape fur seals and coastal birds on the Skeleton Coast after a 35-year absence from this behaviour.
Vast Cape fur seal colonies line the Skeleton Coast, easily seen during the breeding season at sites like Cape Cross.
Brown hyena patrol the coastline at night, scavenging from seal colonies and beach-cast carrion.
Black-backed jackal are commonly seen scavenging around the Skeleton Coast's seal colonies.
Desert-adapted elephants occasionally range into the park's river courses in search of water and vegetation.

name: Namib-Naukluft NP
description: One of Africa's largest conservation areas, protecting the world's oldest desert; oryx, springbok, and the endemic Hartmann's mountain zebra roam the towering red dunes of Sossusvlei.

sightings.notes: Gemsbok (oryx) are the defining large mammal of Namib-Naukluft, well adapted to the desert's extreme heat and scarce water.
Springbok herds cross the desert plains, performing their characteristic bounding "pronking" display.
Hartmann's mountain zebra, endemic to Namibia and southwestern Angola, inhabit the park's escarpment terrain.
A small population of desert-adapted elephants ranges along the park's ephemeral riverbeds.
Giraffe browse the acacia trees lining the park's dry riverbeds.

name: Central Kalahari GR
description: World's second-largest game reserve, a vast expanse of Kalahari sand and dry pans famed for its black-maned lions and thriving cheetah and brown hyena populations.

sightings.notes: The black-maned Kalahari lion, the largest lion variant in Africa, is a signature sight of the Central Kalahari.
Cheetah hunt springbok and other antelope across the reserve's open pans.
The Central Kalahari holds one of southern Africa's most concentrated brown hyena populations.
African wild dogs range across the reserve's vast, thinly-populated terrain.
Springbok are the most abundant antelope species across the reserve's pans and dry valleys.

name: Iona NP
description: Angola's oldest and largest protected area, at the northern edge of the Namib Desert; remnant populations of oryx, springbok, cheetah, and leopard are recovering after decades of civil war.

sightings.notes: Oryx (gemsbok) populations are recovering well in Iona after decades of civil-war-era poaching.
Springbok herds range across Iona's desert plains at the northern edge of the Namib.
A remnant cheetah population survives in Iona, part of ongoing restoration efforts by African Parks.
Leopards persist in Iona's rugged terrain in small numbers.
Brown hyena scavenge across Iona's arid coastal plains.

name: Boma NP
description: South Sudan's largest national park, rivalling Kruger in scale; anchor of the Boma-Badingilo landscape where an aerial survey confirmed the world's largest land mammal migration.

sightings.notes: Boma anchors one end of a migration of roughly six million antelope, the largest confirmed land mammal migration on Earth.
Vast herds of tiang migrate across the Boma landscape alongside white-eared kob.
Nile lechwe, found only in South Sudan and Ethiopia's Sudd-Gambella wetlands, inhabit Boma's floodplain margins.
Elephants are among the large mammals of global conservation importance recorded across the Boma landscape.
Giraffe range across Boma's savanna and floodplain edges.

name: Badingilo NP
description: Floodplain park forming the southern half of the Boma-Badingilo migratory landscape, where millions of white-eared kob, tiang, and Mongalla gazelle move in one of the world's great, and least-known, wildlife spectacles.

sightings.notes: Mongalla gazelle, found only in South Sudan, form part of the vast migratory herds crossing Badingilo's floodplains.
Badingilo forms the southern half of the world's largest land mammal migration, with white-eared kob moving through in their millions.
Tiang migrate through Badingilo alongside kob and Mongalla gazelle.
Bohor reedbuck are part of the great antelope migration moving across Badingilo's floodplain.
Lions follow the migratory herds across the Boma-Badingilo landscape.

name: Way Kambas NP
description: Southern Sumatra's most accessible wildlife park and a key stronghold for Sumatran tigers, Asian elephants, Malayan tapirs, and sun bears in lowland rainforest.

sightings.notes: Way Kambas holds a critical Sumatran tiger population; camera traps confirm their presence but direct sightings are rare.
Way Kambas is one of Sumatra's most important elephant reserves; the Elephant Conservation Centre offers close encounters.
Tapirs are regularly photographed by camera traps and occasionally seen on night drives in Way Kambas.
Sun bears are present throughout the lowland forest of Way Kambas; tracks and signs are commonly encountered.
Saltwater crocodiles inhabit the coastal rivers and estuaries on the eastern edge of Way Kambas.
Way Kambas holds a critically important captive breeding programme for the Sumatran rhino; wild individuals are extremely rarely encountered but camera-trapped.
Banteng are present in the forest clearings of Way Kambas; a small but important population for this endangered species.

name: Kerinci Seblat NP
description: Southeast Asia's largest national park and UNESCO Heritage site, spanning the Barisan Mountains with some of the highest remaining tiger densities in Sumatra.

sightings.notes: Kerinci Seblat holds the largest tiger population in Sumatra; sightings are rare but camera traps confirm a healthy population.
Sumatran elephants inhabit the lowland sections of the park; conflict zones at forest margins offer sighting opportunities.
Tapirs are present throughout the park's lowland and hill forest; nocturnal and difficult to observe.
Sun bears are widespread in Kerinci Seblat's forest; fresh claw marks on trees are a common sign.
Ospreys are migratory visitors to Kerinci Seblat, fishing the rivers and lakes in the park's highland zone.
Sambar deer are abundant throughout Kerinci Seblat's forest; they are the primary prey of Sumatran tigers.
A small population of Sumatran rhinos persists in the remote interior of Kerinci Seblat; camera trap evidence confirms their presence but encounters are extremely rare.

name: Ujung Kulon NP
description: UNESCO World Heritage site at Java's western tip; last refuge of the Javan rhinoceros, with pristine lowland forest, saltwater crocodiles, and Malayan tapirs.

sightings.notes: Saltwater crocodiles are resident in the coastal rivers and mangrove estuaries of Ujung Kulon Peninsula.
Ospreys are commonly seen fishing the coastal waters and rivers around Ujung Kulon.
Sea eagles are resident along the coastline of Ujung Kulon and patrol the shallow bays.
A tiny relict Java elephant population is possible though the Javan subspecies is functionally extinct; domestic-origin animals may be present.
Malayan tapirs are resident in Ujung Kulon's lowland rainforest; best detected by camera traps and footprints in mud.
Ujung Kulon is the ONLY place on Earth where the Javan rhino still exists; approximately 70 survive here — camera traps are the primary means of recording them.
Banteng wild cattle are abundant in Ujung Kulon — one of the few places in Indonesia where significant numbers remain; seen in open forest clearings.

name: Cat Tien NP
description: Vietnam's most visited national park in the southern lowlands, with Asian elephants, gaur, sambar deer, and sun bears in one of the country's largest remaining tropical forest blocks.

sightings.notes: A small but established elephant population inhabits Cat Tien; sightings most likely along the forest edge at dawn.
Gaur herds regularly emerge from the forest to graze the open areas of Cat Tien, especially in the dry season.
Sambar deer are commonly seen at dusk and dawn along the forest trails and open clearings.
Sun bears are present in Cat Tien; the Bear Rescue Centre holds confiscated individuals and encounters in the wild are rare.
Ospreys are migratory visitors to Cat Tien, fishing the Dong Nai River and oxbow lakes through the dry season.
White-bellied sea eagles are seen fishing the Dong Nai reservoir and the larger rivers within Cat Tien.

name: Manas NP
description: UNESCO World Heritage park on the Bhutan border; one of India's most biodiverse parks with one-horned rhinos, tigers, Asian elephants, and gaur in riverine grassland.

sightings.notes: Tigers were recovering in Manas after severe poaching; the population is rebuilding but sightings remain rare.
Rhinos were reintroduced to Manas from Pobitora and Kaziranga; the population is now well established in the grasslands.
Elephant herds are abundant in Manas; a major elephant migration corridor crosses the park from Bhutan.
Gaur are present in the sal forest zones of Manas and occasionally seen in the grassland margins.
Sambar deer are abundant in the mixed forest and grassland of Manas.
Ospreys are common winter visitors along the Manas River and Beki River which flow through the park.
Manas NP is the last stronghold of the world's smallest pig; reintroduced here after captive breeding, a tiny population survives in the tall grass terai.
The golden langur is endemic to a tiny sliver of Assam and Bhutan; Manas NP supports one of the best wild populations, seen in the riverside trees.
The eastern swamp deer (barasingha) is present in the grassland and floodplain of Manas NP; an Indian endemic recovering with conservation efforts.
Leopards inhabit Manas's grassland-forest mosaic alongside tigers and the park's rarer endemic species.

name: Pench NP
description: Madhya Pradesh tiger reserve straddling the Pench River, widely considered the inspiration for Kipling's Jungle Book, with reliable tiger and gaur sightings in teak forest.

sightings.notes: Pench has a well-habituated tiger population and some of Madhya Pradesh's best tiger sighting rates in the dry season.
Gaur herds graze the open teak and mixed forest of Pench; the park holds a significant population.
Sambar are the dominant large deer in Pench and primary prey for the park's tigers.
Ospreys are common winter visitors, fishing the Pench reservoir and the river flowing through the park.
Elephants occasionally move through Pench from adjacent forest; sightings are infrequent.
White-bellied sea eagles are occasional winter visitors to the Pench Reservoir and the Pench River.
The hard-ground barasingha subspecies, endemic to India, occurs in the grassland areas of Pench NP.
Pench's teak forests support a resident leopard population, most active at dawn and dusk.

name: Khao Sok NP
description: Ancient Gondwana-era rainforest on the Malay Peninsula older than the Amazon, with the Cheow Lan reservoir rising from jungle; tapirs, gaur, Asian elephants, and sun bears inhabit the park.

sightings.notes: Tapirs are present throughout Khao Sok's ancient rainforest; night boat trips on Cheow Lan reservoir offer sighting opportunities.
Gaur herds are seen regularly in Khao Sok's forest clearings, particularly near the reservoir edges.
A resident elephant population uses the park; they are sometimes encountered on trails or crossing the reservoir.
Sun bears inhabit Khao Sok's forest; claw marks on trees are frequently seen but direct encounters are uncommon.
Sambar deer are commonly seen grazing at the forest edge and on reservoir banks in Khao Sok.
White-bellied sea eagles are resident at the Cheow Lan reservoir; pairs perch on the karst islands and soar above the lake.
Ospreys are common winter visitors to Cheow Lan reservoir, fishing the calm waters of the lake.

name: Langtang NP
description: Nepal's closest Himalayan park to Kathmandu, where snow leopards patrol high ridges and red pandas inhabit bamboo-rhododendron forest in the Langtang and Gosaikund valleys.

sightings.notes: Snow leopards inhabit the high ridges above 3,500 m in Langtang; camera traps confirm a resident population.
Red pandas inhabit the bamboo and rhododendron forest between 2,500 and 4,000 m in Langtang valley.
Sambar deer inhabit the lower forest zones of Langtang below 3,000 m.
Himalayan grey wolves are present in the upper valleys of Langtang above 4,000 m.
Ospreys are occasional visitors to the rivers and lakes of Langtang during migration and the warmer months.

name: Grand Teton NP
description: The Teton Range rises sharply above the Snake River plain; one of the best parks in North America for grizzly bears, moose, gray wolves, elk, and bald eagles in a single day.

sightings.notes: Grizzlies from the Greater Yellowstone population regularly use Grand Teton; Pilgrim Creek and Willow Flats are hotspots.
Moose are abundant in Grand Teton; Oxbow Bend and the willow thickets along the Snake River are prime spots year-round.
Large elk herds winter in the National Elk Refuge adjacent to the park and migrate through in autumn.
Wolves from Yellowstone packs range into Grand Teton; sightings are less frequent than in Yellowstone but possible.
Bald eagles are year-round residents along the Snake River and Jackson Lake, one of the park's signature raptors.
Black bears are commonly encountered in the forests and berry patches of Grand Teton; sometimes seen alongside grizzlies.
One of the longest land migrations in the Western Hemisphere passes through Grand Teton; pronghorn herds are seen on the sagebrush flats.
Ospreys nest on the Snake River and Oxbow Bend; they are among the most commonly seen raptors in Grand Teton.
Coyotes are abundant in Grand Teton; they are frequently seen trotting through the sagebrush and open parkland.
Bighorn sheep inhabit the rocky terrain above the treeline; rams are seen on the Cathedral Group peaks in summer.
Trumpeter swans winter on the spring-fed sections of the Snake River near Jackson Hole; a remarkable conservation comeback story.

name: Acadia NP
description: Maine's rugged Atlantic coastal park on Mount Desert Island with black bears, moose, nesting bald eagles, and ospreys fishing the rocky coves — the USA's most visited park east of the Mississippi.

sightings.notes: Black bears are common on Mount Desert Island; active in berry patches and along forest trails throughout summer.
Moose wade in Jordan Pond and Eagle Lake and are regularly seen in the park's wet woodland areas.
Bald eagles nest in Acadia and are commonly seen soaring the coastline and fishing the freshwater lakes.
Ospreys nest on platforms and cliff ledges throughout Acadia; fishing the coastal bays and freshwater ponds is a daily spectacle.
White-tailed deer are very abundant throughout Acadia and frequently seen on trails and roadsides.
Eastern coyotes are common throughout Acadia; their howling is frequently heard on the island at night.

name: Katmai NP
description: Alaska's world-famous bear-watching destination; up to 60 brown bears congregate at Brooks Falls in July to catch sockeye salmon leaping upstream in one of nature's greatest wildlife spectacles.

sightings.notes: Up to 60 brown bears congregate at Brooks Falls in July to catch sockeye salmon — one of the world's great wildlife spectacles.
Moose are abundant throughout Katmai's boreal forest and wetlands.
Caribou herds migrate through Katmai seasonally; sightings are most reliable in the interior of the park.
Wolves are present in Katmai but wide-ranging; sightings require luck.
Bald eagles congregate at Brooks Falls alongside bears during the salmon run; dozens may be visible simultaneously.
Wolverines inhabit the remote interior of Katmai; tracks in the snow are occasionally found along backcountry routes.
Ospreys nest along the rivers and lakes of Katmai; they share the salmon harvest with the brown bears at Brooks Falls.
Trumpeter swans nest in the wetlands and lakes of Katmai; the park's remote interior holds significant breeding pairs.

name: Sequoia NP
description: California's High Sierra giant sequoia park where black bears forage through the forest floor and Sierra Nevada bighorn sheep inhabit the highest granite ridges above 4,000 m.

sightings.notes: Black bears are very common in the Giant Forest; visitors frequently encounter them foraging in summer and early autumn.
Mountain lions inhabit Sequoia but are rarely seen; they are most active at dawn and dusk in mule deer habitat.
Sierra Nevada bighorn sheep recolonise the highest ridges above Kings Canyon; sightings are rare but possible above 4,000 m.
Ospreys fish the Kings River and the reservoirs of the Sierra Nevada foothills on the park periphery.
Coyotes are abundant in Sequoia and often seen trotting the meadows and campsite margins.
Mule deer are abundant throughout Sequoia; large bucks are commonly seen in the giant forest meadows.
Bald eagles winter around the reservoirs on the western edge of Sequoia NP; occasional winter sightings.
Elk occur in the lower western foothills of Sequoia; small herds are occasionally encountered in the oak woodland.
California condors soar over the Sierra Nevada escarpment; reintroduced birds from the recovery programme are increasingly seen over Sequoia NP.

name: North Cascades NP
description: Washington State's remote wilderness with the densest glacier network in the contiguous USA; grizzly bears, gray wolves, and wolverines inhabit the rugged interior peaks.

sightings.notes: A small remnant grizzly bear population persists in the North Cascades; one of the most rarely seen in the lower 48 states.
Gray wolves have recolonised the North Cascades from British Columbia; multiple packs are now established.
Moose are increasingly common in the North Cascades; the Methow Valley and Ross Lake area are productive.
Wolverines inhabit the remote alpine terrain of the North Cascades; one of the strongholds for this species in the lower 48 states.
Ospreys are common summer residents along Ross Lake and the Skagit River system in the park.
Black bears are common throughout North Cascades; they are far more frequently encountered than grizzlies.
Bald eagles are resident along the Skagit River; hundreds concentrate at the annual chum salmon run in winter.
California bighorn sheep inhabit the rocky cliffs of the Skagit River canyon and the Methow Valley walls.
White-tailed deer are abundant in the eastern (Methow) portions of North Cascades; mule deer in the western zones.

name: Kluane NP
description: Yukon's vast UNESCO World Heritage wilderness containing Canada's highest peak (Mount Logan) and the world's largest non-polar icefield, with grizzlies, Dall sheep, and large caribou herds.

sightings.notes: Grizzly bears are abundant in Kluane; the Slims River valley and alpine meadows are excellent viewing areas in summer.
Moose are common in the boreal forest and wetlands of the Kluane lowlands.
The Kluane caribou herd numbers in the thousands and can be encountered throughout the alpine zone.
Wolf packs inhabit Kluane and regularly follow caribou herds across the alpine terrain.
Wolverines are present in Kluane's vast wilderness; tracks in snow are more commonly encountered than actual sightings.
Black bears are present in Kluane's boreal forest, sharing habitat with grizzlies in the lower elevations.
Bald eagles are resident along the Slims and Dezadeash rivers in Kluane during the summer season.
Ospreys nest along the rivers and fish the clear lakes of Kluane during the summer.

name: Cape Breton Highlands NP
description: Nova Scotia's highland plateau dropping to the Gulf of St. Lawrence; black bears, moose wading in highland bogs, and bald eagles soaring the famous Cabot Trail cliffs.

sightings.notes: Black bears are very common in Cape Breton Highlands; berry foraging along the Cabot Trail roadsides in autumn.
Moose are abundant and frequently seen wading in highland bogs and wetlands along the Cabot Trail.
Eastern coyotes are common throughout Cape Breton Highlands and often heard at night.
Bald eagles nest in the highlands and fish the coastal fjords; year-round residents.
Ospreys are common summer residents, nesting on the highland lakes and fishing the coastal bays.
White-tailed deer are very common in Cape Breton Highlands; seen regularly along the Cabot Trail and in the forests.

name: Voyageurs NP
description: Minnesota's boreal lake wilderness on the Canadian border, where gray wolves maintain one of the highest densities in the lower 48 states and bald eagles nest on every forested shoreline.

sightings.notes: Black bears are common throughout Voyageurs' boreal forest; seen foraging berries and fish along the shorelines.
Moose are abundant in Voyageurs, frequently wading the interconnected boreal lakes and beaver ponds.
Voyageurs supports one of the highest gray wolf densities in the lower 48 states; pack territories overlap the park's islands.
One of the densest bald eagle nesting populations in the USA lines every forested shoreline in Voyageurs.
Ospreys nest throughout Voyageurs and can be seen diving for fish on virtually every lake in summer.
Eastern coyotes are common throughout Voyageurs; they travel the frozen lake ice in winter in search of deer.
White-tailed deer are abundant throughout the Voyageurs forest; primary prey for the wolf packs in winter.

name: Canaima NP
description: UNESCO World Heritage park of Angel Falls and the Gran Sabana tepuis — the landscape that inspired Conan Doyle's Lost World, with jaguars and harpy eagles in ancient sandstone highland rainforest.

sightings.notes: Jaguars are present in the lowland forests of Canaima; camera trap surveys confirm their presence.
Giant anteaters are regularly seen foraging the Gran Sabana grasslands in Canaima.
Capybaras are abundant in the riverine grasslands and lagoon margins throughout Canaima.
Scarlet macaws are frequently seen in flocks along the rivers and over the tepui forest of Canaima.
Harpy eagles are present in Canaima's tall forest; occasional sightings are reported by guides.
Toco toucans are commonly seen in the forest canopy of the Gran Sabana and along the tepui forest margins.
Giant otter families inhabit the rivers and lagoons of Canaima; the Carrao and Churun rivers are productive.
Spider monkeys are commonly heard and seen in the tall forest canopy surrounding the tepuis of Canaima.
Yacare caimans are abundant in the rivers and lagoons of the Gran Sabana; hundreds bask on sandy banks.
Roseate spoonbills feed in the shallow lagoons and wetlands of the Gran Sabana lowlands.
The Guianan cock-of-the-rock is endemic to Guiana Shield highland forest; Canaima's tepui flanks are key habitat for this spectacular orange bird.
The critically endangered Orinoco crocodile has been recorded in the Caura and Cuyuni rivers feeding into the Canaima region; one of the last wild populations.

name: Sierra Nevada NP (Venezuela)
description: Venezuelan Andes park protecting spectacled bears in cloud forest and paramo above Merida; South American tapirs inhabit the mid-altitude forests between the Andes peaks.

sightings.notes: Spectacled bears are the flagship species of Venezuela's Andes; present in the cloud forest above Merida.
Tapirs inhabit the mid-altitude cloud forest in the park; tracks along muddy trails are commonly found.
Mountain lions range through the high forests of the Sierra Nevada; sightings are uncommon but tracks are regularly found.
Andean condors soar the high paramo above 4,000 m; Venezuela's Andes hold a recovering population.
Giant anteaters are present in the lower savanna and forest edge habitats of the park.
Capybaras are common in the lowland river systems on the eastern slopes of the Venezuelan Andes.
Jaguars inhabit the mid-elevation forest of the Sierra Nevada; camera traps document their presence.

name: Los Katios NP
description: UNESCO Colombian Darien lowland rainforest on the Panama border — one of the most intact and biodiverse rainforest tracts in northern South America, with jaguars, tapirs, and giant otters.

sightings.notes: Los Katios is part of an important jaguar corridor connecting Central and South America; confirmed by camera traps.
Tapirs are present throughout the lowland forest of Los Katios; semi-aquatic habitats around the Atrato River are favoured.
Giant otters inhabit the rivers and oxbow lakes within the park; one of their Colombian strongholds.
Spider monkeys are abundant in the tall forest canopy of Los Katios; troops are commonly encountered.
Harpy eagles are present in the large forest tracts of Los Katios; sightings are rare but the habitat is ideal.
Capybaras are abundant in the wetlands and river margins of Los Katios.
Scarlet macaws are commonly seen in flocks over the tall forest of Los Katios.
Yacare caimans are abundant in the Atrato River wetlands within the park.
Toco toucans are frequently seen in the forest canopy of Los Katios.
Roseate spoonbills feed in the flooded forest and the oxbow lakes of the Atrato River.

name: Chiribiquete NP
description: UNESCO World Heritage tepui wilderness in the Colombian Amazon — the world's largest table-top mountain landscape with ancient rock art, pristine rainforest, jaguars, and harpy eagles.

sightings.notes: Jaguars inhabit the vast untouched forest of Chiribiquete; one of the most important jaguar reserves in Colombia.
Tapirs are present throughout the Amazonian lowland forest surrounding the tepuis of Chiribiquete.
Giant anteaters are found in the savanna and open tepui habitats of the Gran Sabana portions of the park.
Scarlet macaws are abundant throughout Chiribiquete's forest, nesting on the tepui cliff faces.
Harpy eagles nest in the tall trees of Chiribiquete's pristine Amazonian forest.
Giant otter families inhabit the pristine rivers flowing from the Chiribiquete tepuis.
Capybaras are abundant on the riverbanks and savanna margins of Chiribiquete.
Spider monkeys are abundant in the untouched forest of Chiribiquete; large troops are often encountered.
Toco toucans are commonly seen throughout the forest and forest edge habitats of Chiribiquete.
Yacare caimans are abundant in the rivers and seasonal pools throughout Chiribiquete.
The rocky tepui terrain of Chiribiquete is prime cock-of-the-rock habitat; males display at lek sites on the mossy tepui walls.

name: Amacayacu NP
description: Colombian Amazon floodplain park at the Brazil-Peru border with jaguars, tapirs, giant otters, capybaras, and spectacular flooded forest ecosystems on the Amazon River.

sightings.notes: Jaguars inhabit Amacayacu's dense Amazon forest; sightings are rare but tracks along the riverbanks are found regularly.
Tapirs are present throughout Amacayacu; the flooded forest edge during high water is productive for encounters.
Giant otter families inhabit the river channels and oxbow lakes of Amacayacu.
Spider monkeys are commonly seen in the forest canopy above the Amacayacu River.
Capybaras are abundant in the riverine habitats and grassy clearings of Amacayacu.
Scarlet macaws are commonly seen flying over the Amazon River and in the tall forest of Amacayacu.
Toco toucans are a familiar sight in the canopy throughout Amacayacu.
Yacare caimans are abundant in the oxbow lakes, channels, and flooded forest of Amacayacu.
Roseate spoonbills feed in the flooded forest margins and oxbow lakes throughout the park.
Giant anteaters are present in the forest clearings and open ground on the terra firme portions of the park.

name: Los Nevados NP
description: Colombian Central Andes park spanning five snow-capped volcanoes, with spectacled bears in cloud forest and Andean condors soaring the paramo above 5,000 m.

sightings.notes: Spectacled bears inhabit the cloud forest and paramo of Los Nevados; camera traps regularly confirm their presence.
Andean condors are commonly seen soaring the volcanic peaks and paramo of Los Nevados.
Mountain tapirs are present in the cloud forest zones below the paramo; a rare and threatened subspecies.
Mountain lions are present in Los Nevados; tracks are found in the forest and paramo but sightings are uncommon.
Giant anteaters occur in the lower elevation grasslands on the fringes of Los Nevados.
Capybaras inhabit the lower elevation rivers and wetlands on the fringes of Los Nevados.
Jaguars are present in the lower cloud forest zones of Los Nevados; camera traps document occasional visits.

name: Sangay NP
description: UNESCO World Heritage Ecuadorian Andes park with two active volcanoes, spectacled bears in cloud forest, and mountain tapirs in the paramo and humid Amazonian slopes.

sightings.notes: Spectacled bears are the signature mammal of Sangay; cloud forest slopes between 2,000 and 4,000 m are prime habitat.
Mountain lions inhabit the full altitudinal range of Sangay from cloud forest to paramo.
Both lowland and mountain tapirs may occur in Sangay across its wide altitudinal range.
Andean condors soar above the volcanic peaks; sightings are occasional but regular above 4,000 m.
Giant otters inhabit the lower Amazon tributary rivers flowing east from Sangay.
Capybaras inhabit the lower Amazonian tributary rivers on the eastern slopes of Sangay.
Spider monkeys are present in the lowland forest on the eastern Amazonian slopes of Sangay.
Scarlet macaws are commonly seen in the lower eastern forest of Sangay.
Giant anteaters are present in the mid-altitude grasslands and forest clearings of the Sangay highlands.
Toco toucans are common in the lowland and submontane forest of Sangay's eastern slopes.

name: Cotopaxi NP
description: Ecuador's most visited park centred on the perfectly conical Cotopaxi volcano (5,897 m), with Andean condors soaring the summit, spectacled bears on the forested flanks, and mountain lions on the paramo.

sightings.notes: Spectacled bears inhabit the cloud forest on Cotopaxi's lower slopes; camera traps have confirmed their presence.
Andean condors are regularly seen soaring the flanks of Cotopaxi; up to 10 individuals may be visible simultaneously.
Mountain lions are present in Cotopaxi; they are the primary predators of deer and wild horses on the paramo.
Ospreys are occasional migrants and winter visitors to the lagoons in the Cotopaxi paramo.
Mountain tapirs inhabit the cloud forest on Cotopaxi's lower forested flanks; camera traps document their presence.
Capybaras inhabit the wetland areas in the lower valleys on the park margins.
Vicuñas were reintroduced to Cotopaxi NP and are now commonly seen on the páramo grasslands surrounding the volcano.

name: Huascaran NP
description: UNESCO Peruvian Andes park containing Huascaran (6,768 m), Peru's highest peak; spectacled bears inhabit the cloud forest and Andean condors soar the glaciated ridges of the Cordillera Blanca.

sightings.notes: Spectacled bears inhabit the polylepis woodland and cloud forest on Huascaran's lower slopes.
Andean condors are a common sight soaring the glaciated ridges of the Cordillera Blanca.
Mountain lions are present in Huascaran; they prey on taruca (Andean deer) in the upper valleys.
Ospreys are occasional visitors to the higher lakes of the Cordillera Blanca during migration.
Mountain tapirs inhabit the polylepis woodland and cloud forest on the lower slopes of Huascaran.
Vicunas (represented by guanaco in our database) are abundant on the high grasslands of the Cordillera Blanca above 4,000 m.
Vicuñas are abundant on the high grasslands (puna) of the Cordillera Blanca; their cinnamon-and-white coats stand out against the snow-capped peaks.
The Andean cat has been camera-trapped in the rocky terrain above treeline in Huascaran NP; one of the world's rarest and least-studied felids.

name: Tambopata NR
description: Peru's most species-rich accessible Amazon reserve, famous for the Chuncho clay lick where thousands of macaws and parrots gather daily, with jaguars, giant otters, and tapirs throughout.

sightings.notes: Jaguars are present in Tambopata; tracks are found regularly along the riverbanks and occasional sightings are reported.
Tapirs are commonly encountered on the Tambopata riverbanks at dawn and dusk; one of the best parks in Peru for sightings.
Giant otter families inhabit the oxbow lakes of Tambopata; Lake Sandoval has a well-established group.
The Chuncho clay lick in Tambopata attracts hundreds of scarlet macaws and other parrots daily — one of the great wildlife spectacles.
Harpy eagles are present in Tambopata's tall forest; a known nest site makes occasional sightings possible with local guides.
Capybaras are very abundant along the Tambopata and Malinowski rivers; groups of 20 or more are routine.
Yacare caimans are abundant in the oxbow lakes and rivers of Tambopata; the Chuncho lake alone has hundreds.
Spider monkeys are commonly encountered in the canopy; their whooping calls carry across the forest.
Toco toucans are a signature species of Tambopata; pairs are seen daily at clay lick trees and in the canopy.
Giant anteaters are occasionally encountered on the forest trails of Tambopata.
Roseate spoonbills feed in the oxbow lakes and shallow river margins throughout Tambopata.

name: Bahuaja-Sonene NP
description: Peru's most pristine Amazon park on the Bolivian border, adjacent to Madidi NP and together forming one of the most biodiverse protected area complexes on Earth.

sightings.notes: Jaguars inhabit the pristine forest of Bahuaja-Sonene; one of Peru's most important jaguar reserves.
Tapirs are common along the rivers and forest edge habitats throughout the park.
Giant otter families inhabit the Heath and Tambopata rivers flowing through the park.
Capybaras are abundant along the river margins and open grasslands of the Heath River savanna within the park.
Scarlet macaws are abundant throughout the park's vast pristine forest.
Giant anteaters are present in the Pampas del Heath grasslands; one of the few Peruvian parks where they are regularly seen.
Spider monkeys are abundant in the tall forest of Bahuaja-Sonene.
Toco toucans are commonly seen in the forest canopy throughout the park.
Yacare caimans are abundant in the Heath and Tambopata rivers and the Pampas del Heath wetlands.
Maned wolves are regularly seen in the Pampas del Heath — the only Peruvian savanna habitat where this species occurs.

name: Machu Picchu HS
description: UNESCO Inca citadel sanctuary protecting cloud forest on Andean flanks above the Urubamba River, with spectacled bears, Andean condors, and mountain tapirs among the ruins and jungle.

sightings.notes: Spectacled bears inhabit the cloud forest of the Machu Picchu sanctuary; occasionally encountered on forest trails.
Andean condors are regularly seen soaring above the Urubamba gorge and the Machu Picchu citadel.
Tapirs are present in the lower cloud forest of the sanctuary; camera traps have documented their presence.
Mountain lions inhabit the steep forested gorges of the Machu Picchu sanctuary.
Scarlet macaws are commonly seen flying over the Urubamba River canyon below Machu Picchu.
Toucans occur in the lower cloudforest sections of the Urubamba valley below Machu Picchu.
Giant otters are occasionally reported on the Urubamba (Vilcanota) River in the canyon below Machu Picchu.

name: Paracas NR
description: Coastal Peruvian desert reserve on a highly productive Pacific upwelling; ospreys, Andean condors soar the sea cliffs, and Humboldt penguins nest on the offshore Ballestas Islands.

sightings.notes: Ospreys are year-round residents at Paracas, fishing the cold upwelling waters of the Pacific.
Andean condors are regularly seen soaring the coastal cliffs of Paracas and feeding on marine carrion.
Spoonbills occasionally visit the sheltered bays and lagoons of Paracas during the austral spring and summer.
Mountain lions are occasionally recorded in the Paracas desert, preying on marine birds and sea lions.
Guanacos inhabit the Paracas desert and are commonly seen on the coastal clifftops and the Paracas Peninsula.

name: Cordillera Azul NP
description: Isolated Peruvian mountain range between the Andes and Amazon, largely unexplored and extremely biodiverse; jaguars, spectacled bears, and black spider monkeys inhabit pristine forest.

sightings.notes: Jaguars are present in the isolated forest of Cordillera Azul; camera surveys confirm their presence.
Tapirs are present throughout the park's diverse altitudinal habitats.
Spectacled bears inhabit the montane forest in the upper zones of Cordillera Azul.
Spider monkeys are abundant in the undisturbed lowland forest of Cordillera Azul.
Mountain lions are the apex predator across all altitude zones of Cordillera Azul.
Giant otters inhabit the rivers draining the Cordillera Azul; the pristine watershed supports a healthy population.
Scarlet macaws are commonly seen in the forest canopy and along the river corridors of Cordillera Azul.
Toco toucans are commonly seen throughout the park's lowland and submontane forest.
Capybaras are abundant along the rivers and lake margins throughout the park.
Giant anteaters are present in the forest clearings and open terrain within Cordillera Azul.

name: Pacaya-Samiria NR
description: Peru's largest protected area and one of the world's largest flooded forest reserves; jaguars, giant otters, capybaras, and caimans inhabit the permanently inundated Amazon forest.

sightings.notes: Jaguars are present throughout Pacaya-Samiria; the flooded forest edge is the best habitat for chance encounters.
Tapirs are commonly encountered along the riverbanks and lake margins of Pacaya-Samiria.
Giant otter families are abundant in the oxbow lakes and river channels; one of the best places in Peru for sightings.
Capybaras are very abundant on the riverbanks throughout the reserve.
Scarlet macaws are commonly seen in flocks along the rivers and over the flooded forest canopy.
Giant anteaters are present on terra firme ground in Pacaya-Samiria; encountered on forest trail walks.
Spider monkeys are abundant and commonly encountered in the canopy throughout the reserve.
Toco toucans are very common throughout Pacaya-Samiria; their calls are among the most heard in the forest.
Yacare caimans are extraordinarily abundant in the flooded forest; lake margins are lined with basking individuals.
Roseate spoonbills are commonly seen feeding in the oxbow lakes and shallow river margins throughout the reserve.
Harpy eagles inhabit the tall flooded forest of Pacaya-Samiria; occasional sightings are reported.
Giant armadillos are present in the terra firme forest of Pacaya-Samiria; nocturnal and rarely seen, but camera-trapped throughout the reserve.

name: Yanachaga-Chemillen NP
description: Central Peruvian Andes park spanning cloud forest to Amazon lowlands, with spectacled bears on the highland slopes and jaguars and giant otters in the lowland river systems.

sightings.notes: Spectacled bears are the flagship species of Yanachaga; the cloud forest slopes are prime habitat.
