Both mountain and lowland tapirs are present across the altitudinal gradient of the park.
Jaguars are present in the lower Amazon tributaries of the park.
Giant otters inhabit the rivers in the lower sections of the park.
Mountain lions are present throughout the altitudinal range of the park.
Capybaras inhabit the lowland river systems within Yanachaga-Chemillen.
Scarlet macaws are commonly seen in the lowland sections of Yanachaga-Chemillen.
Spider monkeys are present in the lowland forest on the Amazonian side of the park.
Toco toucans are common in the submontane and lowland forest of the park.
Giant anteaters are present in the lowland forest clearings of Yanachaga-Chemillen.

name: Noel Kempff Mercado NP
description: UNESCO World Heritage Bolivian Amazon park on the Brazilian border with extraordinary biodiversity — giant otters, jaguars, giant anteaters, and over 600 bird species in pristine forest.

sightings.notes: Jaguars inhabit the diverse habitats of Noel Kempff; tracks are commonly found along the rivers.
Giant anteaters are commonly seen in the cerrado grasslands and open savanna of the park.
Giant otter families inhabit the rivers and oxbow lakes; the Itenez River is particularly productive.
Tapirs are present across the forest and forest edge habitats of this vast park.
Spider monkeys are abundant in the Amazonian forest sections of the park.
Capybaras are abundant along the rivers and wetland margins of Noel Kempff Mercado.
Scarlet macaws are abundant in the tall forest of Noel Kempff; flocks are seen crossing the park daily.
Toco toucans are extremely common in both the Amazonian forest and the cerrado woodland.
Yacare caimans are abundant in the rivers and wetlands; the Itenez River bank is lined with basking individuals.
Maned wolves are regularly seen in the cerrado sections at dusk; Noel Kempff is one of their Bolivian strongholds.
Roseate spoonbills are commonly seen feeding in the wetlands and river margins of the park.
Giant armadillos are documented by camera traps throughout Noel Kempff Mercado, an important cerrado and Amazonian forest park.

name: Amboro NP
description: Bolivian park at the ecological crossroads of Andes, Amazon, and Chaco — one of the world's top biodiversity hotspots, with spectacled bears, jaguars, and tapirs across multiple altitude zones.

sightings.notes: Spectacled bears are the signature mammal of Amboro; cloud forest above 2,000 m is prime territory.
Tapirs are present in Amboro's lowland and submontane forest zones.
Jaguars are present in the lowland forest sections of Amboro.
Scarlet macaws are abundant in the lowland forest of Amboro and frequently seen in flocks.
Mountain lions are present throughout Amboro's diverse habitats.
Capybaras inhabit the rivers and wetlands in the lowland forest zones of Amboro.
Spider monkeys are abundant in the lowland Amazonian forest of Amboro.
Giant anteaters are present in the open areas and grasslands on the lower slopes of Amboro.
Toco toucans are commonly seen in the lowland and submontane forest of Amboro.
Giant otters are present in the rivers flowing through Amboro's Amazonian zone.

name: Kaa-Iya del Gran Chaco NP
description: South America's largest national park and the world's largest dry forest protected area, managed with indigenous Isoseno-Guarani people; jaguars and giant anteaters inhabit the dry forest.

sightings.notes: Jaguars are present throughout the vast dry forest of Kaa-Iya; camera trap surveys confirm a significant population.
Giant anteaters are present in the dry forest and grassland habitats throughout the park.
Maned wolves are present at the Chaco margins of the park where transitional habitats occur.
Mountain lions are present as apex predators in the Chaco ecosystem.
Yacare caimans inhabit the seasonal waterholes and rivers within the park.
Capybaras inhabit the seasonal waterholes and rivers within the Chaco park.
Tapirs are present in the gallery forest along rivers within the dry Chaco.
Giant otters are occasionally present in the seasonal rivers within the Chaco.

name: Sajama NP
description: Bolivia's oldest national park centred on Nevado Sajama (6,542 m), South America's highest volcano; guanacos graze the surrounding altiplano as Andean condors patrol the glaciated peak.

sightings.notes: Guanacos are abundant on the Sajama altiplano, grazing the high grassland around the volcano base.
Andean condors are regularly seen soaring the thermal currents above Nevado Sajama.
Mountain lions are the primary predators of guanacos and vizcachas on the Sajama altiplano.
Ospreys occasionally visit the high lakes of Sajama during the austral spring and summer.
Vicuñas are very common on the altiplano grasslands surrounding Nevado Sajama; herds of 20–50 animals are routinely seen.

name: Eduardo Avaroa NR
description: Bolivia's most visited reserve in the Atacama highlands, with dramatic volcanic lagoons hosting three flamingo species, guanacos, and Andean condors at extreme altitude above 4,000 m.

sightings.notes: Guanacos are commonly seen grazing the altiplano steppe around the volcanic lagoons of Eduardo Avaroa.
Andean condors are regularly seen soaring above the lagoons and volcanic peaks of the reserve.
Mountain lions are present in the reserve; they prey on vicunias and guanacos across the altiplano.
Ospreys are rare but occasional visitors to the high-altitude lakes of the reserve.
Vicuñas are abundant in the high Bolivian altiplano of Eduardo Avaroa; large herds are seen on the Siloli desert and near Laguna Colorada.
The Andean cat has been recorded in Eduardo Avaroa; camera traps have documented this critically endangered felid in the altiplano.

name: Emas NP
description: UNESCO World Heritage Cerrado park; widely considered the world's single best location to see giant anteaters, giant armadillos, and maned wolves together on the Brazilian highland plateau.

sightings.notes: Emas is arguably the world's best location for giant anteater sightings; they are abundant on the cerrado grassland.
Maned wolves are regularly seen at dawn and dusk hunting the cerrado grassland; Emas is one of their last strongholds.
Capybaras are abundant along the rivers and wetlands of Emas NP.
Giant otters inhabit the rivers flowing through the park; a significant population is resident.
Yacare caimans are abundant in the wetlands and rivers of Emas, particularly in the wet season.
Tapirs are present in the gallery forest and cerrado of Emas; camera traps regularly confirm their presence.
Jaguars are present in Emas NP; sightings are rare but increasing as the population recovers.
Toco toucans are very common in the cerrado woodland and forest gallery of Emas NP.
Roseate spoonbills are commonly seen feeding in the wetlands and veredas of Emas NP.
Scarlet macaws are occasionally seen at the forest gallery margins within Emas NP.
Emas NP is one of the best places to see Hyacinth Macaws outside the Pantanal; pairs nest in buriti palm cavities and fly over the cerrado in brilliant blue flocks.
Giant armadillos are present in Emas NP; camera traps regularly document them raiding termite mounds at night — sightings in the field are rare.
Marsh deer inhabit the gallery forest and vereda wetlands of Emas NP; South America's largest deer, a spectacular sight at the waterways.

name: Jau NP
description: Brazil's largest national park entirely within the Amazon, protecting the entire Jau River basin; UNESCO-listed for pristine blackwater ecosystems and giant river otters.

sightings.notes: Jaguars inhabit the vast blackwater forest of Jau; one of the largest jaguar reserves in the Amazon.
Tapirs are present throughout the flooded and terra firme forest of the Jau River basin.
Giant otters are present in the blackwater rivers; the Jau River system is among their last Amazonian refuges.
Spider monkeys are abundant throughout Jau's pristine forest canopy.
Harpy eagles inhabit the tall forest of Jau; the park's pristine state supports a healthy population.
Capybaras are abundant on the river banks and sandy beaches of the Jau River.
Scarlet macaws are commonly seen in the tall forest canopy and along the river margins of Jau NP.
Toco toucans are abundant throughout Jau's pristine forest.
Yacare caimans are abundant in the blackwater rivers of Jau NP; they thrive in the acidic water.
Giant anteaters are present on the terra firme sections of Jau NP.
Bush dogs are present in Jau NP; sightings of these unusual semi-aquatic pack hunters are rare but documented.

name: Anavilhanas NP
description: World's largest river archipelago — over 400 islands in the Rio Negro near Manaus — creating one of the most extraordinary seasonally flooded Amazonian wildlife habitats on Earth.

sightings.notes: Jaguars are present on the larger islands and the mainland forest of Anavilhanas during the dry season.
Giant otter families are resident in the channels between the archipelago islands; the Rio Negro is an important stronghold.
Tapirs are present on the mainland and larger islands of the Anavilhanas archipelago.
Capybaras are abundant on the island shores and riverbanks throughout the archipelago.
Scarlet macaws are commonly seen along the Rio Negro shores and over the forested islands.
Spider monkeys are commonly encountered on the larger islands and mainland forest of Anavilhanas.
Toco toucans are commonly seen in the forest canopy throughout the Anavilhanas archipelago.
Yacare caimans are abundant in the channels between the Anavilhanas islands; they emerge to bask on the beaches.
Roseate spoonbills are regularly seen feeding in the shallow lake margins around the archipelago.
Giant anteaters are present on the terra firme and mainland edge of the Anavilhanas reserve.

name: Pico da Neblina NP
description: Remote Brazilian park on the Venezuelan border containing Brazil's highest peak (2,994 m); pristine tepui ecosystems, jaguars, and tapirs in some of the most isolated forest on Earth.

sightings.notes: Jaguars are present in the pristine forest of Pico da Neblina; one of the most remote jaguar habitats in South America.
Tapirs inhabit the lowland forest of this remote park; access restrictions ensure minimal disturbance.
Giant otters may be present in the rivers flowing from Pico da Neblina.
Spider monkeys are abundant in the undisturbed forest of Pico da Neblina.
Harpy eagles are present in the tall Amazonian forest; the park's remoteness protects a significant population.
Capybaras inhabit the rivers on the lowland fringes of Pico da Neblina NP.
Scarlet macaws are commonly seen in the lowland forest of Pico da Neblina.
Giant anteaters are present on the terra firme and savanna patches within the park.

name: Chapada Diamantina NP
description: Brazilian highland plateau in Bahia with dramatic waterfalls and table-top mountains; giant anteaters forage the campos gerais and maned wolves hunt at dusk among the rocky terrain.

sightings.notes: Giant anteaters are commonly seen foraging the campos gerais plateau grasslands in Chapada Diamantina.
Maned wolves hunt the plateau grassland and forest edges in Chapada Diamantina.
Mountain lions are present in Chapada Diamantina; they inhabit the rocky terrain and forest remnants.
Capybaras are abundant along the rivers and wetlands in the lower valleys of the park.
Toco toucans are commonly seen throughout the cerrado and forest habitats of Chapada Diamantina.
Tapirs are present in the forest gallery and cerrado of Chapada Diamantina; camera traps confirm their presence.
Jaguars have been documented in Chapada Diamantina; they use the gallery forest as movement corridors.
Roseate spoonbills are occasionally seen in the wetland sections and rivers of Chapada Diamantina.
Caimans inhabit the rivers and seasonally flooded areas within the park.
Scarlet macaws are occasionally seen in the forest gallery and cerrado woodland.

name: Lencois Maranhenses NP
description: Extraordinary Brazilian coastal dune field where thousands of freshwater lagoons form between white sand dunes after the seasonal rains; capybaras and rich birdlife inhabit the wetland margins.

sightings.notes: Ospreys are resident at Lencois Maranhenses, fishing the seasonal freshwater lagoons and the coastal margins.
Roseate spoonbills are commonly seen feeding in the shallow lagoons that form between the dunes after rain.
Capybaras graze the wetland margins on the edges of the dune field.
Caimans inhabit the seasonal lagoons and river channels on the inland side of the park.
Giant anteaters are occasionally encountered in the cerrado and scrub habitats on the park margins.
Tapirs are present in the cerrado and forest gallery on the inland margins of the dune field.
Scarlet macaws are occasionally seen in the woodland on the inland fringes of the park.

name: Serra dos Orgaos NP
description: Dramatic Atlantic Forest peaks near Rio de Janeiro with iconic rock pinnacles, cloud forest tapirs, toucans, and bromeliads in subtropical highland forest.

sightings.notes: Tapirs are present in the Atlantic Forest of Serra dos Orgaos; camera traps document their recovery.
Mountain lions inhabit the steep forested slopes; camera traps confirm their presence near Teresopolis.
Toco toucans are conspicuous in the canopy throughout the accessible parts of Serra dos Orgaos.
Scarlet macaws are seen in flocks over the forested hillsides of Serra dos Orgaos.
Ospreys occasionally visit the reservoirs near Serra dos Orgaos during the southern summer.
Capybaras inhabit the lower valley streams on the margins of Serra dos Orgaos NP.
Jaguars are present in the Atlantic Forest of Serra dos Orgaos; camera traps have recorded their movements.

name: Itatiaia NP
description: Brazil's oldest national park in the Mantiqueira mountains; exceptional Atlantic Forest birding with toucans and scarlet macaws, plus tapirs and mountain lions in the highland forest.

sightings.notes: Tapirs are present in Itatiaia's lower Atlantic Forest; camera traps document their presence.
Mountain lions inhabit the highland plateau of Itatiaia and the surrounding forest.
Toco toucans are commonly seen in the lower forest zones of Itatiaia.
Scarlet macaws are regularly seen flying over the forested valleys of Itatiaia.
Capybaras inhabit the riparian areas in the lower sections of Itatiaia NP.
Maned wolves inhabit the highland grasslands (campos de altitude) of Itatiaia; seen hunting at dawn and dusk.
Jaguars are present in the Atlantic Forest of Itatiaia; camera traps confirm their presence.
Giant anteaters inhabit the open campos areas of the Itatiaia high plateau.

name: Aparados da Serra NP
description: Brazilian canyon park on the RS/SC border with the spectacular Itaimbezinho canyon; maned wolves hunt the grassland plateau above and tapirs inhabit the dense Atlantic Forest below.

sightings.notes: Tapirs are present in the dense Atlantic Forest at the base of the Itaimbezinho canyon.
Mountain lions are present in Aparados da Serra; camera traps document a recovering population.
Toco toucans are commonly seen in the araucaria forest and forest fragments around the park.
Maned wolves occasionally inhabit the grassland plateau above the canyon.
Ospreys occasionally fish the rivers at the base of the Aparados da Serra canyon system.
Capybaras inhabit the rivers at the base of the Itaimbezinho canyon and on the plateau margins.
Jaguars use the Atlantic Forest corridor of Aparados da Serra; camera traps occasionally record their passage.
Giant anteaters are present on the open grassland plateau above the Aparados canyons.

name: Grande Sertao Veredas NP
description: UNESCO Cerrado park protecting the palm-fringed vereda wetlands immortalised by Guimaraes Rosa's novel; maned wolves, giant anteaters, and giant otters inhabit the gallery forest.

sightings.notes: Giant anteaters are commonly seen foraging the cerrado and vereda palm grasslands of the park.
Maned wolves hunt the cerrado at dusk and dawn; the park is an important stronghold in Minas Gerais state.
Giant otter families inhabit the gallery forest rivers and veredas within the park.
Capybaras are abundant along the rivers and in the wet vereda habitats of the park.
Yacare caimans are abundant in the rivers and seasonally flooded veredas of the park.
Tapirs are present in the gallery forest and veredas of Grande Sertao; camera traps regularly document them.
Jaguars are present in Grande Sertao Veredas; the cerrado is an important jaguar habitat.
Toco toucans are commonly seen in the cerrado woodland throughout the park.
Scarlet macaws are commonly seen in the gallery forest and cerrado areas of the park.
Roseate spoonbills are regularly seen in the vereda palm wetlands and river margins.
The veredas (palm-lined streams) of this park are a stronghold for Hyacinth Macaws, which nest in the buriti palms lining the watercourses.
Giant armadillos are documented by camera traps throughout Grande Sertao Veredas, raiding termite mounds and ant colonies at night.
Marsh deer are a signature species of the veredas wetland corridors within this park.

name: Ibera NP
description: Corrientes wetland park at the heart of Argentina's most ambitious rewilding project; jaguars, giant anteaters, and giant river otters are being reintroduced to the vast Ibera marshes.

sightings.notes: Jaguars were successfully reintroduced to Ibera by Rewilding Argentina; cubs have been born and the population is growing.
Capybaras are extraordinarily abundant in the Ibera wetlands — among the highest densities in South America.
Yacare caimans are extremely abundant throughout the Ibera lagoon system.
Giant anteaters were reintroduced to Ibera and the population is now self-sustaining.
Maned wolves are present in the gallery forest and grassland margins of the Ibera marshes.
Giant otters were reintroduced to Ibera and have established a breeding population in the Cambyreta lagoon.
Tapirs are present in the gallery forest and wooded islands of the Ibera wetlands.
Roseate spoonbills are very common throughout the Ibera lagoon system; among the most visible large birds.
Toco toucans are commonly seen in the forest patches and gallery woodland surrounding the Ibera marshes.
Ibera holds one of South America's largest populations of marsh deer; they are seen daily wading through the shallow lagoons and marshes.

name: Lanin NP
description: Neuquen park dominated by the perfect cone of Volcan Lanin (3,776 m); guanacos roam the eastern steppe, mountain lions inhabit the monkey-puzzle araucaria forest, and Andean condors patrol the ridges.

sightings.notes: Guanacos are common on the eastern steppe and transition forest zones of Lanin.
Mountain lions are the main large predator in Lanin; they prey on guanacos and introduced deer.
Andean condors are commonly seen soaring the flanks of Volcan Lanin and the surrounding Andes.
Introduced European red deer are abundant in Lanin's araucaria and lenga beech forest.
Ospreys fish the clear glacial lakes of Lanin including Lago Huechulafquen during the austral summer.

name: El Palmar NP
description: Entre Rios park protecting the last extensive yatay palm savanna in Argentina, with capybaras, caiman, maned wolves, and over 200 bird species.

sightings.notes: Capybaras are very abundant in El Palmar; large groups graze the palm savanna at dawn and dusk.
Yacare caimans inhabit the streams and wetlands within and around El Palmar.
Maned wolves hunt the palm savanna of El Palmar; camera traps confirm regular activity.
Giant anteaters have been recorded in El Palmar; sightings are infrequent but confirmed.
Ospreys fish the Uruguay River along the eastern boundary of El Palmar during the austral summer.
Giant otters have been recorded in the Uruguay River along the eastern boundary of El Palmar.
Roseate spoonbills feed in the wetlands and river margins throughout El Palmar.
Tapirs are present in the gallery forest along the rivers of El Palmar.
Marsh deer inhabit the gallery forest and wetland margins of El Palmar along the Uruguay River.

name: Quebrada de Humahuaca
description: UNESCO World Heritage Jujuy gorge and Andean cultural landscape with guanacos on the high puna, Andean condors circling the painted hills, and mountain lions in the surrounding quebradas.

sightings.notes: Guanacos are commonly seen on the high puna and hillsides surrounding the Humahuaca gorge.
Andean condors soar the painted hills and canyons of the Quebrada de Humahuaca, a regular sight for visitors.
Mountain lions inhabit the surrounding highlands and are occasionally reported near the quebrada.
Ospreys occasionally visit the Rio Grande and high-altitude wetlands of the Humahuaca region.
Mountain tapirs may be present in the cloud forest on the higher slopes above the Humahuaca gorge.
Spectacled bears are occasionally reported in the forest above the Quebrada de Humahuaca gorge.

name: Monte Leon NP
description: Patagonian coastal steppe park with one of Argentina's largest Magellanic penguin colonies; guanacos graze the plain, mountain lions patrol the coast, and Andean condors ride cliff updrafts.

sightings.notes: Guanacos are abundant on the Patagonian steppe of Monte Leon; herds of several hundred are common.
Mountain lions are the dominant predator of Monte Leon; they patrol both the steppe and the dramatic coastline.
Andean condors are commonly seen riding thermals along the Monte Leon sea cliffs.
Ospreys are regular summer visitors to Monte Leon, fishing the Patagonian coast during the austral summer.
Roseate spoonbills are occasionally seen in the sheltered coastal bays of Monte Leon during the austral summer.

name: Lauca NP
description: Altiplano park on the Chile-Bolivia border at 4,500 m; guanacos and vizcachas graze around Lago Chungu'ara, Andean condors soar overhead, and mountain lions stalk the lakeshores at night.

sightings.notes: Guanacos are commonly seen on the altiplano around Lago Chunguara and throughout the park.
Andean condors are regularly seen soaring the volcanic peaks and lakeshores of Lauca.
Mountain lions are present in the altiplano; they are seldom seen but tracks are found around vicunia herds.
Ospreys occasionally visit the high-altitude lakes of Lauca during the austral spring and summer.
Lauca NP is one of the best places in the Andes to see vicuñas; herds of hundreds graze the high puna above 4,500 m around Lago Chungara.
Lauca NP is one of the few places with documented Andean cat records; this elusive feline hunts mountain viscachas in the rocky puna.

name: Conguillio NP
description: Araucania active volcano park with Llaima (3,125 m) surrounded by ancient monkey-puzzle araucaria forests sheltering mountain lions, South Andean deer, and Magellanic woodpeckers.

sightings.notes: Guanacos are present in the open areas and araucaria woodland of Conguillio.
Mountain lions are the primary large predator in Conguillio; they prey on deer in the araucaria forest.
Andean condors soar above Llaima volcano and are a common sight in the Conguillio sky.
Introduced European red deer are abundant in Conguillio's araucaria and lenga beech forest.
Ospreys occasionally fish Lago Conguillio during the austral summer.

name: Vicente Perez Rosales NP
description: Chile's oldest national park around Lago Todos los Santos and Volcan Osorno; mountain lions, Andean condors, and ospreys inhabit the lush Valdivian temperate rainforest.

sightings.notes: Guanacos inhabit the open forest edges and hillsides of Vicente Perez Rosales.
Mountain lions are present throughout the Valdivian rainforest of Vicente Perez Rosales.
Andean condors soar Volcan Osorno and the surrounding Andes; one of the most reliable areas for views.
Ospreys fish Lago Todos los Santos and Lago Llanquihue during the austral summer.
Introduced European red deer are common in the Valdivian forest of Vicente Perez Rosales.

name: Pan de Azucar NP
description: Atacama coastal desert park with guanacos in the fog zone, Humboldt penguin colonies on offshore islands, Andean condors sailing the sea cliffs, and ospreys fishing the cold Pacific.

sightings.notes: Guanacos are abundant in the fog-watered coastal desert of Pan de Azucar; large herds are commonly encountered.
Andean condors soar the coastal cliffs and offshore islands of Pan de Azucar, feeding on marine carrion.
Mountain lions are present in Pan de Azucar; tracks are found along the coastal hillsides.
Ospreys are resident along the cold Humboldt Current coast of Pan de Azucar, fishing the productive waters year-round.
Roseate spoonbills occasionally visit the coastal lagoons and sheltered bays of Pan de Azucar.

name: Kaieteur NP
description: Pristine Guyanese park protecting Kaieteur Falls (226 m, one of the world's most powerful waterfalls) surrounded by untouched Amazon rainforest with harpy eagles, jaguars, and tapirs.

sightings.notes: Jaguars are present in the pristine forest surrounding Kaieteur Falls; one of the most intact jaguar habitats in Guyana.
Tapirs are present in the lowland forest around the Kaieteur plateau; tracks along rivers are commonly found.
Giant otters may be present in the rivers below Kaieteur Falls.
Spider monkeys are abundant in the undisturbed forest of Kaieteur; troops are frequently encountered.
Harpy eagles inhabit the tall forest of the Kaieteur plateau; sightings are rare but the habitat is prime.
Capybaras are abundant on the river banks surrounding Kaieteur Falls.
Scarlet macaws are commonly seen in the forest canopy surrounding Kaieteur Falls.
Giant anteaters are present in the savanna and open forest around the Kaieteur plateau.
Toco toucans are abundant throughout the forest of Kaieteur NP.
The Guianan cock-of-the-rock is abundant in the rocky ravines around Kaieteur Falls; males gather at dawn leks on mossy boulders — one of South America's most spectacular wildlife sights.

name: Central Suriname NR
description: UNESCO World Heritage Surinamese highland wilderness — four million hectares of virtually untouched primary tropical forest with harpy eagles, jaguars, tapirs, and giant otters.

sightings.notes: Jaguars inhabit the vast pristine forest of Central Suriname; one of the largest intact jaguar habitats in South America.
Tapirs are present throughout the reserve; the rivers and forest edge are productive for encounters.
Giant otters inhabit the rivers of the Coppename watershed within the reserve.
Spider monkeys are abundant throughout Central Suriname's untouched forest.
Harpy eagles are present in this pristine forest; the lack of hunting pressure supports a healthy population.
Capybaras are abundant on the river banks throughout Central Suriname NR.
Scarlet macaws are commonly seen in flocks over the forest canopy of Central Suriname.
Giant anteaters are regularly encountered on the open areas and forest clearings of Central Suriname.
Toco toucans are very common throughout the Surinamese forest.
Yacare caimans are abundant in the rivers and oxbow lakes of Central Suriname NR.
Roseate spoonbills are regularly seen in the wetlands and river margins throughout the reserve.
Bush dogs inhabit the pristine forest of Central Suriname NR; their webbed feet make them effective hunters in the flooded forest.

name: Defensores del Chaco NP
description: Paraguay's largest park in the dry Gran Chaco — a semi-arid savanna wilderness with jaguars, giant anteaters, and enormous biodiversity in one of the world's most endangered biomes.

sightings.notes: Jaguars are present in the dry forest of Defensores del Chaco; the park is part of a key jaguar corridor.
Giant anteaters are present throughout the Chaco scrub and grassland habitats of the park.
Maned wolves are present in the transitional habitats at the eastern margins of the park.
Mountain lions are the apex predator in the dry Chaco ecosystem of the park.
Yacare caimans inhabit the seasonal waterholes and rivers of the dry Chaco within the park.
Capybaras are common near the seasonal waterholes and rivers of the dry Chaco.
Tapirs are present in the gallery forest along the rivers and watercourses of the Chaco.
Giant otters are present in the permanent rivers within the Paraguayan Chaco.

name: Cerro Cora NP
description: Paraguay's most accessible national park near the Brazilian border, with Atlantic Forest mammals including tapirs, maned wolves, and an important jaguar corridor.

sightings.notes: Jaguars use Cerro Cora as a corridor; camera trap records confirm their presence.
Tapirs are present in the Atlantic Forest of Cerro Cora; muddy river margins show their tracks regularly.
Maned wolves are present in the grassland and cerrado patches surrounding the forest of Cerro Cora.
Giant anteaters have been recorded in Cerro Cora in the grassland and open forest habitats.
Toco toucans are commonly seen in the forest and forest edge of Cerro Cora.
Capybaras are abundant in the rivers and wetlands of Cerro Cora NP.
Yacare caimans inhabit the rivers and streams of Cerro Cora NP.
Scarlet macaws are commonly seen in the forest canopy of Cerro Cora.
Giant otters are occasionally present in the rivers flowing through Cerro Cora NP.
Roseate spoonbills are occasionally seen in the wetland areas adjacent to Cerro Cora.

name: Serra do Divisor NP
description: Remote Acre state park on the Peruvian border protecting pristine Amazon forest; one of Brazil's least visited yet most biodiverse parks with jaguars, harpy eagles, and giant otters.

sightings.notes: Jaguars are present in this remote park; camera surveys along the river corridors confirm their presence.
Tapirs inhabit the diverse forest of Serra do Divisor across all altitude zones.
Giant otters may be present in the rivers flowing from Serra do Divisor into the Amazon basin.
Spider monkeys are abundant in the pristine lowland forest of Serra do Divisor.
Harpy eagles are present in this vast remote park; the intact tall forest provides ideal habitat.
Capybaras are abundant along the rivers of Serra do Divisor.
Scarlet macaws are commonly seen in the forest canopy of this remote park.
Giant anteaters are regularly encountered in the open areas and terra firme forest of Serra do Divisor.
Toco toucans are very common throughout the forest of Serra do Divisor.
Roseate spoonbills are regularly seen in the river margins and oxbow lakes of Serra do Divisor.
Bush dogs are present in Serra do Divisor; small packs are occasionally camera-trapped in this remote Amazonian park.

name: Podocarpus NP
description: Ecuador's southernmost national park spanning Andean and Amazonian zones; one of the most biodiverse spots on Earth with spectacled bears, tapirs, and extraordinary endemic bird diversity.

sightings.notes: Spectacled bears inhabit the cloud forest of Podocarpus; camera traps regularly document their presence.
Mountain lions are present throughout Podocarpus across its wide altitudinal range.
Both lowland and mountain tapirs are present in Podocarpus across the altitudinal gradient.
Andean condors soar above the higher ridges of Podocarpus; occasional sightings above 3,000 m.
Scarlet macaws are commonly seen in the lowland and submontane forest of the eastern slopes of Podocarpus.
Giant otters inhabit the lower Amazonian tributary rivers on the eastern side of Podocarpus.
Capybaras inhabit the lower river valleys and wetland margins of Podocarpus.
Spider monkeys are present in the lowland Amazonian forest on the eastern slopes of Podocarpus.
Toco toucans are commonly seen in the lower forest zones of Podocarpus.
Jaguars are present in the lower Amazonian forest of Podocarpus; camera traps confirm their presence.

name: Tatra NP
description: The highest massif in the Carpathians, shared between Poland and Slovakia; brown bears, wolves, and Eurasian lynx inhabit the forest zones while chamois bound across the granite ridges.

sightings.notes: Brown bears are present in both the Polish and Slovak sides; the Tatra population connects with the wider Carpathian population.
Gray wolves from the Carpathian population roam the Tatra forests; pack activity is documented on both sides of the border.
Eurasian lynx are present in the Tatra forests; the population is small but resident on both sides.
Chamois are abundant on the granite ridges and rocky terrain above the tree line; easily observed from trails.
Red deer are common in the Tatra forests; rutting stags are heard and seen in autumn valleys.
Ospreys are occasional summer visitors to the Tatra lakes and rivers; breeding pairs are known from nearby areas.
Wolverine presence has been reported in the High Tatra on the Slovak side; occasional individuals dispersing from further north.

name: Durmitor NP
description: UNESCO World Heritage Dinaric mountain park with the Tara River canyon (Europe's deepest); brown bears, wolves, and Eurasian lynx inhabit the ancient beech forest.

sightings.notes: Brown bears are present in the beech forests of Durmitor; the Dinaric population is recovering.
Gray wolves inhabit the forests of Durmitor; the Dinaric wolf population is one of Europe's most genetically diverse.
Eurasian lynx are present in Durmitor; the Dinaric population is one of the most threatened in Europe.
Chamois are abundant on the rocky slopes and ridges of Durmitor above the forest.
Red deer are common in the mountain valleys and beech forests of Durmitor.
Ospreys are occasional summer visitors to the Tara River and the mountain lakes of Durmitor.
A small alpine ibex population has been reported on the high rocky terrain of Durmitor.

name: Velebit NP
description: Croatia's largest mountain range and UNESCO Biosphere Reserve; a stronghold for the Dinaric population of brown bears, wolves, and Eurasian lynx among Europe's most intact large predator communities.

sightings.notes: Brown bears are abundant in Velebit; Croatia has the highest brown bear density in the EU, centred on Velebit.
Gray wolves are well-established in Velebit; the massif supports multiple packs as part of the Dinaric population.
Eurasian lynx are present throughout Velebit; the Dinaric lynx reintroduction is centred on this massif.
Chamois are commonly seen on the limestone ridges of Velebit, particularly in the Southern Velebit Nature Park.
Red deer are abundant throughout the Velebit forest zones.
Ospreys are occasional visitors to the Velebit coastal cliffs and the larger rivers in the karst valleys.

name: Carpathian NP
description: Ukraine's oldest national park in the Eastern Carpathians, protecting primeval beech-fir forest with brown bears, wolves, Eurasian lynx, European bison, and red deer.

sightings.notes: Brown bears are common in the Ukrainian Carpathians; the park supports one of the highest densities in the region.
Gray wolves are well-established in the Carpathian NP; multiple packs inhabit the primeval beech forest.
Eurasian lynx are present throughout the park's forest; camera traps regularly document individuals.
European bison were reintroduced to the Ukrainian Carpathians; a small population now roams the forest.
Red deer are abundant in the primeval beech-fir forest of the Ukrainian Carpathians.
Chamois inhabit the rocky ridges and alpine meadows of the Carpathian peaks; they are regularly seen above the tree line.

name: Grampians NP
description: Victoria's iconic sandstone ranges with Australia's highest eastern grey kangaroo density; emus wander the walking tracks and koalas sleep in the stringybark eucalypts above dramatic rocky lookouts.

sightings.notes: Eastern grey kangaroos are extraordinarily abundant in the Grampians; large mobs graze the valley floors at dusk.
Koalas are commonly seen sleeping in the stringybark and manna gum eucalypts throughout the park.
Emus are commonly encountered walking the trails and roadsides; particularly abundant in the northern Grampians.
Ospreys occasionally fish the larger reservoirs and lakes on the fringes of the Grampians.
Sea eagles occasionally patrol the larger water bodies within and adjacent to the Grampians.
Platypus inhabit the clear streams and rivers of the Grampians; best spotted at dawn and dusk in the Wannon and Glenelg river systems.
Echidnas are abundant and frequently encountered throughout the Grampians; they shuffle along trails probing the soil for ants and termites.

name: Cape Range NP
description: Western Australia's limestone coastal park above Ningaloo Reef, with red kangaroos on the ridge tops, emus on the plains, and ospreys and sea eagles along the dramatic coastline.

sightings.notes: Red kangaroos are abundant on the limestone ridges and valleys of Cape Range; easily seen at dusk.
Emus are commonly seen across the plains and gorges of Cape Range.
Ospreys are commonly seen fishing the sheltered Exmouth Gulf and the Ningaloo coastal waters adjacent to the park.
Sea eagles are resident along the Cape Range coastline, frequently seen perched on the limestone cliffs or soaring.
Echidnas are occasionally encountered in the rocky ranges and spinifex of Cape Range NP.

name: Nitmiluk NP
description: Northern Territory's Katherine Gorge — 13 spectacular sandstone gorges carved by the Katherine River — with freshwater and saltwater crocodiles, ospreys, and white-bellied sea eagles.

sightings.notes: Agile wallabies and antilopine kangaroos are abundant in the gorge woodland; commonly seen at dusk.
Saltwater crocodiles are present in the Katherine River below the first gorge; swimming is prohibited in areas where they occur.
Ospreys are resident along the Katherine River gorges, frequently seen fishing the clear waterways.
Sea eagles are resident and conspicuous along the sandstone gorges of Nitmiluk, nesting on the cliff ledges.
Emus are commonly seen on the open woodland plateau above the Nitmiluk gorges.
Echidnas are frequently encountered on the rocky escarpment of Nitmiluk gorge during the dry season.

name: Jiuzhaigou NP
description: UNESCO World Heritage alpine valley famed for turquoise lakes and waterfalls; bamboo-forested slopes shelter giant pandas, golden snub-nosed monkeys, and Sichuan takin above the tourist boardwalks.

sightings.notes: Troops of golden snub-nosed monkeys inhabit Jiuzhaigou's higher conifer forests, their vivid blue faces and golden coats among the park's most sought-after sightings.
Small herds of Sichuan takin graze the high meadows and forest edges above the lake terraces.
This iridescent pheasant is occasionally seen foraging on the high slopes above the main valley in the warmer months.
Asian black bears forage the bamboo understorey in summer and autumn, though they are rarely seen by visitors confined to the boardwalks.
Giant pandas inhabit the remote high-elevation bamboo forest surrounding Jiuzhaigou, but the resident population is small and sightings within the tourist area are extremely rare.
Snow leopards occasionally range across the highest ridgelines bordering Jiuzhaigou in winter, following blue sheep and other prey.

name: Wanglang NR
description: One of China's oldest and most important giant panda reserves, in the Pingwu county mountains of northern Sichuan; also a stronghold for golden snub-nosed monkeys and Sichuan takin.

sightings.notes: Wanglang is one of the most reliable reserves in China for panda camera-trap records and holds one of the highest wild panda densities in Sichuan.
Large multi-level troops move through Wanglang's mixed conifer-broadleaf forest, especially along the reserve's research trails.
Sichuan takin herds are regularly recorded by camera traps across Wanglang's mid-to-high elevation slopes.
Asian black bears are present throughout Wanglang's forest zones, most active in the warmer months.
Chinese monal are seen on the reserve's high-altitude scree and meadow areas above the treeline.

name: Foping NR
description: Qinling Mountains reserve renowned for one of the world's highest densities of wild giant pandas, alongside golden takin and the golden-coated Qinling subspecies of the golden snub-nosed monkey.

sightings.notes: Foping holds one of the highest wild panda densities anywhere, thanks to unusually rich understorey bamboo.
The striking golden-coated Qinling takin subspecies is a signature sight, moving in herds through the reserve's forests and meadows.
The Qinling subspecies of golden snub-nosed monkey forms large troops that are well habituated to researchers in parts of the reserve.
Asian black bears range through Foping's forested slopes, particularly during the summer and autumn feeding season.
Golden eagles hunt over the Qinling ridgelines bordering the reserve.

name: Hanzhong Crested Ibis NR
description: Wetland and farmland reserve along the Han River in Yangxian County, where the world's last seven wild crested ibis were rediscovered in 1981; the entire global population descends from that single flock.

sightings.notes: Yangxian is the birthplace of crested ibis conservation; the reserve's paddy fields and river shallows hold the core of the recovered wild population.
The reserve's forested upper slopes hold a small resident giant panda population alongside its famous ibis wetlands.
Golden takin range through the reserve's higher forested terrain bordering the Qinling range.
Golden snub-nosed monkeys are occasionally recorded in the reserve's forested hills above the ibis wetlands.
White-tailed eagles have recolonised the Han River valley in recent years, hunting over the reserve's wetlands in winter.

name: Kanas NR
description: Altai Mountains reserve on China's border with Russia, Kazakhstan, and Mongolia; the only part of China with wild moose, alongside brown bear, lynx, and Siberian ibex in pristine taiga and alpine terrain.

sightings.notes: The Altai taiga around Kanas Lake is the only place in China where wild moose survive; they are seen browsing willow thickets near rivers and lakes in the warmer months.
Brown bears forage the Kanas forests and alpine meadows through spring and summer.
Eurasian lynx inhabit Kanas's dense taiga forest, though this elusive cat is very rarely seen.
Siberian ibex are found on the rocky slopes above the treeline in the reserve's higher reaches.
The Altai subspecies of red deer roams Kanas's forest edges and meadows.

name: Hoh Xil NR
description: Vast, uninhabited UNESCO World Heritage wilderness on the Tibetan Plateau, protecting the calving grounds of the migratory Tibetan antelope alongside wild yak and kiang on some of the highest, coldest grassland on Earth.

sightings.notes: Hoh Xil protects the most important calving grounds for the Tibetan antelope's mass summer migration, when thousands gather to give birth.
Wild yak herds graze the reserve's open plateau grassland year-round.
Herds of kiang, the largest of the Asiatic wild asses, roam Hoh Xil's high steppe.
Tibetan gazelle share the plateau grassland with chiru and kiang across the reserve.
Tibetan brown bears range across Hoh Xil's remote basins, though sightings are rare given the reserve's vast size.

name: Zhalong NR
description: One of the world's largest reed marshes, on the Songnen Plain of northeast China; a critical breeding site for the red-crowned crane and other rare cranes and storks.

sightings.notes: Zhalong holds one of the largest breeding populations of red-crowned crane anywhere, with pairs nesting in the reed marsh through summer.
White-naped cranes breed in Zhalong's wetlands alongside the more numerous red-crowned cranes.
Oriental storks nest on platforms and tall structures around the marsh in summer.
Small numbers of migrating Siberian cranes pass through Zhalong during spring migration.
Mandarin ducks breed among Zhalong's reeds and pools alongside a huge diversity of other waterfowl.

name: Poyang Lake NR
description: China's largest freshwater lake, whose seasonal mudflats support the world's most important wintering grounds for the Siberian crane and a spectacular concentration of migratory waterbirds each winter.

sightings.notes: More than 95% of the world's Siberian crane population winters on Poyang Lake's exposed mudflats and shallows each year.
Poyang holds the largest wintering population of Oriental storks anywhere, feeding in the lake's shallow margins.
Large flocks of white-naped crane winter alongside Siberian cranes on Poyang's grassy mudflats.
The great majority of the world's wild swan goose population winters on Poyang Lake's exposed lakebed.
Poyang Lake holds one of the last significant Yangtze finless porpoise populations, following the extinction of the Yangtze river dolphin.

name: Changbaishan NR
description: Ancient volcanic massif on the North Korean border, crowned by the crater lake Tianchi; recovering Amur tiger and leopard populations are returning to its old-growth conifer forest after decades of absence.

sightings.notes: Amur tigers were confirmed back in Changbaishan in December 2024 for the first time since 1994, part of a broader westward expansion from the Russian border population.
Brown bears forage Changbaishan's forests and alpine meadows through the warmer months.
Amur goral cling to Changbaishan's steep, forested cliffs, one of the few Chinese sites for this small goat-antelope.
Eurasian lynx inhabit the reserve's dense conifer forest.
The Manchurian subspecies of red deer roams Changbaishan's forest zones.

name: Shennongjia NP
description: UNESCO World Heritage forest wilderness in central China, the only home of a distinct, unusually pale-coated population of the golden snub-nosed monkey and the ancient Chinese giant salamander.

sightings.notes: Shennongjia holds a genetically distinct, unusually pale-coated population of golden snub-nosed monkey found nowhere else.
Shennongjia's cold, clear mountain streams are one of the last strongholds of the wild Chinese giant salamander.
Asian black bears range through Shennongjia's dense, ancient forest.
Clouded leopards are occasionally camera-trapped in Shennongjia's remote forest interior.
Reeves's pheasant, with the longest tail of any pheasant, inhabits Shennongjia's mixed forest.

name: Baima Snow Mountain NR
description: The largest reserve in Yunnan and birthplace of Yunnan snub-nosed monkey research; high-altitude fir and rhododendron forest supports roughly half the world population of this black-and-white primate.

sightings.notes: Baima Snow Mountain protects roughly half the world's Yunnan snub-nosed monkey population; habituated troops are tracked daily by reserve researchers.
Asian black bears range through Baima Snow Mountain's fir and rhododendron forest.
Alpine musk deer inhabit the reserve's high conifer forest and scrub.
Golden eagles hunt over Baima Snow Mountain's high ridges.
Red pandas inhabit the reserve's bamboo understorey at the western edge of their Himalaya-to-Yunnan range.

name: Xishuangbanna NR
description: Southernmost tropical rainforest reserve in China, holding over 90% of the country's wild Asian elephants alongside gaur and clouded leopard in five disjunct forest blocks near the Laos and Myanmar borders.

sightings.notes: Xishuangbanna holds the large majority of China's wild Asian elephant population, with regular sightings and crossings recorded at Wild Elephant Valley.
Gaur, the world's largest wild cattle, range through Xishuangbanna's forest blocks.
Clouded leopards are among the rarest and most elusive predators recorded in Xishuangbanna's forest.
Sun bears reach the northern edge of their range in Xishuangbanna's tropical forest.
Green peafowl, far rarer than the familiar Indian peafowl, survive in a handful of Xishuangbanna's river valleys.

name: Tangjiahe NR
description: Northern Sichuan reserve bordering Wanglang and the Min Mountains, a key giant panda corridor also renowned for large herds of Sichuan takin and the elusive Asian golden cat.

sightings.notes: Tangjiahe is one of Sichuan's most reliable reserves for panda camera-trap records and part of a key habitat corridor to Wanglang.
Tangjiahe holds some of the largest takin herds in Sichuan, regularly seen crossing the reserve's rivers.
Golden snub-nosed monkey troops move through Tangjiahe's forest canopy.
Asian black bears forage Tangjiahe's bamboo understorey in the warmer months.
Camera traps in Tangjiahe have documented one of China's best-studied Asian golden cat populations.

name: Wuyishan NP
description: Subtropical forest UNESCO World Heritage site in southeastern China with exceptional bird and amphibian diversity, a key refuge for the Chinese giant salamander, Elliot's pheasant, and Cabot's tragopan.

sightings.notes: Wuyishan's cold mountain streams support one of southern China's last wild Chinese giant salamander populations.
Wuyishan is one of the most reliable sites to see the endemic Elliot's pheasant, foraging the forest floor and understorey.
Cabot's tragopan, endemic to southeastern China, is among Wuyishan's most sought-after birds.
Asian black bears persist in Wuyishan's more remote forest interior.
Reeves's muntjac is common throughout Wuyishan's forest, often heard barking at dusk.

name: Qilianshan NP
description: Vast mountain range separating the Hexi Corridor from the Tibetan Plateau; snow leopard, white-lipped deer, and blue sheep inhabit its alpine meadows and coniferous forest.

sightings.notes: Qilianshan is a confirmed snow leopard corridor connecting the Tibetan Plateau to the Gobi's fringing ranges.
Qilianshan's high alpine meadows hold one of the more accessible white-lipped deer populations on the Tibetan Plateau's northern fringe.
Blue sheep graze the reserve's rocky alpine slopes, the principal prey of Qilianshan's snow leopards.
Argali occur in Qilianshan's higher, more open terrain.
Himalayan marmots are abundant across Qilianshan's high meadows in the warmer months, an important prey base for golden eagles and snow leopards.

name: Altyn-Emel NP
description: Ili River valley park in the Almaty region famed for its Singing Dune and volcanic Aktau mountains; a reintroduction stronghold for the kulan alongside goitered gazelle and argali.

sightings.notes: Reintroduced from just 27 animals in 1982, Altyn-Emel's kulan population has grown to several thousand, one of the great wild-ass recoveries in Central Asia.
Altyn-Emel holds one of Kazakhstan's largest goitered gazelle populations, with thousands wintering across the park's plains.
Argali inhabit the rockier terrain of the Aktau and Kalkan mountains within the park.
Siberian ibex are found on the steeper slopes of Altyn-Emel's mountain ranges.
Golden eagles hunt over Altyn-Emel's open steppe and mountain terrain.

name: Aksu-Zhabagly NR
description: Central Asia's oldest nature reserve, in the western Tian Shan; a stronghold for the snow leopard and the endemic Menzbier's marmot.

sightings.notes: Snow leopards are the most prominent large predator in Aksu-Zhabagly, though sightings remain rare.
Aksu-Zhabagly protects a core population of the endangered Menzbier's marmot, endemic to the western Tian Shan.
The Tian Shan argali is somewhat more frequently seen than the reserve's snow leopards.
Siberian ibex range across Aksu-Zhabagly's steep mountain slopes.
Golden eagles hunt over the reserve's mountain ridges.

name: Ile-Alatau NP
description: Trans-Ili Alatau range immediately south of Almaty, protecting glaciated peaks and alpine meadows around Big Almaty Lake; a well-studied population of snow leopard, ibex, and Tian Shan brown bear.

sightings.notes: Snow leopard is Ile-Alatau's most emblematic large predator, with a small resident population tracked by conservation researchers.
Siberian ibex are particularly visible on the rocky slopes above the treeline around Big Almaty Lake.
Argali are among the park's notable ungulates, grazing the higher alpine terrain.
The Tian Shan brown bear inhabits Ile-Alatau's forested zones.
Pallas's cat is among the reserve's red-listed species, inhabiting rocky steppe terrain.

name: Korgalzhyn NR
description: Vast steppe wetland complex around Lake Tengiz, the world's northernmost breeding site for flamingos; a Ramsar-listed refuge for Dalmatian pelicans and hundreds of other waterbirds.

sightings.notes: Korgalzhyn holds roughly a tenth of the world's Dalmatian pelican population, breeding on the reserve's lake islands.
Lake Tengiz hosts the world's northernmost flamingo colony, with up to 14,000 breeding pairs in good years.
Steppe eagles hunt the grasslands surrounding Korgalzhyn's wetlands.
Saiga occasionally range across the steppe bordering the reserve.
White-tailed eagles hunt over Korgalzhyn's lakes, preying on waterbirds and fish.

name: Naurzum NR
description: Steppe, semi-desert, and pine-forest "island" reserve in northern Kazakhstan, part of the Saryarka UNESCO World Heritage site; critical refuge for the critically endangered saiga antelope and nesting raptors.

sightings.notes: Naurzum's 200,000-hectare steppe provides an important refuge for the critically endangered saiga.
Naurzum is a stronghold for steppe eagle and other raptors, with dozens of pairs nesting in the reserve.
Golden eagles are among the reserve's nesting birds of prey.
Eastern imperial eagle nests in isolated pines overlooking Naurzum's open steppe.
Corsac foxes hunt Naurzum's steppe and semi-desert for rodents and birds.

name: Barsakelmes NR
description: Former Aral Sea island turned peninsula as the sea receded; historic reintroduction site for the kulan and now a refuge for goitered gazelle, saiga, and corsac fox on the exposed former seabed.

sightings.notes: Barsakelmes was known as "the island of kulans" after a successful 1953 reintroduction, though many animals dispersed onto the mainland once the sea receded and the island became a peninsula in the 1990s.
Goitered gazelle are resident across Barsakelmes's steppe and former seabed habitat.
Saiga range across the reserve's plains alongside kulan and gazelle.
Corsac foxes hunt the reserve's arid grassland and semi-desert.
Wolves are the reserve's main large predator, ranging across the former island's open terrain.

name: Ala Archa NP
description: Kyrgyzstan's most visited alpine park, a Tian Shan gorge just south of Bishkek; snow leopard, Siberian ibex, and argali inhabit its glaciated peaks above 4,000 metres.

sightings.notes: A camera trap recorded Ala Archa's first confirmed snow leopard photograph in 2017.
Siberian ibex are a common sight on the park's higher rocky slopes.
Argali graze Ala Archa's higher alpine terrain alongside ibex.
Menzbier's marmot is an important prey species in Ala Archa's subalpine zone.
Wolves range through Ala Archa's gorge and higher slopes.

name: Sarychat-Ertash NR
description: Remote high-altitude reserve in eastern Kyrgyzstan established to protect one of Central Asia's densest snow leopard populations, with exceptionally healthy argali and ibex numbers.

sightings.notes: Camera-trap studies estimate around 18 snow leopards in Sarychat-Ertash, one of the highest densities recorded anywhere.
Sarychat-Ertash holds around 1,300 ibex, with densities more than four times higher than in adjacent hunting areas.
Argali populations in Sarychat-Ertash are exceptionally healthy thanks to two decades free of grazing and hunting.
Golden eagles hunt marmots and hares across Sarychat-Ertash's high terrain.
Wolves prey on ibex and argali across the reserve's remote valleys.

name: Tajik NP (Pamir)
description: UNESCO World Heritage high-mountain wilderness at the "roof of the world", where the Pamir, Tian Shan, Hindu Kush, and Karakoram ranges converge; home to the giant-horned Marco Polo sheep.

sightings.notes: The Tajik National Park's high plateaus hold roughly 5,400 Marco Polo sheep, the largest-horned wild sheep on Earth.
An estimated 120 snow leopards inhabit the park, preying on Marco Polo sheep and ibex across its vast territory.
More than 4,000 Siberian ibex inhabit the park's rugged terrain.
Golden eagles hunt over the Pamir's high plateaus.
Wolves range across the Pamir's high plateaus, following ibex and Marco Polo sheep herds.

name: Zorkul NR
description: Remote high-altitude lake reserve on the Afghan border in the eastern Pamir, one of the most important breeding and staging sites for Marco Polo sheep and migratory waterbirds at extreme altitude.

sightings.notes: Zorkul Lake's surrounding plateau is one of the most important Marco Polo sheep habitats in the Pamir.
Siberian ibex share the rugged terrain around Zorkul Lake with Marco Polo sheep.
Snow leopards range across the mountains surrounding Zorkul Lake, following wild sheep herds.
Golden eagles patrol the high plateau around the lake.
Zorkul Lake, at over 4,100 metres, is one of the highest-altitude breeding sites for bar-headed goose in Central Asia.

name: Nuratau-Kyzylkum NR
description: Combined mountain-and-desert reserve in central Uzbekistan protecting the last strongholds of the Bukhara deer along riverine tugai forest, alongside a rare population of Severtzov's argali.

sightings.notes: Nuratau-Kyzylkum protects one of the last wild Bukhara deer populations, restricted to narrow tugai forest along Central Asian rivers.
The Nuratau mountains hold a population of the rare Severtzov's argali subspecies.
Urial inhabit the rocky foothills of the Nuratau range.
Goitered gazelle range across the reserve's desert margins.
Golden eagles hunt over the Nuratau range's rocky slopes.

name: Badhyz NR
description: Remote reserve on the Afghan and Iranian border protecting relict pistachio savanna and an extinct volcano; the last stronghold of the Turkmenian kulan and a major goitered gazelle population.

sightings.notes: Badhyz was once the world's last refuge for the Turkmenian kulan, whose population here has fluctuated between roughly 200 and 5,000 over the reserve's history.
Badhyz holds 4,500-5,000 goitered gazelle, one of the largest populations anywhere.
Urial are common across Badhyz's rocky badlands alongside goitered gazelle.
Persian leopards are among Badhyz's predators, alongside striped hyena and wolf.
Striped hyena hunt and scavenge across Badhyz's semi-desert terrain.

name: Hustai NP
description: Steppe reserve west of Ulaanbaatar where the Przewalski's horse, extinct in the wild since 1969, was first returned to free-ranging life in 1992.

sightings.notes: Hustai was the first release site for reintroduced Przewalski's horses in 1992 and now supports one of the largest free-ranging populations anywhere.
Mongolian gazelle move across Hustai's steppe grassland.
Argali inhabit Hustai's rockier hill terrain.
Steppe eagles hunt Hustai's open grassland for rodents.
Wolves range across Hustai's steppe, an important natural predator of the reintroduced horses.

name: Gobi Gurvansaikhan NP
description: Mongolia's largest national park, spanning 27,000 km² of the Gobi Desert's dune fields, ice-filled gorges, and rocky ranges; home to snow leopard, wild Bactrian camel, argali, and ibex.

sightings.notes: Snow leopards inhabit the rocky Gurvan Saikhan range within the park.
The park's desert fringes and dune areas hold part of the range of the critically endangered wild Bactrian camel.
Argali graze the park's rockier mountain terrain.
Siberian ibex are common on the cliffs of the Gurvan Saikhan range, including around Yolyn Am gorge.
Pallas's cat inhabits the park's rocky steppe terrain, rarely seen due to its camouflage and nocturnal habits.

name: Great Gobi SPA "A"
description: Southwestern Mongolia's largest strictly protected area, established to protect the wild Bactrian camel and the Gobi bear, the world's rarest bear subspecies, restricted entirely to this single reserve.

sightings.notes: Great Gobi SPA "A" is the only place on Earth with wild Gobi bears, with a population of just 30-40 individuals.
The reserve protects the largest remaining range of wild Bactrian camel, alongside the Lop Nur population across the Chinese border.
The Mongolian subspecies of kulan ranges across the Great Gobi's plains.
Snow leopards inhabit the reserve's mountain ranges (Tsagaan Bogd, Atas, Inges) alongside the Gobi bear.
Argali range across the reserve's remote mountain terrain.

name: Burabay NP
description: Granite-outcrop lake district in northern Kazakhstan, the "Switzerland of Kazakhstan" and one of the country's most visited parks, with pine forest sheltering moose, wolf, and lynx.

sightings.notes: Moose inhabit Burabay's pine forest and lakeshore willow thickets.
Roe deer are commonly seen browsing Burabay's forest meadows at dawn and dusk.
Wild boar root through Burabay's forest floor, often in family groups.
Golden eagles are among the raptors recorded over Burabay's granite outcrops.
Wolves range through Burabay's forest, though sightings are infrequent.
Eurasian lynx are present but rarely seen in Burabay's dense pine forest.

name: Karkaraly NP
description: Pine-forested granite range rising from the central Kazakh steppe like a mountain "island"; a refuge for argali and a stronghold for raptors including golden eagle and saker falcon.

sightings.notes: Argali (arhar) inhabit Karkaraly's rocky granite highlands, listed in Kazakhstan's Red Book.
Siberian roe deer are among the park's most commonly recorded large mammals.
Wild boar inhabit Karkaraly's pine and birch forest.
Golden eagle is among 11 Red Book bird species recorded in the park.
Saker falcon has been recorded among Karkaraly's notable birds of prey.
Black stork breeds in Karkaraly's forest, nesting away from human disturbance.
Pallas's cat is a very rare resident of Karkaraly, on the verge of extinction locally.

name: Kolsai Lakes NP
description: A chain of turquoise glacial lakes on the northern slope of the Tian Shan near the Kyrgyzstan border, known as the "Pearls of the Tian Shan"; home to snow leopard, Tian Shan brown bear, and ibex.

sightings.notes: The Tian Shan brown bear subspecies inhabits Kolsai's higher forested slopes.
Siberian ibex are a common sight navigating the rocky terrain above the lakes.
Tian Shan argali inhabit Kolsai's higher, more remote terrain.
Snow leopards are among the six rare mammal species confirmed in Kolsai Lakes NP.
The Turkestan lynx subspecies inhabits Kolsai's forested and rocky zones.

name: Bayanaul NP
description: Kazakhstan's first national park (1985), a granite highland rising from the Pavlodar steppe with pine forest and freshwater lakes supporting argali, roe deer, and abundant waterfowl.

sightings.notes: Argali sheep graze Bayanaul's granite highland, Kazakhstan's first protected national park.
Roe deer are found throughout Bayanaul's forested areas.
Golden eagles are among Bayanaul's notable raptors.
Whooper swans visit Bayanaul's freshwater lakes.
European badgers inhabit the park's upland woodlands.

name: Issyk-Kul SNBR
description: UNESCO biosphere reserve encompassing Kyrgyzstan's vast alpine lake, one of the largest wintering sites for migratory waterbirds in Central Asia, ringed by Tian Shan foothills.

sightings.notes: Issyk-Kul is one of Central Asia's most important wintering sites for the endangered white-headed duck.
Large flocks of whooper swan winter on Issyk-Kul's ice-free waters alongside geese and ducks.
Steppe eagles hunt the grassland surrounding the lake basin.
Golden eagles range over the Tian Shan foothills ringing the reserve.
Siberian ibex inhabit the mountainous fringes of the biosphere reserve above the lake basin.

name: Zaamin NP
description: Uzbekistan's oldest protected area, the "Switzerland of Uzbekistan" in the western Tian Shan; juniper forest and alpine meadow shelter Tian Shan brown bear, snow leopard, and Turkestan lynx.

sightings.notes: The Tian Shan brown bear population in Zaamin has stabilised at around two dozen individuals after decades of decline.
Snow leopards have been recorded in Zaamin's high Tian Shan terrain.
The Turkestan lynx has been seen in Zaamin's forested mountain slopes.
Zaamin is an important nesting site for the shy black stork.
Argali range across Zaamin's higher, more open Tian Shan terrain.

name: Dashtijum NR
description: Strict nature reserve on the southern Darvaz range near the Afghan border, established to protect Central Asia's largest viable population of the corkscrew-horned Bukhara markhor.

sightings.notes: Dashtijum protects Central Asia's largest viable Bukhara markhor population, recovering from roughly 2,000 to over 6,000 animals region-wide in a decade.
The Central Asian mouflon (urial) shares Dashtijum's juniper woodland with the markhor.
Persian leopards are among the predators recorded in the mountains of southern Tajikistan surrounding the reserve.
Golden eagles hunt over Dashtijum's rugged Darvaz range terrain.
Wolves range across Dashtijum's mountain slopes.

name: Kopet Dag NR
description: Arid mountain range on the Iranian border south of Ashgabat, Turkmenistan's most important stronghold for the Persian leopard alongside urial and bezoar goat.

sightings.notes: Kopet Dag is Turkmenistan's most important stronghold for the Persian leopard.
Urial is the flagship wild sheep of the Kopet Dag range, the leopard's primary prey.
Bezoar goat, the wild ancestor of the domestic goat, inhabits Kopet Dag's steep rocky slopes.
Onager historically ranged the foothills of the Kopet Dag, with occasional records at the reserve's margins.
Golden eagles hunt over Kopet Dag's arid mountain terrain.

name: Repetek NR
description: One of the world's oldest desert reserves, protecting a pristine stretch of the Karakum sand desert; home to the giant desert monitor lizard, caracal, and a relict goitered gazelle population.

sightings.notes: Repetek is a stronghold for the giant desert monitor, one of the top predators of the Karakum sands.
Caracal, known locally as the desert lynx, hunts Repetek's dunes and scrub by night.
A small relict population of around 30 goitered gazelle survives within the reserve.
Indian crested porcupine shelters by day in burrows across Repetek's sand desert.
Central Asian cobra is among the reptiles protected within Repetek's desert ecosystem.

name: Gorkhi-Terelj NP
description: Mongolia's most accessible national park, a short drive from Ulaanbaatar; granite outcrops and river valleys support argali, ibex, and brown bear alongside its famous rock formations.

sightings.notes: Argali sheep inhabit Gorkhi-Terelj's rockier highland terrain.
Siberian ibex are found on the park's steeper granite outcrops.
Brown bears range through Gorkhi-Terelj's more remote forest, rarely seen near the main tourist areas.
Wolves are among the elusive predators recorded in the park's wilder reaches.
Red deer inhabit the deeper forest of the surrounding mountains.
Wild boar root through Gorkhi-Terelj's forest floor alongside red deer and roe deer.

name: Khovsgol Lake NP
description: "Mongolia's Blue Pearl", a vast Siberian-taiga lake in the far north near the Russian border, ringed by forest that shelters reindeer herded by the nomadic Tsaatan people alongside elk, moose, and musk deer.

sightings.notes: The Siberian wapiti (maral) subspecies of elk ranges through Khovsgol's taiga forest.
Siberian moose inhabit Khovsgol's northern taiga alongside elk and reindeer.
Reindeer, both wild and herded by the nomadic Tsaatan people, range across Khovsgol's northern taiga.
Siberian musk deer inhabit the dense taiga forest surrounding the lake.
Wolverine is among the rarer taiga predators recorded around Lake Khovsgol.
Argali inhabit the more open, mountainous terrain around the lake basin.

name: Altai Tavan Bogd NP
description: Mongolia's highest and most glaciated national park, surrounding the Tavan Bogd massif at the China-Russia-Mongolia tripoint; snow leopard, Altai argali, and Siberian ibex inhabit its rugged peaks.

sightings.notes: Snow leopards hunt ibex and wild sheep across Altai Tavan Bogd's glaciated high terrain.
The Altai argali, the largest wild sheep in the world, grazes the park's open steppe and mountain terrain.
Siberian ibex are common on the rocky slopes surrounding the Tavan Bogd massif.
Golden eagles, traditionally used in Kazakh eagle-hunting in this region, hunt over the park's high terrain.
Brown bears range across the park's remote valleys and forested lower slopes.

name: Touran NP
description: Iran's largest protected area and the most important stronghold anywhere for the critically endangered Asiatic cheetah, spanning desert steppe and salt basin in Semnan Province.

sightings.notes: Touran holds the largest documented population of Asiatic cheetah anywhere, with roughly 11 individuals recorded and the only confirmed recent breeding.
Touran protects one of Iran's most important Persian onager populations, alongside the cheetah.
Goitered gazelle range across Touran's desert steppe.
Wolves are among the predators sharing Touran's vast protected area with the cheetah.
Golden eagles hunt over Touran's desert steppe and salt basin.

name: Kavir NP
description: One of Iran's oldest protected areas, a salt-desert basin south of Tehran named for the Dasht-e Kavir; among the last strongholds of the Asiatic cheetah alongside Persian onager and goitered gazelle.

sightings.notes: Kavir is one of five protected areas where Iran's last Asiatic cheetahs survive.
Persian onager range across Kavir's salt-desert basin.
Goitered gazelle inhabit Kavir's desert plains.
Persian leopards occur in Kavir's rockier fringes.
Wolves range across Kavir's desert basin.

name: Asir NP
description: Cool, mountainous escarpment park in southwestern Saudi Arabia, the country's most biodiverse national park and a last refuge for the critically endangered Arabian leopard.

sightings.notes: Asir NP is one of the last areas of historic Arabian leopard range in Saudi Arabia, though none has been confirmed in the wild here for over a decade.
Troops of hamadryas baboon are a common sight along Asir's escarpment roads and cliffs.
Nubian ibex inhabit Asir's steep rocky terrain.
Rock hyrax colonies bask on Asir's rocky outcrops.
Egyptian vultures soar over Asir's escarpment cliffs.

name: Ibex Reserve
description: Rugged section of the Tuwayq Escarpment south of Riyadh, holding the largest Nubian ibex population in the Kingdom alongside reintroduced Arabian gazelle.

sightings.notes: The Ibex Reserve holds Saudi Arabia's largest Nubian ibex population, on the wild cliffs of the Tuwayq Escarpment.
Reintroduced Arabian mountain gazelle range across the reserve alongside its ibex.
Rock hyrax are common among the reserve's rocky outcrops.
Arabian wolves, a small desert-adapted subspecies, range across the reserve.
Blanford's fox, an agile rock climber, has been recorded on camera traps in the reserve.

name: Uruq Bani Ma'arid
description: Saudi Arabia's first natural UNESCO World Heritage Site, an unfenced 12,658 km² reserve on the western edge of the Rub' al Khali (Empty Quarter); the site of the Arabian oryx's return to the wild.

sightings.notes: Uruq Bani Ma'arid holds a free-ranging, self-sustaining Arabian oryx population, part of the species' recovery from wild extinction.
Sand gazelle share the reserve's dune habitat with Arabian oryx.
Sand cats hunt the reserve's dunes by night, rarely seen even by researchers.
Arabian wolves occasionally range across the reserve's desert margins.
Egyptian vultures are occasionally seen over the reserve's dune fields.

name: Jebel Samhan NR
description: Dhofar escarpment reserve on Oman's southern coast, one of the last strongholds anywhere of the critically endangered Arabian leopard.

sightings.notes: Jebel Samhan is one of the last confirmed wild Arabian leopard populations, with around 17-20 adults identified by camera trap.
Jebel Samhan is considered a major stronghold for Nubian ibex, the leopard's most important prey.
Arabian gazelle are among the leopard's key prey species on the escarpment.
Caracal share Jebel Samhan's rugged terrain with the rarer Arabian leopard.
Striped hyena are among the reserve's other predators.

name: Wadi Wurayah NP
description: The UAE's first protected mountain area, in the Hajar Mountains of Fujairah, set aside by royal decree in 2009 for the critically endangered Arabian tahr.

sightings.notes: Wadi Wurayah was designated in 2009 specifically to protect the Arabian tahr, though the species is thought to have declined further within the reserve in recent years.
Arabian gazelle range through Wadi Wurayah's wadis and rocky slopes.
Blanford's fox has been camera-trapped on Wadi Wurayah's steep rocky terrain.
Egyptian vultures are occasionally seen over the Hajar Mountains within the reserve.
Rock hyrax inhabit the reserve's rocky wadi walls.

name: Sir Bani Yas (Arabian Wildlife Park)
description: Abu Dhabi's largest natural island, turned into an unfenced wildlife reserve under Sheikh Zayed's "Greening of the Desert" programme; now home to free-roaming Arabian oryx, gazelle, and introduced giraffe.

sightings.notes: Sir Bani Yas holds a free-roaming herd of more than 400 Arabian oryx.
Sand gazelle are among the most numerous of the park's roughly 13,000 native Arabian animals.
Mountain (Arabian) gazelle also range freely across the island alongside sand gazelle.
Striped hyena are among the free-roaming predators and scavengers on the island.
Introduced (not native to Arabia) reticulated giraffe are among Sir Bani Yas's most photographed sights, roaming the unfenced reserve alongside native desert wildlife.

name: Dana Biosphere Reserve
description: Jordan's largest nature reserve, spanning four biogeographical zones from Mediterranean highland to Saharo-Arabian desert; a conservation success story for the Nubian ibex.

sightings.notes: Dana's ibex population has recovered from fewer than 50 to over 120 individuals through careful management.
Syrian wolves — a small, pale desert-adapted subspecies — range across Dana's mountain ridges by night.
Sand cats prowl Dana's eastern desert lowlands.
Caracal inhabit Dana's rocky highland terrain.
Golden eagles soar over Dana's mountain ridges and canyons.

name: Ein Gedi NR
description: Desert oasis reserve on the shores of the Dead Sea, Israel's most visited nature reserve and home to the country's largest Nubian ibex population.

sightings.notes: Ein Gedi holds Israel's largest Nubian ibex population, regularly seen on cliff ledges and near the reserve's entrance.
Rock hyrax colonies bask on rocks throughout Ein Gedi, especially in morning sun near the trailheads.
Striped hyena occasionally range through Ein Gedi's canyons at night.
Caracal are occasionally recorded in Ein Gedi's rugged terrain.
Egyptian vultures pass along the Dead Sea's Rift Valley migration corridor through Ein Gedi.

name: Hai-Bar Yotvata NR
description: Breeding and reintroduction reserve in the southern Arava valley, central to restoring the Arabian oryx, Persian onager, and Persian fallow deer to the wild in Israel.

sightings.notes: Hai-Bar Yotvata launched Israel's Arabian oryx reintroduction in 1978; descendants now range free across the northern Arava.
Hai-Bar Yotvata bred Persian onager as a proxy for the extinct Syrian wild ass, now free-ranging in the reserve and surrounding desert.
Persian fallow deer, thought extinct until rediscovered in Iran in the 1950s, were bred at Hai-Bar Yotvata for reintroduction to Israeli nature reserves.
Dorcas gazelle are native to the Arava and range freely around Hai-Bar Yotvata's reserve.
Hai-Bar Yotvata bred ostrich for reintroduction after the local population went extinct in the wild by the mid-20th century.

name: Al Shouf Cedar NR
description: Lebanon's largest nature reserve, protecting ancient cedar forest on the slopes of Jebel Barouk; the best prospect for the country's national animal, the striped hyena, alongside wolf and wildcat.

sightings.notes: Lebanon's national animal is present in Al Shouf's cedar and oak forest, though it is nocturnal and rarely seen.
Gray wolves inhabit Al Shouf's forested highlands above 1,150 metres.
Wildcat are among the reserve's more elusive resident carnivores.
Golden eagles soar above Al Shouf's cedar-forested ridges.
Egyptian vultures are occasionally seen over the reserve's highland terrain.

name: Mesopotamian Marshes (Hawizeh)
description: The "Garden of Eden" wetlands of southern Iraq, a UNESCO World Heritage site of reed marsh sustaining the near-endemic Basra reed warbler and one of the last populations of smooth-coated otter in the Middle East.

sightings.notes: A geographically isolated population, sometimes called Maxwell's otter, survives only in Iraq's marshes.
The marshes hold the large majority of the world's Basra reed warbler breeding population.
The Mesopotamian marshes are a global stronghold for the vulnerable marbled duck.
Golden jackals are common and vocal throughout the marshes, often heard calling at dusk.
Migratory flamingos visit the marshes' shallow saline margins in winter.

name: Socotra Archipelago
description: UNESCO World Heritage "Galápagos of the Indian Ocean", isolated for millions of years; roughly 90% of its reptile species and a third of its plants occur nowhere else on Earth.

sightings.notes: Egyptian vultures are unusually common and unafraid of people across Socotra, filling the ecological role of scavenger with few competitors.
Socotra sunbird, one of the island's ten endemic bird species, feeds on the nectar of Socotra's endemic flowering plants.
Socotra starling forages around the island's cliffs and villages, found nowhere else on Earth.
Socotra warbler inhabits the archipelago's scrub and semi-desert.
Socotra chameleon is one of the reptile species found only on the island, among the roughly 90% of Socotra's reptiles that are endemic.

name: Kaçkar Mountains NP
description: Turkey's most biodiverse national park, in the Pontic Mountains along the eastern Black Sea coast; ancient forest and alpine meadow support brown bear, wolf, and chamois.

sightings.notes: Kaçkar's mountain forests and meadows are a key Turkish stronghold for brown bear.
Wolves hunt wild goat and deer across Kaçkar's remote valleys.
The Caucasian chamois inhabits Kaçkar's rugged alpine terrain.
Eurasian lynx range through Kaçkar's forest zones.
Golden eagles nest on Kaçkar's high cliffs and ridges.
